sources.summary: The paper models six XMM-Newton observations of 1H 0419--577, interpreting its flux-dependent X-ray spectra as variable continuum plus nearly constant relativistically blurred ionized reflection.

quotes.quote: We report results obtained from six XMM--Newton observations of the Seyfert galaxy 1H 0419--577. The source was observed in a wide range of different flux levels, allowing its long--term spectral variability to be studied in detail as already reported by Pounds et al (2004a; 2004b). Here we show that the X--ray spectrum is well described by a simple two--component model comprising a power law with constant spectral shape and variable normalisation and a much more constant ionised reflection component from the inner accretion disc which carries the signature of strong relativistic effects. One of the observations was performed when the source was in a particularly low flux state in which the X--ray spectrum is rather peculiar and exhibits a very flat hard spectrum (with spectral index close to 1 in the 2--10 keV band) with broad residuals below 6.6 keV (rest--frame) and a steep soft excess below about 1 keV. We interpret the spectrum as being reflection--dominated by X--ray reprocessed emission from the inner accretion disc.

We have analysed all six available XMM--Newton EPIC--pn observations of . The source was observed in widely different flux states, allowing us to group the observations in low flux (LF), intermediate flux (IF) and high flux (HF) states and to study the spectral variability. \ exhibits a strong soft excess at all flux levels, but steeper in the LF states and more gradual in the HF states.

sources.summary: The paper maps IRAS F04250 5718 as a radio-quiet active galaxy with a biconical ionized outflow extending to about 2.9 kpc and consistent with prior UV constraints.

quotes.quote: In Mrk 509, we find a quasi-spherical outflow with a radius of 1.2 kpc and a velocity of 290 km s ^ 1 , while IRAS F04250 5718 is driving a biconical outflow extending out to 2.9 kpc, with a velocity of 580 km s ^ 1 and an opening angle of 70 . The derived mass flow rate is 5 and >1 M _ yr ^ 1 , respectively, and the kinetic luminosity is 1 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 for both. Adopting the outflow radii and geometric parameters measured from IFU, absorption line analyses would yield mass flow rates and kinetic luminosities in agreement with the above results within a factor of 2 . We conclude that the spatial locations, kinematics and energetics revealed by this IFU emission-line study are consistent with pre-existing UV absorption line analyses, providing a long-awaited direct confirmation of the latter as an effective approach for characterizing outflow properties.

IRAS F04250 5718 ( z=0.104 , Thomas98 ) was discovered in X-ray as 1H 0419 577 Wood84 , and is also known as LB 1727 and 1ES 0425 573. So far, the X-ray spectral energy distribution has been the primary interest of the existing studies on it (the reader is referred to the introduction section of Edmonds11 for a detailed description of its observational history). Edmonds11 identified three kinematic components from its HST/COS UV spectra, and determined a conservative lower limit of 3 kpc for the outflow.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HST/COS absorption in IRAS F04250-5718, identifying intrinsic outflow components, constraining ionization and density, and arguing for a kiloparsec-scale outflow or galactic wind.

quotes.quote: We present absorption line analysis of the outflow in the quasar IRAS F04250-5718. Far-ultraviolet data from the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph onboard the Hubble Space Telescope reveal intrinsic narrow absorption lines from high ionization ions (e.g., , , and ) as well as low ionization ions (e.g., \ and ). We identify three kinematic components with central velocities ranging from -50 to -230 km s ^ 1 .

IRAS F04250-5718 is a radio-quiet, low luminosity quasar (with absolute blue magnitude M_B 24.7 ; Veron-Cetty06 ). Due to the low luminosity and redshift ( z=0.104 ), IRAS F04250-5718 is on the quasar/Seyfert borderline and is spectrally classified as a type 1.5 Seyfert Veron-Cetty06 . After some confusion due to erroneous positions recorded in some finding charts it was confirmed that the position of this galaxy is consistent with that of the faint blue star LB 1727 (see Halpern98,Turner99 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H0419 577 as a narrow-line Seyfert with soft X-ray absorption, intrinsic UV absorption, and low-velocity outflow components including a likely galactic-scale component.

quotes.quote: The narrow-line Seyfert 1 1H0419 577 has fairly strong soft X-ray absorption which attribute to low-ionization gas internal to the source. characterize this as a warm absorber whose parameters are not well determined. They quote an ionization parameter for this gas of log 1.2 . This is roughly compatible with the strong, intrinsic UV absorption visible in FUSE and HST spectra.

describe three low-velocity outflow systems ranging from 50 to 230 km s^ 1 . The strongest component, at 230 km s^ 1 , has low density < 10^3 cm^ 3 , and is located at 9 kpc, suggesting it is part of a full-scale galactic outflow.

sources.summary: The paper models 1H 0419-577 with two-component disc reflection, fixes the reflection redshift to the host galaxy, finds a good high-energy fit, and adds radial disc structure for residuals.

quotes.quote: 1H 0419-577 Here we also begin with a two component disc reflection model, as for 1365. The photon index is again required to be the same for all the model components, the redshift of the reflection is fixed at the value of the host galaxy ( z=0.104 ; Thomas98 ), and the outer radius of the disc fixed at 400 . This model provides a good representation of the data with = 423/436, and importantly fits the \ data well. However, there are still some residual features in the \ spectrum. To resolve these we adopt the approach used in when modelling spectra of this source, and allow the emissivity index and ionisation parameter of the disc to have a radial dependence.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 0419--577 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: 1H 0419--577 is a well-known nearby (z=0.104) Seyfert 1 AGN that has been observed with almost all X-ray observatories. This AGN shows complex X-ray broad-band spectrum Page2002, Walton2010 : strong soft-excess below 2 keV, reflection hump above 10 keV and an Fe K _ line near 6 keV Pal2013, Walton2010 . The estimated mass of the SMBH, harboured in its nucleus, is 3.8 10^8 M _ Nandra2005 . XMM-Newton and Swift /XRT observed 1H 0419--577 in multiple epochs in nearly regular intervals from 2002 to 2018. The details of the observation log are given in Table table:obs_log .

sources.summary: The paper includes 1H 0419 577 among Seyfert galaxies with long EUVE monitoring, reports a marginal 5.8-day signal, and uses it in the period-luminosity comparison.

quotes.quote: Three objects had exceptionally long light curves: 33 days for Ton S180, 26 days for 1H 0419 577, and 20 days for RX J0437.1 4711. Periodicity analysis as outlined above revealed evidence for a 2.08-day signal in Ton S180 with global confidence level of 98 , a 0.908-day signal in RX J0437.4 4711, and a 5.8-day signal in 1H 0419 577 with a global confidence level of only 64 .
Ton S180 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0419 577 is a Seyfert 1.5, and both NGC 4395 and RX J0437.1 4711 are both classified as ordinary Seyfert 1 galaxies.
Remarkably, the linear relationship between the log of the period and luminosity is maintained over the 8 decades of luminosity between Cyg X-1 and 1H 0419 577.

sources.summary: The paper includes LB 1727 after resolving WFC source confusion, identifying it as the Seyfert galaxy component of a blended EUV detection that meets the sample criteria.

quotes.quote: LB 1727: The WFC 2RE catalogue reports the detection of an EUV source identified with the Seyfert 1 galaxy LB 1727 (1H 0419--577) but at a position over 3 \ offset from the optical counterpart and a corresponding RBSC detection. A close examination of the WFC data for this field reveals that the 2RE source is a fact a blend of two sources of roughly equal brightness in the S1 band. These two sources are clearly resolved in pointed EUVE and \ HRI observations and have been identified respectively with the Seyfert galaxy LB 1727 (Guainazzi 1998; Turner 1999) and an AM Her star EUVE J0425.6--5714 (Halpern 1998). When the effects of the source confusion are taken into account, the WFC source associated with LB 1727 meets all the criteria defined in 2 and hence we include it in our sample.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 1H 0419-577's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 1H 0419-577 is a radio-quiet low-luminosity quasar at a redshift of z= 0.1004 ( D 499 Mpc) with an optical Sy 1.5 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It was first discovered with the HEAO-1 satellite in X-rays brissenden_multiwaveband_1987 . Since then, it has mainly been studied in X-rays (e.g., fabian_x-ray_2005,turner_suzaku_2009 ), and is also included in the nine-month BAT sample.

sources.summary: The paper fits Suzaku spectra of 1H0419--577 as a Seyfert galaxy hosting a near-extremal supermassive black hole and finds the Kerr hypothesis still consistent.

quotes.quote: We apply our model to fit Suzaku data of four Seyfert galaxies (Ton S180, Ark 120, 1H0419--577, and Swift J0501.9--3239). These galaxies host at the center supermassive black holes that were previously interpreted as near-extremal Kerr black holes. For Ton S180 and 1H0419--577, our measurements are still consistent with the Kerr hypothesis.

gmw_00000044.name: 1RXS J112716.6+190914
gmw_00000044.overview: 1RXS J112716.6+190914 is a radio-quiet, low-luminosity quasar located at a redshift of z=0.1055 [arXiv:1310.2770]. Discovered in the ROSAT all sky survey, the galaxy is optically classified as a Seyfert 1.8 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770].

sources.summary: The atlas gives 1RXS J112716.6+190914's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J112716.6+190914 -- 2MASX J11271632+1909198 1RXS J112716.6 is a radio-quiet low-luminosity quasar at a redshift of z= 0.1055 ( D 512 Mpc) with an optical Sy 1.8 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 , discovered in the ROSAT all sky survey voges_rosat_1999 . Since its discovery, it has been studied little but it is a member of the nine-month BAT AGN sample. No data are available for this object.

gmw_00000045.name: 2MASX J03565655-4041453
gmw_00000045.overview: 2MASX J03565655-4041453 is a starburst galaxy [arXiv:1303.0009] and a possible spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0748 [arXiv:1310.2770]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus optically classified as a Seyfert 1.9 [arXiv:1310.2770] that exhibits intrinsic variability and a complex X-ray spectrum [arXiv:1303.0009].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 2MASX J03565655-4041453 as a starburst galaxy with a complex X-ray spectrum, increased absorbing column, and possible complex absorption.

quotes.quote: 6dF spectra for 2MASX J03565655-4041453 and XSS J05054-2348 reveal that these two objects can be classified as a starburst galaxy and a Seyfert 2 respectively (using the line ratio diagnostics of 1987ApJS...63..295V and 2006MNRAS.372..961K ).

2MASX J03565655-4041453 The best-fitting model to the combined XMM and BAT data is again a double power-law with an Iron line at 6.4 keV ( TBabs*zTBabs(powerlaw + zTBabs(powerlaw + zgauss)) ), as for NGC 454E (see Table fitresults for details). The absorbing column found here is 7.1^ +0.6 _ 0.4 10^ 22 cm^ 2 which again represents an increase in a factor of 2 over the absorbing column found in W09 using an XRT spectrum. The intrinsic luminosity found here is about half that found in W09, suggesting significant intrinsic variability as well as a substantial variation in the absorbing column density. Additionally, the XMM data reveal a complex spectrum (resembling partially-covered absorption) as opposed to the simple absorbed power-law shape found using the XRT data in W09.

Although the reflection fraction is consistent with zero (within errors) if we freeze the photon index, the better constrained photon index for this source may suggest that reflection is present on some level, potentially with a high reflection fraction. A high effective reflection fraction could indicate light bending or extremely complex absorption in this source.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 2MASX J03565655-4041453's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J03565655 is a galaxy, possibly a spiral, at a redshift of z= 0.0748 ( D 324 Mpc) with an active nucleus optically classified as a Sy 1.9. The AGN was discovered only recently through Swift observations tueller_swift_2008 and thus belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. No data are available for 2MASX J03565655, and it appears very compact in the WISE images.

gmw_00000046.name: 2MASX J09180027+0425066
gmw_00000046.overview: 2MASX J09180027+0425066 is a radio-quiet Type II quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.1564 [arXiv:1305.3622, arXiv:1310.2770]. The active nucleus is moderately absorbed [arXiv:1305.3622], exhibits weak iron fluorescence [arXiv:1305.3622], and has been classified optically as a Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770].

sources.summary: The paper presents J0918.5+0425 as a far, radio-quiet Type II QSO/AGN with possible merging optical structure, moderate absorption, weak Fe emission, and high luminosity.

quotes.quote: The source Swift J0918.5+0425, the farthest among our sample (z = 0.15644) and the only radio-quiet AGN, can be defined as peculiar for many reasons. Detected by Rosat/PSPC in March 2000, the source has been identified as AGN only after the nine-month Swift/BAT survey. After being classified as a QSO Type II ( tue08 ) AGN, J0918.5+0425 has been removed from the second BAT survey release because of variability, and it is not part of the 58-month catalog.

The X-ray spectrum of J0918.5+0425 was modeled by a partially covered power law, resulting in a 0.9 scattered factor. The source is moderately absorbed (N _ H 2 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 local column density) and shows a weak Fe fluorescence line (EW < 90 eV). We stress that this is the highest luminosity source in our sample with an intrinsic, unabsorbed 2--10 keV luminosity of 2 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , confirming the QSO nature of the source as reported by the Swift/BAT AGN catalog.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 2MASX J09180027+0425066's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J09180027 is a quasar at a redshift of z= 0.1564 ( D 781 Mpc) with an optical Sy 2 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It was only recently identified in the SDSS data reyes_space_2008 . 2MASX J09180027 is also a member of the nine-month BAT AGN sample, but does not appear in later BAT catalogues.

gmw_00000047.name: 3C 29
gmw_00000047.overview: 3C 29 is a nearby Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:1310.2770] hosted by an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:1310.2770] with an optical LINER classification [arXiv:1310.2770]. It features biconical radio lobes [arXiv:1310.2770], a compact optical and ultraviolet nucleus [arXiv:1210.6027], and an inner jet detected in X-rays [arXiv:1210.6027].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 29 as a nearby FR I elliptical radio galaxy with optical and ultraviolet nucleus detections and evidence for an X-ray inner jet.

quotes.quote: 3C 29 is a nearby FR I radio galaxy at redshift 0.0448, showing the typical optical elliptical morphology and having a compact nucleus in both the optical and ultraviolet. This source is probably an outlying member of the nearby cluster of galaxies Abell 119, even if no significant companion appears within 100 kpc. The radio emission of 3C 29 is described in Feretti et al. (1999). Although the available radio data are not adequate to trace the S jet back close to the nucleus, there is reasonably convincing evidence that the inner jet is detected in the X-ray band, see Figure fig:3c29app . For a rectangular region extending to the SE (PA=163 ^ ) from just outside the nucleus for 10 , we find 15 4 net counts in the 0.5-2 keV energy range.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 29's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 29 is a radio-loud AGN in an elliptical host galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.045 ( D 202 Mpc) with a FR I radio morphology and an optical LINER classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It features supergalactic-scale biconical radio lobes in the north-south directions (PA 165 ; morganti_radio_1993 ). The /IRAC and MIPS images are dominated by compact nuclear emission with only weak extended components in the IRAC 5.8 and 8 m images.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 029 as an FR I/LEG source with marginally extended line emission and a hint of rotation along a 120 degree line of nodes.

quotes.quote: 3C 029: , FR I/LEG, 1 = 0.89 kpc. Similarly to 3C 015, the line emission is only marginally extended. There is a hint of rotation along a line of nodes oriented at 120 ^ .

gmw_00000048.name: 3C 33
gmw_00000048.overview: The nearby radio galaxy 3C 33 is an elliptical Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:1404.0620] that hosts a compact nuclear X-ray source [arXiv:astro-ph/9709228] and a redshifted nuclear outflow [arXiv:2106.09743]. Its extended, disturbed ionized gas shows ordered rotation [arXiv:1903.10768] alongside patches of intense star formation [arXiv:1811.04946].

sources.summary: The paper reports ROSAT observations of 3C 33, a powerful radio galaxy with optical hot spots, detecting a compact nuclear X-ray source but no hot-spot X-rays and constraining magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: We report on ROSAT HRI observations of the nearby powerful radio galaxies 3C 33 and 3C 111, which both have detected optical hot spots. We find nuclear X-ray sources in both objects, but no X-ray emission from the hot spots. This confirms the presence of a high-energy cutoff in the spectrum of synchrotron-emitting electrons.

3C33 3C 33 was observed with the ROSAT HRI for a total of 51.9 ks between 1996 January 16 and 22. We analysed the data using the Post Reduction Off-line Software (PROS), filtering out time intervals with a `maximum high background' level of > 5 10^ 7 counts pixel ^ 1 s ^ 1 so as to maximise our chances of detecting faint components. This process left us with 47.7 ks of good data. Fig. \ 3C33-pic shows an overlay of the radio contours of Leahy Perley (1991) on a smoothed X-ray image. An X-ray source is detected at a J2000.0 position of 01 08 52.7 +13 20 15.8, roughly 3 arcsec from the radio core position on the radio map.

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C 33 with integral-field spectroscopy, finding structured ionized gas, two kinematic components, rotation plus nuclear-strip residuals, and evidence for inflow-linked activity.

quotes.quote: We present optical integral field spectroscopy -- obtained with the Gemini Multi-Object Spectrograph -- of the inner 4.0 5.8 kpc ^2 of the narrow line radio galaxy 3C 33 at a spatial resolution of 0.58 kpc. The gas emission shows three brightest structures: a strong knot of nuclear emission and two other knots at 1.4 kpc south-west and north-east of the nucleus along the ionization axis. We detect two kinematic components in the emission lines profiles, with a ``broader component'' (with velocity dispersion 150 ) being dominant within a 1 kpc wide strip (``the nuclear strip'') running from the south-east to the north-west, perpendicular to the radio jet, and a narrower component ( 100 ) dominating elsewhere. Centroid velocity maps reveal a rotation pattern with velocity amplitudes reaching 350 in the region dominated by the narrow component, while residual blueshifts and redshifts relative to rotation are observed in the nuclear strip, where we also observe the highest values of the [ N ii ] /H , [ S ii ] /H and [ O i ] /H line ratios, and an increase of the gas temperature ( 18000 K), velocity dispersion and electron density ( 500 ). We interpret these residuals and increased line ratios as due to a lateral expansion of the ambient gas in the nuclear strip due to shocks produced by the passage of the radio jet.

A possible signature of inflow is revealed by an increase in the [ O i ] /H ratio values and velocity dispersions in the shape of two spiral arms extending to 2.3 kpc north-east and south-west from the nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 33 as a spectacular HEG with extended disturbed emission, high off-nuclear [O III]/H ratios, and ionized patches linked to star formation, jet shocks, or interaction.

quotes.quote: Spectacular are the cases of 3C 33 and 3C 180. Their extended emission-line regions (ELRs) show tails, bridges, shells, irregular features and amorphous halos.
Interesting is the case of 3C 33, two regions with [ O III ] /H line ratios of 4-8 are observed 1.5 ( 1.7 kpc) to the north-east and south-west of the nuclear region.

The extended disturbed patches of ionized gas observed in 3C 33, 3C 234 and 3C 285 seem to be associated with regions of intense star formation, induced by jet shocks or by a tidal merger/interaction, as suggested by the HST images madrid06,floyd10,baldi08 . These sources are all classified as HEGs.

sources.summary: MUSE maps 3C 033 as an FR II/HEG radio galaxy whose ionized gas extends around the nucleus and shows ordered rotation with twisting line nodes.

quotes.quote: 3C 033: FR II/HEG, 1 = 1.17 kpc. The MUSE field-of-view covers only 1/4 of the extension of the radio source. The ionized gas has an elliptical shape, extending out to 8 ( 9 kpc) on both sides of the nucleus, and elongated along PA 50 ^ , then twisting along PA 70 ^ . The velocity field is broadly dominated by ordered rotation, with a line of nodes initially at PA 75 ^ , then twisting at smaller angles. Outside the central regions of high velocity gradient, the most distant gas shows a constant rotation of 300 .

sources.summary: The MURALES paper highlights 3C 033 as the only source with a redshifted nuclear outflow wing, likely due to obscuration, and describes fast bipolar ionized gas.

quotes.quote: All but one source (3C 033) show blueshifted wings with v _ 20 velocities between approximately 1200 and 400 km s ^ 1 and v _ 50,wing velocities between 900 and 300 km s ^ 1 . In 3C 033, the high-speed residuals are located on the red side of the emission line (see Section properties_outflow for more details).

Nuclear obscuration can be the origin of the redshifted nuclear outflow found in the 3C 033. In this HEG we do not have a clear view of the central nuclear outflow, but we can observe the fast-moving ionized gas in its immediate vicinity, which shows a well-defined bipolar morphology (see Fig. fig:velocitymap ). The redshifted component of the bipolar outflow in the central region is most likely stronger than the blueshifted component and results in a net positive outflow velocity.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Suzaku observations of the nearby radio galaxy 3C 33, modeling its absorbed nuclear continuum and finding no neutral disk-reflection signatures.

quotes.quote: We present results from a new 100-ks observation of the nearby radio galaxy 3C 33, and investigate the nature of absorption, reflection, and jet production in this source. We model the 2--70 keV nuclear continuum with a power law that is absorbed either through one or more layers of pc-scale neutral material, or through a modestly ionized pc-scale obscurer. The expected signatures of reflection from a neutral accretion disk are absent in 3C 33: there is no evidence of a relativistically blurred Fe K emission line, and no Compton reflection hump above 10 keV. We discuss the implications of this for the nature of jet production in 3C 33.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 33's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 33 is a FR II radio object identified with the galaxy LEDA 4088 at a redshift of z= 0.0597 ( D 273 Mpc). It hosts a Sy 2 AGN koski_spectrophotometry_1978 with detected polarized broad lines cohen_polarimetry_1999 . It is also an X-ray ``buried" AGN candidate noguchi_new_2009 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 033 as an elliptical Seyfert with a small upward BPT trend likely tied to measurement uncertainty or stellar-continuum over-subtraction.

quotes.quote: 3C 033 : This is an elliptical Seyfert galaxy having a small upward trend on the BPTs, but within the uncertainties of the emission-line measurements this could be statistically insignificant. Having a quiescent star-formation activity as an early-type galaxy, this small upward placement with increasing aperture is indicative of possible over-subtraction of the stellar continuum.
Again, the upward tendency of 3C 033 on the optical diagnostic diagrams can be attributed to the uncertainties in the emission-line measurements.

gmw_00000049.name: 3C 78
gmw_00000049.overview: 3C 78 is an isolated Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy [arXiv:0901.0913, arXiv:1910.00952] hosted by the early-type galaxy NGC 1218 [arXiv:0901.0913, arXiv:1310.2770]. The galaxy features a face-on dust disk [arXiv:0901.0913], a central compact X-ray core [arXiv:astro-ph/0408451], and an approximately conical synchrotron jet [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:2308.00842] that exhibits extensive polarization [arXiv:0901.0913, arXiv:astro-ph/0509559].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C78 as an isolated E/S0 FRI radio galaxy with a dust disk, optical jet, extensive VLBI jet polarization, and a transverse RM difference.

quotes.quote: 3C78 resides in the isolated, small E/S0 galaxy NGC1218 Matthews64 at a distance of 124 Mpc. HST observations have revealed that the optical galaxy has a face-on dust disk and an optical jet Sparks95 . Baum88 have reported that the innermost emission-line gas is elongated roughly in the direction of the radio jet, following the ``alignment effect'' e.g., McCarthy87,Privon08 .

3C78: Among the three FRI radio galaxies considered here, 3C78 shows the most extensive polarization along its radio jet. We detect polarization in the jet at 3 and 6 mas from the VLBI core at 5, 8 and 15 GHz.

3C78 is the only one of the three radio galaxies for which the rotation measure could be estimated using all the three frequencies. The rotation measure in 3C78 ranges from about +200 rad m ^ 2 to 400 rad m ^ 2 . We observe an RM difference of >300 rad m ^ 2 transverse to the inner VLBI jet over a region that is 5 mas (two beam-sizes) across (Fig. fig:3c78rm ).

sources.summary: The paper highlights 3C 78 as an FR I radio galaxy among the best new TeV candidates, with simulated short CTA observations suggesting detectability by current Cherenkov observatories.

quotes.quote: Only potentially new TeV sources with a TS 100 in the 50 hours observations with E_ cut =0.5 TeV have been considered, i.e. 3C 78, 4C +39.12, B2 1113+29, Cen B, B2 1447+27 and TXS 1541+050. Not surprisingly, all of these sources are classified as FR I. Our likelihood analysis of the simulated five hours observations shows that 3C 78 and TXS 1541+050 can be detected with TS > 25 even with short integration times, in the case E_ cut =0.5 TeV. This suggests that a VHE detection of these sources is most likely already within reach of current ground-based ray observatories.

We have found that two FR I sources, i.e., 3C 78 and TXS 1541+050, would still be detected with >5 significance within 5 hours, under the assumption of E_ cut =0.5 TeV. This implies that these two sources are very likely within reach of current-generation Cherenkov observatories, if observed for a full targeted campaign, i.e. with an integration time of a few tens of hours.

sources.summary: The paper reports VLBI observations of 3C 78, detecting pc-scale core and jet polarization, ordered magnetic fields, and alternating transverse and longitudinal jet-field regions reminiscent of BL Lac jets.

quotes.quote: We observed the four FRI radio galaxies viz., 3C 66B, 3C 78, 3C 264 and 3C 270, at 8.4 GHz ( ) with the Very Large Baseline Array (VLBA) 4 The NRAO is operated for the National Science Foundation (NSF) by Associated Universities, Inc. (AUI), under a cooperative agreement. The European VLBI Network (EVN) is a joint facility of European, Chinese, South African and other radio astronomy institutes funded by their national research councils. and five stations of the European VLBI Network ^4 viz., Effelsberg, Onsala, Yebes, Medicina, and the phased WSRT, on 2002 March 1. The observations were made in a ``snap-shot'' mode with the observing time per target totalling five to eight hours.

The fractional polarization in the pc-scale radio cores is m_ 0.4-1 , with the degree of polarization rising to m_ i-j 5-10 in the inner jet ( 1 pc from the core; see Table 1). We also detected jet polarization of m_ j 20 further from the cores of 3C 78 and 3C 264, and m_ j reaches 60 in a knot about 1.5 pc from the core of 3C 264. These values are comparable to those observed for the pc-scale jets of BL Lacs and quasars, indicating the presence of appreciably ordered B fields and little depolarization.

3C 78 shows a clear alternation of regions with transverse and longitudinal inferred B field along its jet, reminiscent of similar alternating B field structures observed in the BL Lacs OJ 287 GabuzdaGomez01 and 1418+546 (P05), which have been interpreted as evidence for ``global'' intrinsic fields associated with the jets.

sources.summary: 3C 78 is newly analyzed with Suzaku and Fermi data; its X-rays are argued to be jet-origin and its SED is fit with a one-zone synchrotron/self-Compton model.

quotes.quote: The Suzaku spectra for five of those have been presented previously, while the remaining three (M 87, PKS 0625 354, and 3C 78) are presented here for the first time. Based on the Fe-K line strength, X-ray variability, and X-ray power-law photon indices, and using additional information on the [ O III ] line emission, we argue for a jet origin of the observed X-ray emission in these three sources. We also analyzed five years of Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) GeV gamma-ray data on PKS 0625 354 and 3C 78 to understand these sources within the blazar picture.

Overall, we note that the Suzaku spectra for both PKS 0625 354 and 3C 78 are rather soft, while the LAT spectra are unusually hard when compared with other ray detected low-power (FR I) radio galaxies. We demonstrate that the constructed broad-band spectral energy distributions of PKS 0625 354 and 3C 78 are well described by a one-zone synchrotron/synchrotron self-Compton model.

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C 78 as a radio galaxy, measuring knot motions, modeling its jet SED, and finding an approximately conical jet with evidence for bulk acceleration.

quotes.quote: We present our initial work on the jet of radio-galaxy 3C 78, as part of CAgNVAS (Catalogue of proper motions in Active galactic Nuclei using Very Large Array Studies), with a goal of constraining nature of jet plasma on larger ( >100 parsec) scales. In 3C 78 we find three prominent knots (A, B and C), where knot B undergoes subluminal longitudinal motion ( 0.6c at 200 pc), while knot C undergoes extreme (apparent) backward motion and eventual forward motion ( 2.6c , 0.5c , at 300 pc). Assuming knots are shocks, we infer the bulk speeds from the pattern motion of Knots B and C. We model the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the large-scale jet and observe that a physically motivated two-zone model can explain most of the observed emission. We also find that the jet profile remains approximately conical from parsec to kiloparsec scales.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1218's gamma-ray SED with a log-parabola rather than a power law and identifies it as variable, hard-spectrum, and emitting above 30 GeV.

quotes.quote: In the NGC 1218 SED (Figure fig:seds-setB ) we see some tension between a power-law model description and the highest energy bin upper limit. As a result we also considered a log-parabola model for NGC 1218, and find that with a test statistic value of 235 between the log-parabola and power-law models, the fit significantly improves and hence we discard the initial power-law model.
Apart from NGC 1218 and NGC 6251, all the radio galaxy SEDs in this subset are best-fitted with a simple power-law model.
Six of the radio galaxies studied, 3C 264, 4C +39.12, B2 1447+27, NGC 1218, NGC 2329 and PKS 0625-35, have particularly hard spectra and emission above 30 GeV.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 1218 among seven nearby gamma-ray loud 1LAC AGNs associated with UHECR directions within 16.9 degrees and considered potential accelerators.

quotes.quote: We identify 7 `` ray loud'' AGNs which are associated with UHECRs within 17^ and are likely candidates for the production sites of UHECRs: Centaurus A, NGC 4945, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 1218, RX J0008.0+1450 and NGC 253.

Within the distance _1 , 4 PAO events correlate with 3 AGNs: NGC 4945, Centaurus A and 4C+04.77; at _2 , 7 PAO events correlate with 4 AGNs: NGC 4945, Centaurus A, ESO 323-G77 and 4C+04.77; at _3 , 17 events are associated with 7 AGNs: NGC 4945, Centaurus A, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 253, NGC 1218 and RXJ0008.0+1450.

The potential UHECR accelerators Our results pinpoint 7 `` ray loud'' 1LAC AGNs as the potential sites for the production of PAO UHECRs (see Table tab:agn ): Cen A, NGC 4945, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 1218, RX J0008.0+1450 and NGC 253 (ordered by increasing number of associated UHECRs at the angular distance 16.9^ ).

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 78's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 78 is a FR I radio source coinciding with the early-type spiral galaxy NGC 1218 at a redshift of z= 0.0287 ( D 127 Mpc), which is a radio-loud Sy 1 AGN. A prominent synchrotron jet, similar to M87, was detected at radio and optical wavelengths (PA 50 ; unger_kiloparsec_1984,sparks_discovery_1995 ), which coincides with the inner optical emission line structure baum_extended_1988 . The first MIR observations of 3C 78 were performed by .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes 0305+039 (3C78) and finds its RM maps show a roughly transverse gradient, clearer and 3.6 sigma with an equal-area circular beam.

quotes.quote: 0305+039 (3C78). The RM maps for this source are shown in Fig. fig:rmmaps-PK . The RM map made using the elliptical beam (left panel) shows a roughly transverse RM gradient with a significance of about 2.7 . The RM map made with an equal-area circular beam (middle panel) shows a clear, monotonic transverse RM gradient across this region, with a significance of 3.6 , whose direction is very close to orthogonal to the jet.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 78 as a radio galaxy whose strong core pileup prevents reliable radial-profile fitting, but the pileup itself indicates unresolved X-ray emission and a central compact X-ray core.

quotes.quote: 3C 78: due to the strong core pileup, which distorts the shape of the PSF, no reliable fit to the radial profiles could be performed. However, the presence of central pileup is di per se indication of strong unresolved X-ray emission. Therefore, we conclude that a CCCX is present in 3C 78.

gmw_00000050.name: 3C 93
gmw_00000050.overview: 3C 93 is a radio-loud quasar at a redshift of approximately 0.357 [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:1807.10774] that is optically classified as a Seyfert 1.0 galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770]. It features a lobe-dominated radio morphology with symmetric biconical lobes [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:1807.10774] and exhibits extended X-ray emission on scales of hundreds of kiloparsecs [arXiv:1807.10774].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3CR 93 as a lobe-dominated quasar with an HST-resolved host, bright X-ray core, power-law nuclear spectrum, and significant extended X-ray emission on hundreds of kpc scales.

quotes.quote: 3CR 93 . This is a z =0.358 quasar with a lobe-dominated radio morphology see e.g. bogers94 . In the optical image, available in the HST archive, 3CR 93 has a host galaxy with 3 \ diameter lehnert99 . In the X-ray image the core is clearly detected with more than 1000 counts but there is no detection of hotspots. X-ray spectral analysis of the nuclear emission shows a power law spectrum with absorption consistent with the Galactic column density. Significant extended X-ray emission on hundreds of kpc scale was found around 3CR 93 with a significance above 3 (see Fig. fig:clusters ).

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 93's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 93 is a radio-loud quasar with an optical Sy 1.0 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 at a redshift of z= 0.3571 ( D 1967 Mpc) and thus the most distant object in the AGN MIR atlas. It features symmetric biconical radio lobes on supergalactic scales in the north-east and south-west directions but no clear detections of collimated jets (PA 45 ; FR II; e.g., price_vla_1993,bogers_high_1994 ). No data are available for 3C 93, and it appears as a faint point source in the images.

gmw_00000051.name: 3C 98
gmw_00000051.overview: 3C 98 is an elliptical radio galaxy [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1310.2770] classified as a type 2 Seyfert [arXiv:1505.01166, arXiv:1310.2770] that exhibits X-ray flux variability [arXiv:1505.01166]. It features a double-lobe radio structure [arXiv:0908.4391, arXiv:1310.2770] with a collimated jet aligned near the host galaxy's major axis [arXiv:0912.3001, arXiv:1310.2770].

sources.summary: The paper describes 0356+10 as a narrow-line FR II radio galaxy with extended emission lines, double lobes, hotspots, a radio jet, and a compact VLBI core.

quotes.quote: 0356+10 -- (3C 98) -- The host galaxy is a typical narrow-line radio galaxy (NLRG), with extended emission lines studied by . On kiloparsec scales, 3C 98 shows a double-lobe FRII radio structure which spans 216 arcsec at 8.35 GHz, with a radio jet that crosses the northern lobe and terminates in a bright hotspot. There is little evidence of a southern jet, but a twin hotspot in the southern lobe is present ( ). The core of this symmetric FRII radio galaxy is pointlike in our VLBI image, with the total flux density 2.2 mJy. Most of the arcsecond core flux is missing on the parsec scale ( S_ VLBA /S_ 24 ), suggesting the presence of faint jets below our noise level and invisible because they are oriented in the plane of the sky.

sources.summary: 3C 98 has ISM near chip boundaries but clear ellipticity, diffuse thermal emission near the northern radio lobe, and a radio jet aligned near the host major axis.

quotes.quote: 3C 98 ( z=0.030 ) : The ISM of the galaxy in this ACIS-I observation is very close to the chip boundaries. However, it has clear ellipticity and is not contaminated by any companions, so we are able to fit an ellipse. There is diffuse thermal emission associated with the northern radio lobe that is similar to the ISM, but we see no evidence for such gas in the southern lobe. Notably, the orientation of the major axis of the HST image in martel99 disagrees with our DSS ellipse by 20^ . The HST image is more reliable, meaning that the radio jet is aligned close to the major axis of the host.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 98.0 as an optically classified type 2 Seyfert with radio core and jets, X-ray flux variability, and nuclear-continuum variations.

quotes.quote: 3C 98.0 Using the optical line measurements in , it can be optically classified as a type 2 Seyfert (see an optical spectrum in Appendix multiimages ). A nuclear core plus jets structure was observed at radio frequencies with VLA leahy1997 . 3C 98.0 was observed twice with XMM -Newton in 2002 and 2003 and once with Chandra in 2008. studied the two XMM -Newton data, and fitted its spectra with a thermal plus a power law model, reporting X-ray luminosities of log(L(2--10 keV)) = 42.90 [ 42.88-42.93 ] and 42.66 [ 42.60-42.71 ] , respectively, indicating flux variability. These measurements agree well with ours, where variations due to the nuclear continuum were found.

sources.summary: 3C 098 is an FR II/HEG with bright emission-line knots on both sides of the nucleus, complex but rotating gas kinematics, and radially structured excitation.

quotes.quote: 3C 098: FR II/HEG, z=0.030, 1 = 0.60 kpc (see Fig. 3c098 ). A series of bright knots extends on both sides of the nucleus to a distance of 15 kpc, oriented at PA 10^ , 35^ away from the radio axis. The velocity field is quite complex, but a general rotation is seen with the S side of the ELR receding from us and the N side approaching. The line width exceeds the instrumental value only in the central component of the ELR. The / \ ratio has a radial structure, decreasing at larger angles from the radio axis, while no trend with distance is observed.

sources.summary: The atlas describes 3C 98 as an undisturbed elliptical FR I radio galaxy with a smaller elliptical companion, no visible NICMOS2 internal structure, and north-south double radio lobes.

quotes.quote: 3C 98; z=0.030 A NLRG FR I, hosted by an undisturbed elliptical with smaller companion elliptical. No internal structure is visible in the NICMOS2 image. The radio source is double lobed, running almost north-south miller+85 .

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 98's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 98 is a FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy LEDA 14213 at a redshift of z= 0.0305 ( D 137 Mpc). It contains a radio-loud Sy 2 AGN veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It features the classical FR II super-galactic radio lobes in the north-south directions with a collimated jet extending into the northern lobe (PA 20 ; e.g., baum_extended_1988,leahy_study_1997 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds 3C 98 has a short-wavelength mid-infrared SED affected by starlight contamination, so 3.6 m is excluded, while extinction remains high without silicate absorption.

quotes.quote: It is clear from Fig. tablefluxAGN that the mid-IR SED turns up toward shorter mid-IR wavelengths for 3C 192, and to a smaller degree for 3C 98 and 3C 305.
Because 3C 98 and 3C 305 have starlight contamination at 3.6 m, we did not use this wavelength to estimate the spectral index for these two sources.
For instance, in 3C 98 and 3C 192 no silicate absorption is detected, however, a large amount of extinction is derived based on the other four independent techniques.

gmw_00000052.name: 3C 105
gmw_00000052.overview: The galaxy 3C 105 is a Fanaroff-Riley II narrow-line radio galaxy [arXiv:0810.3764, arXiv:1305.3622] hosted by a highly flattened, faintly disturbed elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1003.2467]. Its active nucleus is heavily obscured [arXiv:1003.2467, arXiv:1305.3622] and exhibits highly absorbed, non-variable line-of-sight X-ray emission [arXiv:1305.3622, arXiv:2301.07138].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 105 as a narrow-line radio galaxy with possible relic jet-direction protrusions and a structured southern hotspot selected for near-infrared observations.

quotes.quote: 3C 105 at redshift z=0.089 is hosted by a Narrow Line Radio Galaxy (Tadhunter et al. 1993). The most striking properties of this source are the protusions in northern and southern direction of the northern and southern lobes, respectively. These could be interpreted as remainders of a previous jet direction. Both hotspots are well-defined with the southern one much brighter than its northern counterpart (see also Fig. 7 in Leahy et al. 1997). For our near-infrared detection experiment we have selected the southern hotspot complex (Fig. 1).

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 105 as a faint, highly flattened and disturbed FR II elliptical host whose extreme red color likely reflects heavy obscuration.

quotes.quote: 3C 105 is an FR II radio source at z=0.089 , hosted by a highly flattened and faintly disturbed (in our H band image) elliptical with M_H( Host ) = -22.88 . The host galaxy of 3C 105 is known to be exceptionally red, with strong stellar absorption lines tadhunter+93,cohen+99 , and both existing optical HST images provide insufficient detection to produce a color-map. We estimate an R-H color for the host galaxy of 8 2 (very approximate due to the barely 3 detection in R band) indicating a very heavily obscured host galaxy. Cohen et al. commented that the lack of polarized flux could be due to the obscuration of the scatterers, but discount this hypothesis on the basis that the obscuring material would have to cover a large fraction of the ISM. The HST images, although shallow in the optical, suggest that a large quantity of absorbing material could indeed be the cause.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 105 as an FRII narrow-line radio galaxy whose Suzaku spectrum is highly absorbed, variable, and not dominated by Compton reflection.

quotes.quote: The source 3C 105 is an FRII narrow-line radio galaxy with a spatial size of the radio emission of 764 kpc ( baum88 , hard98 ), associated to an elliptical host galaxy ( do07 ). From the radio map of , both jets are detected, but the radio emission is dominated by the southern hotspot and the wealth of other knots and structures suggests ( lea97 ) that the system is rapidly evolving, with a short lifetime. Chandra detected X-ray emission from the core and southern hot spot of 3C 105 in December 2007 ( mas10 , hereafter M10), both from the region where the jet appears to enter the hot spot and at the terminal hotspot itself.

The source 3C 105 intrinsic power-law emission is highly absorbed by an N _ H 5 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , while 1.4 of the nuclear X-ray photons is scattered at lower energies and reaches the observer unabsorbed. As previously noted, the XIS extracted region also covers the radio galaxy southern hotspot.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 3C 105 is well fitted by all torus models, agrees with Zhao2020, and does not require line-of-sight N_H variability.

quotes.quote: 3C 105 Data reduction/fitting: No issues to report. Analysis of results: The data is well-fitted by all models, which are in good agreement. Our results are also consistent with those of Zhao2020 . Introducing N_ H,los variability is not necessary to explain the data, and the p-value is also >0.01 for all models. We thus classify this source as `Non-variable in N_ H,los '.

sources.summary: J0407+0342 is presented as a Seyfert 2 galaxy whose host has bulge and disk components, FR II radio morphology, and an unresolved low-resolution spectrum.

quotes.quote: J0407+0342 This is a Seyfert 2 galaxy. According to Inskip et al. (2010), the host galaxy has both a bulge and a disk component, with the first being the brighter of the two. In radio the source has a typical FR II morphology, with a weak core and bright hot spots . Its spectrum, retrieved in the NED archive and derived from the Low Resolution Spectrograph at TNG, has a spectral resolution of 20 , therefore the [ OIII ] line was not resolved.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 105's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 105 is a FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy LEDA 14492 at a redshift of z= 0.089 ( D 421 Mpc). It contains a radio-loud Sy 2 AGN veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 that belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. It features the classical FR II super-galactic radio lobes in north-west and south-east directions without a collimated jet evident (PA 20 ; e.g., baum_extended_1988,leahy_study_1997 ).

sources.summary: The paper combines multi-epoch spectra for 3C 105, fixes its inclination because the data cannot constrain it, and derives X-ray spectral absorption parameters.

quotes.quote: For the case of 3C 105, we also combined the spectra of the three observations spanning over 2 days (Table tab:obs ).
For 3C 105, we fix it at i=70^ , since it cannot be constrained by our data.

gmw_00000053.name: 3C 120
gmw_00000053.overview: 3C 120 is a nearby broad-line radio galaxy [arXiv:0806.4240, arXiv:1305.4937] and Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1907.08632, arXiv:1412.3559] hosted by a disturbed S0 or spiral disk [arXiv:1907.08632, arXiv:1710.02194]. The active nucleus drives a superluminal radio jet [arXiv:0806.4240, arXiv:1412.3559] and exhibits strong multiwavelength variability [arXiv:1801.09381, arXiv:2109.03619].

sources.summary: The paper presents 43 and 86 GHz VLBI maps of 3C 120, measuring parsec-scale jet structure, spectral-index changes, and steepening with core separation.

quotes.quote: In Figure 3c120b we show a 43 GHz VLBA map and a 86 GHz GMVA map of the radio galaxy 3C 120, observed in October 2004. The left panel shows uv-tapered maps convolved with a (0.1 x 0.3) mas beam. The right panel shows the un-tapered central 0.5 mas region of the jet, convolved with the smaller 86 GHz beam (0.06 x 0.25 mas). At the redshift of 3C 120 ( z=0.033 ) an angular scale of 1 mas corresponds to a spatial scale of 0.63 pc.

Assuming negligible variability on the 16 day interval between the two observing dates, it is possible to determine the spectral indices _ 43/86 GHz along the jet ( S ^ ). For the labeled regions A to F in Figure 3c120b (left), we obtain spectral indices of -0.35 for A ( =0 mas), -0.07 for B ( =0.1 mas), -0.36 for C ( =0.25 - 0.4 mas), -0.46 for D ( =0.8 mas), -0.7 for E ( =1.2 mas) and -0.8 for F ( =1.8-2.2 mas). A general trend of spectral steepening with increasing core separation is obvious. We note that component B exhibits a much flatter spectrum than the core component A.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C 120 as a bright broad-line radio galaxy with a superluminal jet, X-ray variability, Suzaku observations, and evidence linking jet activity to the accretion disk.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 ( z = 0.033) is the brightest broad line radio galaxy (BLRG), exhibiting characteristics intermediate between those of FR-I radio galaxies and BL Lacs. It has a one-sided superluminal jet on 100 kpc scales ( ), and superluminal motion (with an apparent velocity _ app = 8.1) has been observed for the jet component. This provides an upper limit to the inclination angle of the jet to the line of sight of 14 deg ( ). Interestingly, found that dips in the X-ray emission of 3C 120 are followed by ejections of bright superluminal knots in the radio jet, which clearly indicates an important connection between the jet and the accretion disk. In X-rays, 3C 120 has been known to be a bright ( 5 10 ^ 11 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 at 2 10 keV), variable source with a canonical power-law spectral shape that softened as the source brightened (e.g., ).

3C 120 was observed with Suzaku four times in February and March 2006 with a total (requested) duration of 160 ksec ( ). It was in a relatively high state during the 1st 40 ksec observation ( 1 ; the summed count rates of 4 XISs detectors was 15.94 0.01 counts s ^ 1 ), then its count rate dropped by 20 in the 2nd observation, and finally reached a minimum in the 4th observation ( 4 ; 12.02 0.01 counts s ^ 1 ). As given in Figure 5 ( left ), Suzaku has successfully resolved the iron K line complex of 3C 120 and also was first to verify the broad component's asymmetry.

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C120 as a nearby X-ray bright broad-line radio galaxy with a merger-marked host and superluminal jet, supporting a jet-cycle with a refilling disk.

quotes.quote: In this work we study 3C120 ( z=0.033 ), a nearby and X-ray bright BLRG with a bulge dominated host, showing signs of a recent merger garcia:05a . It is known to harbor a black hole with a mass of (5.7 2.7) 10^7 M _ pozo:12a . 3C120 has been studied in detail at many wavelengths e.g., chatterjee:09a and is well known to show a one sided superluminal jet.

Our preferred spectral model to describe the multi-epoch XMM-Newton and Suzaku data is the jet + disk model (see discussion in jetdisk ). The fits of this model to the data directly support the predictions of the jet-cycle model. We find a recessed accretion disk ( R_ in 20-70R_ isco ) during the 2012- Suzaku observations when the X-ray flux is on a rising trend and the jet-cycle picture would suggest a refilling accretion disk. This scenario is supported by a rising trend in the UV flux.

Our overall picture is supported by the appearance in the jet of a new superluminal knot in the VLBA images about one month after the 2012- Suzaku pointings and two months following the middle of the X-ray dip between JD 2455915 (December, 19th 2011) and 2455960 (February, 2nd 2012).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 120 as a nearby Seyfert 1 broad-line radio galaxy with a massive black hole, powerful radio jet, Fermi gamma-ray detection, and compact variable emission region.

quotes.quote: At the red shift z=0.033 , is a nearby Seyfert 1 radio galaxy which is active and powerful emitter of radiation at all the observed wavebands. Having a bright continuum and broad optical emission lines is usually classified as a Broad Line Radio Galaxy (BLRG). Hosting a black hole with a mass 5.5 10^ 7 M_ , well constrained from the reverberation mapping peterson04 , it has a radio morphology more similar to the Fanaroff-Riley class I radio sources fanaroff74 . The source has a powerful one sided radio jet extending from a sub- pc up to 100 kpc scales walker . Observations with Very Long Baseline Array at frequencies (22, 43, and 86 GHz) reveal a very rich inner jet structure containing several superluminal components with apparent speed up to 4-6 homan01,gomes98,gomes99 that can be investigated with better resolution than most other extragalactic superluminal sources because of the relatively low redshift.

At High Energies (HE; >100 MeV) the source has not been detected with the EGRET on board the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory even though with several pointing observations lin93 . The 2 upper limit on the source flux above 100 MeV is set to 9 10^ 8 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . Afterwards, the source is detected with the Fermi Large Are Telescope ( ) using 15 months of all sky exposures abdo10c .

sources.summary: The paper maps radio galaxy 3C 120, resolving its southwestern jet beyond 10 mas and five model components, while noting future KaVA data can test jet kinematics.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 3C 120 (Figure fig:fig4 ) is well known for its extended structure toward the south-west. The source is classified as radio galaxy with a redshift of 0.033 Michel1988 , corresponding to an angular scale of 0.65 pc/mas. Our map shows an extended jet reaching out to more than 10 mas from the core. 3C 120 has been studied with respect to its ray emission e.g., Sahakyan2015, Casadio2015 and its polarization e.g., and references therein Gomez2008 .

Here, we focus on its jet kinematics. The known maximum apparent jet speed is 6.43c Lister2013 . With additional data from future KaVA observation, we expect to trace the motion of the jet components and to test its kinematics. We were able to detect five model components, which also shows a general trend of increasing size as function of distance from the core.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 87 MOJAVE images of the radio galaxy 3C 120, finding no statistically separate transverse components but measuring longitudinal jet acceleration followed by apparent deceleration.

quotes.quote: Acceleration in the jet of 3C 120 The analysis of the jet in 3C 120 comprises the total of 87 MOJAVE images covering the period of 1996--2010. The average beam of the MOJAVE images is 0.5 mas 1.2 mas. The SWD decomposition was performed using four wavelet scales ranging between 0.2 mas (scale 1) and 1.6 mas (scale 4). The SCC result does not reveal any statistically different velocity components, nor does comparing SCC results individually on small- and large-scale structure. Contrary to 3C 273, the jet in 3C 120 is not transversally resolved in the MOJAVE images, which limit the possibility of detecting the flow stratification.

However, the high precision of the SCC measurement can be used in this case to track the evolution of the flow speed with distance from the core. We computed the SCC individually for ten regions of the jet between a distance from the core from 0.5 mas to 10.5 mas with a bin size of 1 mas. The result of this analysis (Fig.  fig:3c120_acceleration ) reveals a significant flow acceleration from _ app = 4.0 0.2 at z_ obs = 1 mas to _ app = 6.0 0.4 at z_ obs = 7 mas, followed by an apparent deceleration of the flow.

sources.summary: The paper monitors 3C 120 in a bright state and argues its broad-line and dust reverberation behavior supports a bowl-shaped BLR-dust torus with foreshortening effects.

quotes.quote: Therefore, we have embarked on such a study of 3C 120. The BLR galaxy belongs to the most frequently monitored Seyfert-1 galaxies and results from several campaigns have been reported: \ spectroscopic RM by covering the H region, velocity resolved RM covering H , H , and H by and . Photometric reverberation mapping (PRM) of H has been reported by .

For the 3C 120 data, a tentative correction of the foreshortening effect by spectroscopic or geometric H/R_x estimates yields consistent M _ vir values for different Balmer lines ( , ) and different AGN luminosities. Evidence favoring a bowl-shaped BLR dust geometry The cross correlations of the respective light curves B - and B -NIR yield that the echo is broad with a pronounced low- peak (Fig. fig:dcf_BHa ) and the dust echo is symmetric and sharper than the BLR echo (Fig. fig:dcf_BJ ). To make these results consistent, we compared the data with bowl-shaped BLR--torus models by KM and GKR. We adopted an inclination i = 16^ . The KM bowl is more convex than the GKR bowl. The comparison shows, if the hot dust emission comes from the entire rim, then, both the KM and GKR models yield no good fit of the lag to the data. The predicted asymmetries in the transfer functions and CCFs are not consistent with the symmetry of the data.

sources.summary: The paper reports reverberation mapping of 3C 120, finding a changed, blueshifted rms H profile, a shorter rest-frame lag, and complex velocity-resolved BLR structure.

quotes.quote: As the first paper of the series, we provide here our RM measurements of 4 AGNs: 3C 120, Ark 120, Mrk 6, and SBS 1518+593 (see Table tab:obj for their coordinates and some general information). Their luminosities place them among Seyfert galaxies and broad line radio galaxies rather than the more energetic quasar category. Ark 120 and Mrk 6 show extreme red asymmetric profiles, while 3C 120 and SBS 1518+593 show milder red asymmetries. None are extreme super-Eddington accretors, although 3C 120 and SBS 1518+593 have dimensionless accretion rates on order unity see also sec:BHmass du2015 . The light curves of all these sources have shown unambiguous peaks or dips, and are sufficient for us to measure \ time lags.

The detected \ lag is 20.2_ 4.2 ^ +5.0 days in the rest frame, which is slightly shorter than the value in , but the difference is not significant considering the uncertainties. In addition, the rms spectrum in our campaign is different from that in . Our rms spectrum is significantly blueshifted (see Figure fig:v_resolved_lags ), while the rms spectrum in has a strong red asymmetry. This implies that the varying part of the BLR in 3C 120 has significantly changed after 7 years.

sources.summary: The paper presents LCO reverberation mapping of broad-line radio galaxy 3C 120, measuring emission-line and continuum lags, black-hole mass, velocity-resolved lags, and accretion-disc continuum lag behavior.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We carried out photometric and spectroscopic observations of the well-studied broad-line radio galaxy 3C 120 with the Las Cumbres Observatory (LCO) global robotic telescope network from 2016 December to 2018 April as part of the LCO AGN Key Project on Reverberation Mapping of Accretion Flows. Here, we present both spectroscopic and photometric reverberation mapping results. We used the interpolated cross-correlation function (ICCF) to perform multiple-line lag measurements in 3C 120. We find the H , He II 4686 , H and He I 5876 lags of _ cen = 18.8_ 1.0 ^ +1.3 , 2.7_ 0.8 ^ +0.7 , 21.2_ 1.0 ^ +1.6 , and 16.9_ 1.1 ^ +0.9 days respectively, relative to the V-band continuum. Using the measured lag and rms velocity width of the H emission line, we determine the mass of the black hole for 3C 120 to be M= 6.3^ +0.5 _ 0.3 10^7 (f/5.5) M _ .

Our black hole mass measurement is consistent with similar previous studies on 3C 120, but with small uncertainties. In addition, velocity-resolved lags in 3C 120 show a symmetric pattern across the H line, 25 days at line centre decreasing to 17 days in the line wings at 4000 km s ^ 1 . We also investigate the inter-band continuum lags in 3C 120 and find that they are generally consistent with ^ 4/3 as predicted from a geometrically-thin, optically-thick accretion disc.

Here, we undertake a robotic RM study of 3C 120 (Table object_properties ) from long-term monitoring with the Las Cumbres Observatory (LCO) global robotic telescope network. 3C 120 was shown to exhibit a type 1 Seyfert spectrum by 1967PASP...79..369S and it was one of the first radio sources for which variability was confirmed 1966ApJ...146..634P .

sources.summary: The paper applies PFVG to 3C120, a nearby Fanaroff-Riley I radio galaxy with strong variability, recovering host-galaxy fluxes consistent with SED/FVG estimates despite H-alpha contamination.

quotes.quote: 3C120 3C120 is a nearby Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy at redshift z = 0.033 ( 1987ApJ...316..546W ). It has often been the target of several RM campaigns due to its strong variability, with amplitudes of up to 2 mag on timescales of a few years ( 2010ApJ...711..461S ; 2012A&A...545A..84P ; 2012ApJ...755...60G ). More recently, (R+2018) carried out a PRM campaign in order to study the BLR and the dust torus. R+2018 used the Johnson B and V broadbands to estimate the AGN continuum emission and the R and I broadbands to estimate the fraction of the continuum underneath the H emission line. The FVG analysis by R+2018 was performed using the BVI bands.

Following R+2018, we adopted a host-galaxy color given by an S0 template ( 1996ApJ...467...38K ). The results are shown in Figure fig:App3C120 . Notably, the recovered R band galaxy flux 5.24 0.32 mJy is in full agreement with the SED value reported by R+2018.

sources.summary: The paper includes 3C 120 as a Seyfert 1 reverberation-mapping target, drawing on prior SRM work, high-cadence LCO data, and tests of extracted broadband H light curves.

quotes.quote: Here we present an ICCF-Cut method for broadband PRM to obtain the H broad line lag and apply it to four Seyfert 1 galaxies, MCG+08-11-011, NGC 2617, 3C 120 and NGC 5548. All of them have high quality broadband lightcurves with daily/sub-daily cadence, which enables us to extract H lightcurves from the line band by subtracting the contributions from the continuum and host galaxy. Their extracted H lightcurves are compared with the lagged continuum band lightcurves, as well as the lagged H lightcurves obtained by spectroscopic RM (SRM) at the same epochs. The consistency of these lightcurves and the comparison with the SRM H lags provide supports to the H lags of these AGNs, in a range from 9 to 19 days, obtained by the ICCF-Cut, JAVELIN and ^2 methods.

3C 120 has been widely studied with SRM 1998ApJ...501...82P,2012ApJ...755...60G,2013ApJ...764...47G,2014A&A...566A.106K,2020MNRAS.497.2910H . 2018A&A...620A.137R used the narrow band to obtain the H narrow band PRM lag for 3C 120, which is much larger than the SRM H lag at a different time epoch, mainly due to the much higher luminosity in this period.
We apply the same methods to 3C 120, as shown in Figure 7. But the extracted H line lightcurve is not well consistent with the continuum band (panel (b) in Figure 7).

sources.summary: The paper applies GPCC reverberation mapping to 3C120, finding a delay posterior near the ICCF estimates and deriving a black-hole mass consistent with earlier work.

quotes.quote: Based on the reported host subtracted luminosity of 3C120, L_ 5100 = (9.12 1.15) 10^ 43 erg \ s^ 1 (see Table 9 in G+2012), we infer the posterior distribution of the delay using the prior p( )= U (0, _ max (l,z)) as defined in Section sec:prior_on_delay (top panel, Figure fig:GPCCexamp ). The introduction of the physically motivated prior (see sec:prior_on_delay ) suppresses all peaks at larger delays and accentuates the peaks at lower delays. The peak of highest probability occurs at 27.6 days which is close to the ICCF peak and centroid estimates.

In the following, we use the recovered delay posterior (see top panel in Figure fig:GPCCexamp ), calculated with the prior presented in Section sec:prior_on_delay , to estimate the black hole mass distribution of 3C120 and Mrk1501. According to the virial theorem, the black hole mass M_ BH is given by M_ BH = f R _ V ^2 G \ , where _ V is the velocity dispersion of the emission lines, R= is the size of the BLR, and the factor f depends on the geometry and kinematics of the BLR ( 2014A&A...568A..36P and references therein). As in G+2012, we use the velocity dispersion given by the linewidth of the RMS residual spectrum, _ V = 1514 65 km/s (their table 9), and assume a scaling factor f=5.5 ( 2004ApJ...615..645O ). We show the resulting probability distribution for the black hole mass in Figure fig:3C120Bhmass . We emphasize that the distribution we calculate accounts for the uncertainty in the velocity dispersion _ V .

sources.summary: The paper presents GMVA 3 mm VLBI maps of 3C 120, resolving sub-mas jet structure, epoch-to-epoch misalignment, an S-shaped inner feature, and extended weak jet emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 120: In Figure 3c120 we show two VLBI maps of 3C 120 obtained with the GMVA in April 2003 (10 stations, mode 256-8-2, 11 hrs) and in October 2004 (12 stations, mode 512-8-2, 12 hrs). The dynamic ranges of the two maps are 200:1 and 300:1, respectively. At the redshift of z=0.033 the observing beam size of 0.25 0.06 mas corresponds to a spatial resolution of 0.16 0.04 pc, or in terms of a 3 10^7 M black hole to a linear scale of 4.1 10^4 R_S^7 for the minor beam axis. At and below the 0.5 mas scale, structural differences and a misalignment of the orientation of the inner jet between both epochs are visible. Much denser time sampled observations will be necessary to follow the expected motion of 2 mas/yr ( ).

Slightly super-resolving the map of April 2003, reveals an 'S-shaped' structure on the 0.1-0.2 mas scale. This and the change of the position angle of the sub-mas jet on a 1.5 yr time scale supports the idea of helical Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities, which may be excited by some sort of `rotation' at the jet base ( , ). Beyond the 0.5 mas scale, the 3 mm VLBI jet fades and breaks into filamentary sub-components.

sources.summary: VLBA observations of the radio galaxy 3C 120 show polarization and Faraday-rotation gradients along and across the jet, including a high-rotation region consistent with ambient interaction or a helical-field sheath.

quotes.quote: Very long baseline interferometric observations of the radio galaxy 3C 120 show a systematic presence of gradients in Faraday rotation and degree of polarization across and along the jet. These are revealed by the passage of multiple superluminal components throughout the jet as they move out from the core in a sequence of 12 monthly polarimetric observations taken with the VLBA at 15, 22, and 43 GHz. The degree of polarization has an asymmetric profile in which the northern side of the jet is more highly polarized. The Faraday rotation measure is also stratified across the jet width, with larger values for the southern side.

Superposed on this structure we find a localized region of high Faraday rotation measure ( 6000 rad m ^ 2 ) between approximately 3 and 4 mas from the core. This region of enhanced Faraday rotation may result from the interaction of the jet with the ambient medium, which may also explain the stratification in degree of polarization. The data are also consistent with a helical magnetic field in a two-fluid jet model, consisting of an inner emitting jet and a sheath of nonrelativistic electrons.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C120 as a nearby variable Seyfert 1 galaxy, measuring its reverberation lag, host-subtracted AGN luminosity, BLR size, and central black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: 3C120 is a nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy known to be strongly variable in the optical, characterized by short and long term variations with amplitudes of up to 2 mag on a timescale of 10 years ( 1979SvA....23..518L,1988AJ.....95..374W ). Subsequent studies showed amplitude variations of about 0.4 mag ( 1997MNRAS.292..273W ) and 1.5 mag ( 2010ApJ...711..461S ) on a timescale of 4 years monitoring. Furthermore, 3C120 was included in a complete eight years AGNs spectroscopic monitoring campaign conducted by 1998ApJ...501...82P . Although 3C120 was a lower priority object (with 50 days average intervals between observations) the spectroscopic reverberation mapping results have shown that the H emission line time response allows one to determine the BLR size with an uncertainty of about 50 ( 2004ApJ...613..682P ).

Through 3C120's redshift of z = 0.0331 , the H line falls into the OIII filter at 5007 . Consequently, it is an ideal candidate for our instrumentation, a robotic 15 cm refractor. Here we present new measurements of the BLR size, host-subtracted AGN luminosity and black hole mass based on a well-sampled photometric reverberation mapping campaign, allowing us to revisit the position of 3C120 in the BLR size - luminosity relationship.

sources.summary: The paper applies WISE to 87 VLBI images of the Seyfert galaxy 3C 120, tracing many moving jet features and helical structures consistent with instability models.

quotes.quote: The particular focus of the tests is on two prominent radio jets in the quasar 3C 273 and the Seyfert galaxy 3C 120. The MOJAVE database for 3C 120 comprises 87 images from observations made in 1996--2010, averaging to one observation every three months (but with individual gaps as long as one year). Applied to the MOJAVE images of 3C 120, WISE detects a total of 30 moving SSP.

We defer this to future analysis of the data on 3C 120, while noting again that WISE has achieved its basic goal of providing an effective automated measure of kinematics in a jet with remarkably rapid structural changes. The magnitude of the structural variability of the jet in 3C 120 is further emphasized in Fig. The shape of individual tracks suggests a helical morphology, consistent with the patterns predicted from modeling the jet in 3C 120 with linearly growing Kelvin-Helmholtz instability hardee_p_2005 . Altogether, the example of 3C 120 again demonstrates the reliability of the WISE decomposition and analysis of a structural evolution that can be inferred from comparing multiple images of an astronomical object.

sources.summary: The paper demonstrates its VLBI map-alignment software on 3C 120, using archival 8.4 and 12.1 GHz maps to derive aligned spectral-index behavior along the jet.

quotes.quote: We used two Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) maps of the AGN 3C 120 obtained in 2006 at 8.4 and 12.1 GHz, respectively. We obtained raw data from the archive of the MOJAVE program http://www.physics.purdue.edu/astro/MOJAVE/ lister2009 and converted them into images using the modelfit task of Difmap . Observation details and intensity maps are provided in Table tab:sources and Figure fig:illustration , respectively.

After correcting for the relative offset, we computed the spectral index map (cf. Section sec:indexmap ). We adopted an intensity limit of 0.9 of the peak value of the 8-GHz map and an upper limit on the spectral index error of 0.5. Spectral index maps before and after image alignment are shown in Figure fig:result2_spix ; the impact of the offset between the input maps is evident. Figure fig:result3_err_spixevol provides the spectral index error map (top panel) and the evolution of the spectral index along a one-dimensional cut following the jet (center and bottom panels).

sources.summary: The paper monitors 3C 120 across gamma-ray, submillimeter, and VLBA bands, linking MeV/GeV activity to core brightening, knot ejection, and sub-parsec jet dissipation.

quotes.quote: The Fanaroff-Riley Class I radio galaxy Walker87 \ ( z=0.033 ) is optically classified as a broad-line radio galaxy (BLRG). Its broadband spectrum is dominated by the non-thermal jet emission along with a thermal disk component, hence this BLRG is an ideal source to study the jet-disk connection. Indeed, Marscher02 observed X-ray dips in \ followed by radio knot ejections through Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) monitoring.

After the MeV/GeV detection from \ in MJD 56240--56300, 43 GHz VLBA monitoring revealed a brightening of the radio core, followed by the ejection of a superluminal knot. Since we observed the ray and VLBA phenomena in temporal proximity to each other, it is naturally assumed that they are physically connected. This assumption was further supported by the subsequent observation that the 43 GHz core brightened again after a ray flare occurred around MJD 56560.

We can then infer that the MeV/GeV emission took place inside an unresolved 43 GHz core of \ and that the jet dissipation occurred at sub-parsec distances from the central black hole, if we take the distance of the 43 GHz core from the central black hole as 0.5 pc, as previously estimated from the time lag between X-ray dips and knot ejections Marscher02,Chatterjee09 .

sources.summary: The paper monitors 3C 120, connecting MeV/GeV detections with 43 GHz core brightening, knot ejection, and sub-pc synchrotron-self-Compton jet emission.

quotes.quote: We present six-year multi-wavelength monitoring result for broad-line radio galaxy 3C 120. The source was sporadically detected by \ and after the MeV/GeV ray detection the 43 GHz radio core brightened and a knot ejected from an unresolved core, implying that the radio-gamma phenomena are physically connected. We show that the ray emission region is located at sub-pc distance from the central black hole, and MeV/GeV ray emission mechanism is inverse-Compton scattering of synchrotron photons. We also discuss future perspective revealed by next-generation X-ray satellite Astro-H .

fancy , known as broad-line radio galaxy (BLRG) at z=0.033 , is classified as Fanaroff-Riley Class I (FR I) radio galaxy based on the radio morphology. Since the viewing angle of the jet is not as small as blazars (whose jets are directed toward the Earth), both thermal disk and non-thermal jet components are present in the broad band spectrum. In this regard, \ and also 3C 111 are ideal objects to investigate the disk-jet connection.

sources.summary: The paper monitors the Seyfert galaxy 3C 120 in B and V, finding a 1.4 mag AGN brightening, stable optical color, and FVG host-galaxy estimates consistent across epochs.

quotes.quote: Using data from 2009 -- 2010, performed B , V band monitoring of the Seyfert 1 Galaxy 3C 120 \ including dense daily observations over a period of five months. Here we report new B , V band results from a six-month monitoring project in 2014 and 2015. The total brightening (by about 1.4 mag) and the dense time-sampling of observations allow us to check whether or not brightness and time-dependent color variations are present.

Our results show a highly linear evolution of B versus V fluxes for 120 \ on all timescales -- from days to several years. Additionally, the color is highly constant, despite the AGN undergoing a strong increase of 1.4 mag in flux over a duration of five years.

The flux variation gradients of both epochs yield consistent estimates of the host galaxy contribution to our 7 5 aperture. These results confirm that the optical flux variation gradient method works well for Seyfert galaxies

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 120 as a bright high-excitation radio galaxy whose line luminosity agrees with prior estimates and whose [O IV] emission may be boosted by jet-ISM interaction.

quotes.quote: Additional examples are NGC 1052 ( 3.7 vs. 3.4 from 2019MNRAS.485.5377F ), Centaurus A ( 2.7 vs. 2.4 from 2011A&A...531A..70B ), NGC 1068 ( 0.5 vs. 0.2 from 2020MNRAS.492.3872Z ), and 3C 120 ( 0.4 vs. 0.5 from 2016MNRAS.458.2454L,2018ApJ...856..120R ).

Regarding the primary ionising spectrum, we computed two main sets of simulations intended to reproduce the most extreme cases in the LED: i) low-excitation nuclei with bright [ Ne ii ] _ 12.8 emission and faint [ O iv ] _ 25.9 ; ii) the high-excitation nuclei where [ O iv ] _ 25.9 can be the most prominent line in the mid-IR spectrum (e.g. NGC 1068 and 3C 120 in 2010ApJ...709.1257T ).

Radio galaxies in the LED The majority of radio-loud Seyfert nuclei represented by dark-filled symbols in Fig. fig_led correspond to bright high-excitation radio galaxies, such as 3C 120, 3C 273, and Cygnus A. These objects typically show enormous radio lobe structures extending several to hundreds of kiloparsecs beyond the boundaries of the host galaxy 1974MNRAS.167P..31F . The cores of these galaxies are, however, not as loud as those of low-luminosity AGN when their radio continuum and X-ray emission are compared. We note that in some of these nuclei the [ O iv ] _ 25.9 emission might be boosted due to the jet-ISM interaction, as seems to be the case of 3C 120 2004ApJ...615..156S,2017ApJ...838...16T . However, the current accretion state in the core of these galaxies might be completely decoupled from the past activity that created the enormous radio lobe structures.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 120 as a nearby source with a detected jet-shape break, fits it with maximum Lorentz factors 10 and 20, and confirms its cores lie in an accelerating parabolic region.

quotes.quote: Our sample of 11 sources include 3C 111, 3C 120 and BL Lac with the earlier detected jet boundary shape break Kovalev20_r1 . This detection was possible due to the sources proximity (redshift <0.07 ), which provided the necessary linear resolution. Our aim is to check the consistency of the analytical and semi-analytical modelling for these sources, data on the value of a Lorentz factor and the additional data on the parabolic jet shape.

We are able to fit the data for 3C 111 and 3C 120 with _ max =10, 20 . The models with higher _ max start further downstream, so applying these models would require extrapolating the analytical curves upstream. However, we observe, that fitting the particular point in z -- plane provides smaller spin for larger _ max (unlike fitting the jet shape break in NKP20_r2 ), so taking smaller _ max we model larger BH spin values.
