The 3C 120 black hole spin values for M2, _ max =10 are a_*=0.052^ +0.432 _ 0.037 . For _ max =20 a_*=0.021^ +0.019 _ 0.008 . Here we confirmed that the observed cores at 8 and 15 GHz are in EA parabolic region for these sources.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 120 to test jet composition, finding its radio-to-IR synchrotron core and gamma-ray constraints favor a pair-dominated jet and magnetic flux near the black-hole maximum.

quotes.quote: We calculated the electron-positron pair production rate at the base of the jet of 3C 120 by collisions of photons from the hot accretion flow using the measurement of its average soft gamma-ray spectrum by Compton Gamma Ray Observatory. We found that this rate approximately equals the flow rate of the leptons emitting the observed synchrotron radio-to-IR spectrum of the jet core, calculated using the extended jet model following Blandford K o nigl. This coincidence shows the jet composition is likely to be pair-dominated. We then calculated the jet power in the bulk motion of ions, and found it greatly exceeds that achievable by the magnetically arrested disk scenario for the maximum black hole spin unless the jet contains mostly pairs. Next, we found that the magnetic flux through the synchrotron-emitting jet equals the maximum poloidal flux that can thread the black hole.

We have found the broad-line radio galaxy 3C 120 to be well suited for such calculations. This is a well-studied, strong and prominent jet source Marscher02_3C120 , but at the same time its X-ray emission is dominated by its accretion flow, as evidenced by the detection of strong reflection features ZG01,Lohfink13 .

sources.summary: 3C 120 is treated as a variable BLRG with FR I/BL Lac-like traits and superluminal VLBI motion; continuum modeling changes the inferred iron-line strength and favors a broken power law.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 3C 120 is a BLRG exhibiting characteristics intermediate between those of FR I galaxies and BL Lacs (Urry Padovani 1995). It is highly variable in all energy bands, and shows superluminal motion in the VLBI core (Muxlow Wilkinson 1991; Walker, Benson Unwin 1988; Walker, Walker Benson 1988).

Similarly to the case of 3C 382, we find the fitted line parameters depend very strongly on the way the continuum is modeled. If we model the continuum as a single power law, the line is very strong, 1 keV, and broad, 2 keV (as obtained before by Reynolds 1997). On the other hand, the line strength becomes moderate, 100 eV, and relatively narrow, when the power-law continuum is allowed to break.
The spectrum corresponding to the latter model is plotted in Figure 8a. Allowing a break in the power law also improves the fit at the significance of 99.6 per cent.

sources.summary: The paper monitors 3C 120 across X-ray, optical, radio, and VLBA bands, linking X-ray dips to superluminal knot ejections and modeling correlated disk-corona emission.

quotes.quote: 68527 18 Key Laboratory for Research in Galaxies and Cosmology, Shanghai Astronomical Observatory, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 80 Nandan Rd., Shanghai, 200030 China 19 Present Address: Department of Physics and Astronomy, Iowa State University, Ames, IA, 50011-3160 20 Department of Astronomy, National University of Uzbekistan, Tashkent 700095, Uzbekistan We present the results of extensive multi-frequency monitoring of the radio galaxy 3C 120 between 2002 and 2007 at X-ray (2-10 keV), optical (R and V band), and radio (14.5 and 37 GHz) wave bands, as well as imaging with the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) at 43 GHz. Over the 5 yr of observation, significant dips in the X-ray light curve are followed by ejections of bright superluminal knots in the VLBA images. Consistent with this, the X-ray flux and 37 GHz flux are anti-correlated with X-ray leading the radio variations.

The X-ray power spectral density of 3C 120 shows a break, with steeper slope at shorter timescale and the break timescale is commensurate with the mass of the central black hole based on observations of Seyfert galaxies and black hole X-ray binaries. The X-ray and optical variations are strongly correlated in 3C 120, which implies that the optical emission in this object arises from the same general region as the X-rays, i.e., in the accretion disk-corona system. We numerically model multi-wavelength light curves of 3C 120 from such a system with the optical-UV emission produced in the disk and the X-rays generated by scattering of thermal photons by hot electrons in the corona.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 120 as an observed radio-galaxy jet example whose C80 knot polarization can be modeled by shocks but needs gradual shock collimation downstream.

quotes.quote: An interesting recent example is the C80 knot in the jet of radio galaxy 3C 120, reported by . It has a bow-like shape with polarization vectors aligned perpendicularly to its outline. It was well reproduced by a conical shock model with compressed chaotic magnetic field distribution. However, this model can hardly explain the polarization vectors perpendicular to the jet axis observed immediately downstream of the C80 knot, even when a poloidal field component is introduced. Additional perpendicular shock waves can explain a parallel polarization measured even farther downstream (knot C99), but are inconsistent with a perpendicular polarization.
We propose a simple explanation of the perpendicular polarization vectors downstream of the C80 knot by a gradual collimation of the conical shock into a roughly cylindrical structure.

sources.summary: The paper finds that broad H-line variations in 3C 120 lead 15 GHz radio changes by about 0.34 yr, placing the radio outburst region roughly five light-years from the central engine.

quotes.quote: In the paper, we investigate correlation between broad-line and radio variations for broad-line radio galaxy 3C 120. By the z-transformed discrete correlation function method and the model-independent flux randomization/random subset selection (FR/RSS) Monte Carlo method, we find that the broad H line variations lead the 15 GHz variations. The FR/RSS method shows that the H line variations lead the radio variations by a factor of _ ob =0.34 0.01 yr.

This time lag can be used to locate the position of emitting region of radio outbursts in jet, on the order of 5 light-years, from the central engine. This distance is much larger than the size of broad-line region. The large separation of the radio outburst emitting region from the broad-line region will observably influence the gamma-ray emission in 3C 120.

sources.summary: The paper links prolonged gamma-ray activity in radio galaxy 3C 120 to mm-VLBI core flares and superluminal jet-component ejections, placing the gamma-ray production near the core.

quotes.quote: We present the analysis of the radio jet evolution of the radio galaxy 3C 120 during a period of prolonged ray activity detected by the Fermi satellite between December 2012 and October 2014. We find a clear connection between the ray and radio emission, such that every period of ray activity is accompanied by the flaring of the mm-VLBI core and subsequent ejection of a new superluminal component. However, not all ejections of components are associated with ray events detectable by Fermi .

The radio galaxy 3C 120 ( z =0.033) presents an FRI morphology, but it also has a blazar-like radio jet, showing multiple superluminal components at parsec scales Gomez:2000ys, Gomez:2001fk, Gomez:2011vn , as well as at distances as large as 150 pc in projection from the core Walker:2001uq . This radio galaxy reveals also X-ray properties similar to Seyfert galaxies, with the X-ray spectral slope increasing with intensity Maraschi:1991kx , and a prominent iron emission line at a photon energy of 6.4 KeV. This implies that most of the X-ray emission comes from, or near the accretion disk, rather than in the jet.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 120 as a nearby broad-line radio galaxy whose gamma-ray flares, rapid variability, averaged emission, and flare SED are modeled with a bent fast jet spine.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed Fermi /LAT data analysis for the broad-line radio galaxy 3C 120. This source has recently entered into a state of increased ray activity which manifested itself in two major flares detected by Fermi /LAT in September 2014 and April 2015 with no significant flux changes reported in other wavelengths. We analyse available data focusing our attention on aforementioned outbursts. We find very fast variability timescale during flares (of the order of hours) together with a significant ray flux increase. We show that the 6.8  years averaged ray emission of 3C 120 is likely a sum of the external radiation Compton and the synchrotron self-Compton radiative components.

We show that with the addition of the fast spine occasionally bent towards the observer we are able to explain observed spectral energy distribution of 3C 120 during flares with the Compton up-scattered broad-line region and dusty torus photons as main rays emission mechanism.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes 3C120 VLBA Faraday-rotation data and finds several tentative counter-clockwise transverse RM gradients across the jet on 30-100 pc scales.

quotes.quote: 3C120 .---In contrast to the other objects considered in this section, the Faraday-rotation map of Coughlan et al. (2010) is based on VLBA data at four frequencies between 1.4 and 1.7 GHz, and thus probes slightly smaller angular scales. Transverse RM gradients are present at several locations on scales of approximately 30--100 pc from the core, all oriented CCW. The top panel of Fig. 4 presents our 1.358 GHz intensity and RM images of 3C120 (beam size 19.6  mas 7.6  mas in position angle 27^ ); these essentially reproduce the images of Coughlan et al. (2010).

Our analysis of the entire RM distribution showed the presence of monotonic RM gradients across the jet in the regions surrounded by the gray boxes. Thus, these transverse RM gradients cannot yet be considered firm. However, 3C120 remains on the list of sources displaying tentative transverse RM gradients; deeper VLBA observations with a wider frequency range could help determine whether these gradients are statistically significant.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 120 as a radio galaxy with pc-to-kpc jets, Fermi variability, flares, and broadband emission from synchrotron and inverse-Compton processes.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C 120, at a distance of 144.9 Mpc, is an active and powerful emitter in all the observed wavebands. In the radio band, its characteristics are closer to the Fanaroff-Riley class I radio sources fanaroff74 with a powerful one-sided radio jet from sub- pc to 100 kpc scales walker . The kpc-scale jet of 3C 120 has a complex structure with several knots, k4, k7, s2, s3, and k25 (see Fig. 2 of hariss04 , where on the 1.6 GHz radio contours the section of the jet with knot labels is shown), detected in the radio, optical, and X-ray bands HARIS99,hariss04 .

The photons from a circular Region of Interest (ROI) centered on VLBI radio positions of 3C 120 (RA,dec) = (68.296, 5.354) are used in the analysis. During the flaring periods, the flux increased nearly by an order of magnitude and the photon index hardened. Taking into account the results of the previous studies of other Fermi LAT-observed radio galaxies abdom87,abdo2010a,sahak3c120,tanaka3c120 , the multiwavelength emission of 3C 120 is modeled using the synchrotron/Synchrotron Self-Compton (SSC) maraschi,bloom,ghisellini and/or External Inverse-Compton (EIC) sikora models.

sources.summary: The paper models the host with a tiny bulge and disturbed spiral disk, noting disagreement with other bulge-disk interpretations caused by disk-like disturbance.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 (Fig. 11.3) --- \ \ The host galaxy is fit with a tiny bulge ( R_e 0 1) and a disturbed disk with spiral arms. However, we also find that a fit with a single S ersic component can be acceptable. Using ground-based K- band data, Inskip et al. (2010) also fit the host with two components, resulting in B/T=0.88 .

However, they claim that the inner component ( R_e 0.62 kpc) and the larger component ( R_e 8.62 kpc) can be regarded as as a disk and a bulge, respectively, which is the opposite of our interpretation ( B/T=0.19 ). By contrast, Bennert et al. (2010) concluded that the host is an elliptical galaxy (but see Bentz et al. 2009). The reason for this controversy is due to presence of the disturbed disk-like feature.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 3C 120 was mostly below threshold until late 2014, then brightened dramatically, including a 19-minute gamma-ray flux peak in 2015.

quotes.quote: 3C 120: The light curve with 90-day binning (see the insert in Fig. var_all [ b ] ) shows that up to MJD 56900 the source flux is below the sensitivity threshold and is mostly undetectable (only upper limits). Afterwards, the flux increased being detected by almost all the time. More dramatic changes in shorter time scales can be seen on the adaptively binned light curve shown in Fig. var_all (b) (red data).

The data collected from MJD 54682 (4 August 2008) up to MJD 56919.31(19 September 2014) are necessary to reach the 20 uncertainty in the first bin, while it took shorter time afterwards. The most dramatic increase in the flux was observed on April 24, 2015 when within 19.0 min the flux reached (7.46 1.56) 10^ 6 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 with =2.29 0.21 and 11.2 detection significance. Then the flux slowly decreased and the data collected within 10 to 35 days were enough to reach 20 uncertainty.

sources.summary: The paper applies Gaussian-process timing to 3C 120, a broad-line radio galaxy with a jet and disc reflection, measuring low-frequency X-ray reverberation.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 is a nearby ( z=0.033 ), bright, broad line radio galaxy (BLRG) that exhibits a one-sided super-luminal jet. Unlike many radio galaxies, however, 3C 120 shows the reflection of an X-ray continuum from the inner regions of the accretion disc, with the detection of a relativistically broadened iron K line in spectra observed by Suzaku kataoka+2007,cowperthwaite+2012 . 3C 120 exhibits a prominent jet cycle marscher+2002 and conducting detailed multi-epoch X-ray studies with Suzaku and XMM-Newton , lohfink_3c120 were able to observe evidence of the interplay between the accretion disc and the jet. They find that the X-ray spectrum is well described by a composite model consisting of jet emission, the X-ray continuum and reflection from the accretion disc, and that the ejection of knots within the jet coincides with the inner disc becoming truncated (and potentially being ejected) before refilling.

Gaussian processes were therefore employed to sample time lags in the low frequency Fourier components across multiple XMM-Newton observations of 3C 120. Observations 3C 120 has been observed on three occasions by XMM-Newton , for 12 \ in 2002, for 133 \ in 2003 (one segment) and for a total of 159 \ in 2013.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 120 as a broad-line radio galaxy whose K4 and S2 jet regions have hard X-ray spectra and CTAO-detectable IC/CMB predictions.

quotes.quote: The second source predicted as a VHE candidate for CTAO under IC/CMB model is 3C 120 located at z=0.033 which is a broad line radio galaxy initially classified as Seyfert I 2015A&A...574A..88S . The radio morphology of the source is similar to FR I class with radio structures extending up to 100 kpc and superluminal components identified with VLBI studies 2001ApJ...556..756W,1987ApJ...316..546W . The X-ray jet consists of four bright knots K4, K7, K25 and K80 and two sections of the jet S 2 and S 3 bright in X-rays 1999ApJ...518..213H . The X-ray spectrum for the knot K 7 and the jet component S 3 is steep supporting a synchrotron origin while K 4 and S 2 suggests IC/CMB origin due to hard X-ray spectrum.

Hence, in this work we have considered only the knot K 4 and the jet component S 2, and modelled their X-ray emission through IC/CMB process. Extrapolating this emission model to VHE suggests, the gamma-ray flux from these regions fall within the 50-hour sensitivity of CTAO.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 120 as a broad-line radio galaxy with a blazar-like jet, rapid GeV variability, superluminal motion, and moderate Doppler boosting.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 (4FGL J0433.0+0522) is a broad-line radio galaxy (BLRG) with a blazar-like jet, which has been discussed by many authors due to its rapid variability in the Giga-electron volt (GeV) band. The rapid variability suggests that the ray emission of 3C 120 originates from a very compact region cas15,rul20 . cas15 analyzed the jet properties of 3C 120 based on radio and ray data from 2012 to 2014, and found that the apparent superluminal motion is as high as 6.2 .

3C 120 shows a blazar-like jet with a Doppler factor of 6.2 cas15 . hov09 compared the observed radio brightness temperature and radio equipartition brightness temperature to obtain the Doppler factor ( _ var =5.9 ) of 3C 120. Our Doppler factor estimation for 3C 120, _ =2.32 , is derived from the average flux density during the Fermi LAT observations.

sources.summary: The paper includes 3C 120 in its Seyfert sample, noting its radio-loud nature, Seyfert-like Fe K/reflection features, and weaker, narrower Fe K line results after calibration updates.

quotes.quote: We include 3C 120, as it is present in the sample of N97. Although it is radio-loud, its Fe K and reflection spectral features are similar to those of radio-quiet Seyferts (Zdziarski Grandi 2001).

An illustrative example is that of 3C 120. N97 obtained = 0.74_ 0.27 ^ +0.34 keV and = 0.33_ 0.13 ^ +0.20 keV (their error ranges are for ^2=4.7 ). Then, Wo zniak et al. \ (1998) obtained =0.28^ +0.96 _ 0.16 keV (hereafter the errors are for 90 per cent confidence, i.e., ^2=2.7 ) and 0.1 keV, i.e., a line 3 times weaker and narrower, using the same data and model as N97, but with the calibration of 1997. The weakness of the line has then been confirmed by broad-band observations by and RXTE , which yielded = 0.40_ 0.18 ^ +0.87 keV, 0.09_ 0.09 ^ +0.32 keV, and = 0.08_ 0.03 ^ +0.07 keV, 0.09 0.02 keV, respectively (Zdziarski Grandi 2001).

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 120 as a nearby radio galaxy whose jet shows superluminal motion, rich VLBI polarization structure, a new superluminal component, and trailing-shock behavior.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C 120 was one of the first four sources in which superluminal motion was detected on the scale of parsecs ( ) to tens of parsecs ( ). High resolution polarimetric VLBI observations ( , ) have revealed a richer, more rapidly changing structure in total and linearly polarized intensity than that found in other relatively nearby compact extragalactic jets ( , , ). Thanks to its proximity ( z =0.033), millimeter VLBI observations allow to probe the inner jet structure of 3C 120 with very high linear resolution, 0.1 h_ 65 ^ 1 pc at 43 GHz.

The images show the appearance of a new strong superluminal component, labeled ``O'' in Fig. 3c120 , coincident with a major outburst in the light curve. The passage of this new superluminal component triggered the appearance of a stationary feature (``M'' in Fig. 3c120 ) that presents also enhanced linearly polarized emission. This behavior is in agreement with the numerical simulations of ``trailing shocks'' by , which explain the appearance of this stationary feature as a consequence of the jet instabilities produced by the passage of the strong leading shock, which would be associated with component ``O''.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 120 as a well-studied radio galaxy and finds a 4.3-year wavelet timescale at 22 and 37 GHz, consistent with earlier DCF analysis.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 ( 0430+052 ) : This radio galaxy has been monitored in Mets ahovi for over 20 years at 22 and 37 GHz and for 15 years at 90 GHz. It is a well-studied object at all wavelengths and has been a target of many multiwavelength campaigns. Its radio jet has been studied and modelled using VLBI observations by several authors e.g. walker01, gomez01, hardee05 . Our wavelet analyses reveal a timescale of 4.3 years at both 22 and 37 GHz. At 22 GHz the timescale has been present for 85 of the total observing period lasting for 4.4 cycles.

sources.summary: The paper links 3C 120 optical continuum variability to radio-jet activity, arguing that non-thermal jet emission contributes substantially to ionizing the broad-line region.

quotes.quote: We report a relation between radio emission in the inner jet of the Seyfert galaxy 3C 120 and optical continuum emission in this galaxy. Combining the optical variability data with multi-epoch high-resolution very long baseline interferometry observations reveals that an optical flare rises when a superluminal component emerges into the jet and its maxima is related to the passage of such component through the location a stationary feature at a distance of 1.3 parsecs from the jet origin. This indicates that a significant fraction of the optical continuum produced in 3C 120 is non-thermal and it can ionize material in a sub-relativistic wind or outflow.
The 15 GHz radio structure of 3C 120 shown in Figure Fig:3c120_jet is characterized by a one-sided jet directed at a P.A. of about 120^ .
We therefore conclude that S1 is indeed likely to be a stationary formation inside the relativistic flow in 3C 120.

sources.summary: Suzaku spectra of 3C 120 are included among BLRGs with significant >7 keV absorption lines, interpreted as blue-shifted Fe ultra-fast outflows from accretion disk winds.

quotes.quote: In Tombesi et al. (2010b) we performed a systematic 4--10 keV spectral analysis of long Suzaku observations of five BLRGs, i.e. 3C 111, 3C 120, 3C 390.3, 3C 382 and 3C 445. We detected, for the first time at X-rays in radio-loud AGNs, several absorption lines at energies greater than 7 keV in the first three sources. The lines are detected with high significance ( > 99 ) according to both the F-test and extensive Monte Carlo simulations (see Fig. 1). Their likely interpretation as blue-shifted Fe XXV--XXVI K-shell resonances implies an origin from highly ionized gas outflowing with mildly relativistic velocities, in the range v 0.04--0.15c. A fit with specific photo-ionization models gives ionization parameters in the range log 4--5.6 erg s ^ 1 cm and column densities of N_H 10^ 22 - 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 .

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C120 as the broad-line radio-galaxy comparison for Fairall 9, noting its lower black-hole mass, similar Eddington ratio, and jet-producing disk disruptions.

quotes.quote: In fact, it is instructive to compare directly Fairall 9 with the BLRG 3C120. 3C120 has a slightly lower-mass black hole, 6 10^7 M_ for 3C120 compared to 3 10^8 M_ for Fairall 9 peterson:04a . However, they possess a very similar Eddington ratio, L _ F9 0.15 compared to L _ 3C120 0.11 , derived using the bolometric luminosity for 3C120 from . Thus, their accretion disks might be expected to be in rather similar regimes of behavior. It is possible that the X-ray dips seen in Fairall 9 have the same physical origin as the ejection-related X-ray dips seen in 3C120 but, for some reason, these disk disruption events lead to the creation of powerful jet outflows in 3C120 but not in Fairall 9 indeed, Fairall 9 has yet to be detected in the radio band, with an upper limit of <36 mJy at 1.4 GHz, see whittle:92a .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 120 as a soft gamma-ray FRI source with a powerful accretion disk, variable radio jet, broad optical lines, and UV disk signatures.

quotes.quote: 3C 120: a FRI with a powerful accretion disk 3C 120 (z=0.033; ) is the softest -ray source of the MAGN sample ( _ 2.7). Its -ray emission was detected by Fermi -LAT in the 0.1--100 GeV energy band with a significance of 5.6 . It is classified as FRI with a prominent radio jet lying at an angle of 20 ^ to our line--of--sight and showing strong flux and structure variability , . Its optical spectrum is typical of Seyfert 1 galaxies with strong and broad emission lines, quite unusual for FRI radio sources. At UV wavelengths it shows a strong blue bump and strong emission lines signatures of a standard optically--thick geometrically--thin accretion disk .

sources.summary: The paper targets the radio-loud Seyfert 1 galaxy 3C 120, using HET spectra and photometric monitoring to model its stratified broad-line region and line-profile variability.

quotes.quote: We selected the radio-loud Seyfert 1 galaxy 3C120 as a target for this study. We took spectra with a high signal-to-noise ratio of 3C 120 with the 9.2m Hobby-Eberly Telescope between Sept. \ 2008 and March 2009. In parallel, we photometrically monitored the continuum flux at the Wise observatory. We analyzed the continuum and line profile variations in detail (1D and 2D reverberation mapping) and modeled the geometry of the line-emitting regions based on the line profiles. We show that the BLR in 3C 120 is stratified with respect to the distance of the line-emitting regions from the center with respect to the line widths (FWHM) of the rms profiles and with respect to the variability amplitude of the emission lines.

sources.summary: The catalog says 3C 120 is absent from FGL catalogs but reported in a 15-month study, with flaring indications, a nearby soft 3FGL source, and ambiguous association.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 is not listed in any of the FGL catalogs but its detection was reported in a 15-month study magn . There are indications that 3C 120 undergoes a series of flares with a low long-term average flux. For instance, in September 2014 a flaring source positionally consistent with 3C 120 was detected with a high significance TS> 50; ATel6529 . The closest 3FGL source, 3FGL J0432.5+0539, lies 0 35 away with an 95 error radius of 0 15 , hampering association with 3C 120 by our methods. This gamma-ray source has a soft spectrum ( =2.7 0.1 ) comparable with that ascribed to 3C 120 magn,kat11 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 120 as an FR-I radio galaxy with radio-to-X-ray monitoring, a composite jet-plus-accretion-disk fit, jet-dominated SED, soft gamma spectrum, and no TeV detection.

quotes.quote: 3C 120, another FR-I radio galaxy, was not included in the first, second or third Fermi catalogue, but using a 15-month data set Abdo2010misaligned derived a significance of 5.6 between 0.1--100 GeV for this source.

3C 120 has been monitored in the radio, UV and X-ray band Lohfink2013 . From the X-ray observations by Suzaku and XMM-Newton a 0.4--10 keV spectrum is derived, where a fit with a composite jet+accretion disk model is favoured. The broad-band SED of this source has shown that the jet dominates the radio and ray emission, and contributes only 10 in the optical, UV and X-ray bands Kataoka2011 . The ray spectrum is soft, with _ 2.7 , and the source is not detected in the TeV band, implying that the inverse Compton peak of this source is located in the X-ray to MeV band.

sources.summary: The paper presents 22, 43, and 86 GHz maps of 3C 120, noting extended jet structure, component-identification difficulty, and 22 GHz polarized structure.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 3C 120 is another famous radio galaxy in our sample. We were able to produce total intensity maps at 22, 43, and 86 GHz (Figure fig:3c120_imaps ). For this source the cross-identification of jet components is not straightforward since the 22 GHz structure is more extended than the rather compact 43 GHz structure. The extended emission features visible at 43 GHz may have propagated outward in the 86 GHz map as there is a time gap of four months between the two observations and the maximum apparent velocity of the moving features in 3C 120 is 3 mas year ^ 1 lister13 . Figure fig:3c120_polmaps shows the polarized structure of 3C 120 at 22 GHz.

sources.summary: The review presents 3C 120 as a broad-line radio galaxy with a powerful accretion disk, where gamma-ray flares are linked to sub-pc radio-core knot ejection.

quotes.quote: In order to establish where the high-energy radiation is emitted a multi-wavelength (MW) approach is necessary, as demonstrated by the successful results obtained for the broad-line radio galaxies 3C 111 and 3C 120 3C 111 is a FR II, while 3C 120 although morphologically classified as FR I, is characterised by a powerful accretion disk. Interestingly, these are the only AGN for which a disk/jet connection has been established ( , ).

A similar result was obtained by from a six-year MW monitoring of 3C 120. Also in this case the ray flare was related to the ejection of a knot from the radio core at sub-pc distance from the black hole. This finding was further confirmed by who could better constrain the site of the emitting region within 0.13 pc by exploiting mm-VLBI data.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 120 as an active Seyfert galaxy with a precessing jet, complex optical morphology, merger evidence, optical variability, and a possible nuclear black-hole binary.

quotes.quote: galaxies: active -- galaxies: individual: (3C 120) -- galaxies: jets -- radio continuum: galaxies 3C 120 (z=0.033; bal80 ), also known as II Zw 14 and PKS 0430+052, is usually classified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy, although its morphology in the optical band is not as simple as that of a typical galaxy of this class. Indeed, photometric and spectroscopic studies seem to indicate that 3C 120 either passed, or it is still passing through a merger process (e.g., sou89,hjo95 ).

At lower resolution, VLBI observations at 1.7 GHz have revealed that the jet structure of 3C 120 is extremely complex wal01 ; it presents sub-structures in scales of tenths of a parsec that move superluminally, a possible stationary component located at an angular distance of about 81 mas from the core and a jet aperture that is larger in the southern direction, specially after about 180 mas, where there is also a decrease in the apparent velocity. The luminosity of 3C 120 observed in the optical band might be the result of the superposition of thermal contribution from the accretion disc with non-thermal contribution produced, e.g., in the underlying jet and in the superluminal components.

Among the possible mechanisms that could produce jet precession, we consider that 3C 120 harbours a super-massive black hole binary system in its nuclear region and that torques induced by misalignment between the accretion disc and the orbital plane of the secondary black hole are responsible for this precession; we estimated upper and lower limits for the black holes masses and their mean separation.

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 120 as a nearby radio galaxy whose VLBA jet shows superluminal components, slower trailing features, and evidence for jet precession.

quotes.quote: One of the best candidates for such intensive monitoring programs is the radio galaxy 3C 120. This is a one of the closest known extragalactic superluminal sources ( z =0.033) and is a powerful emitter of radiation along the whole spectrum. Monthly 16 epochs of polarimetric 43 GHz VLBA observations of 3C 120 (G omez et al. 2001) reveal multiple superluminal components with velocities in the rage between 4 and 5.8 h^ 1 _ 65 . Model fitting of the u-v is shown in Fig. 1 ( left ). By the end of 1997 the source was observed to flare, followed by the ejection of a new strong superluminal component, soon resolved into several distinct features ( o1 , o2 and p in Fig. 1).

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 120 as a well-known AGN with an extended, complexly polarized jet and a transverse Faraday-rotation gradient consistent with a helical magnetic field.

quotes.quote: The well known AGN 3C 120 has an extended jet with complex polarisation structure. The polarisation map of 3C 120 at 1358 Mhz presented in Figure 2(a) has been corrected for Faraday rotation. The rotation measure distribution is shown in Figure 2(b). A tendancy for the RM to be higher along the upper edge of the jet of 3C 120 is visible; one possible origin for this transverse gradient in Faraday rotation is a helical magnetic field carried by the jet: the Faraday rotation gradient is due to a systematic change in the line-of-sight magnetic field across the jet.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 120 as one of three anomalous AGN with unusually high [CII]/PAH ratios, reliable [CII] detections, and weak PAH emission not explained by measurement error.

quotes.quote: Among all of the sources in Figures 5 and 6, only three appear anomalous. These are all AGN and have the largest ratios f( [ CII ] )/f(PAH) - sources 3C 120, Mrk 590, and NGC 3393 in Table 1 - with f( [ CII ] )/f(PAH) about a factor of ten greater than the median. All have strong and reliable [ CII ] detections with no unusual profiles or evidence of spatial mismatches or source extension. The PAH 11.3 feature is weak in all three, but independent CASSIS spectra yield fluxes consistent to within 30 , so there is no indication that the anomalous weakness of PAH is a measurement error.

sources.summary: For 3C 120, the paper jointly fits variable Suzaku spectra, finds a broad soft excess, and cannot directly confirm previously reported Fe K absorption lines.

quotes.quote: In 3C 120 we followed the analysis method of kataoka:2007 and tombesi:2010b by co-adding OBSIDs 700001020, 70001030 and 70001040, which are all of a similar spectral shape and flux level, and jointly fitted them with OBSID 700001010 which has a more prominent underlying soft-excess.
In 3C 120 and Mrk 509 the soft-excess is very broad and extends beyond that which can be fitted with a simple blackbody.

state that 3/5 of the BLRGs in their sample, namely 3C 111, 3C 120 and 3C 390.3, show evidence for highly-ionised absorption lines in their spectra; with that found in 3C 111 also being confirmed in more recent subsequent observations ( tombesi:2011b ) We are able to confirm the absorption lines detected in both 3C 111 and 3C 390.3 at a similar confidence level. However, while we are unable to directly confirm the line detection reported in 3C 120 we note that by adding two narrow ( =10 eV) Gaussians at the rest-frame energies for the and lines reported by ( 7.25 keV and 7.54 keV, respectively) we can place lower limits of EW_ He >-8 eV and EW_ Ly >-9 eV for the and absorption lines, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C120 as a radio-loud AGN where inferred disc truncation and a post-outburst change in inner disc radius could instead reflect increased coronal height or outward coronal motion.

quotes.quote: Truncation has also been inferred in some radio-loud AGN, such as 3C120 (Marscher et al 2003; Lohfink et al 2013).

Both coronal height and motion could be relevant to jetted sources, such as the low state of black hole binaries, for example GX339-4, and radio-loud AGN such as 3C120. Lohfink et al (2013) find that the inner radius of the disc in 3C120 appears to change following a radio outburst (see also Marscher et al 2002). Possible alternative interpretations are that either coronal height has increased or the coronal material has developed an outward velocity of a few tenths of the speed of light.

sources.summary: J0433+0521 is discussed as a Seyfert 1 with FR I morphology, extended radio and optical jets, and a disk-plus-bulge host including a nuclear point source.

quotes.quote: J0433+0521 This is a Seyfert 1 galaxy with a low H broad component, 1500 km s ^ 1 . It has a confirmed FR I morphology, with jets whose total extent exceeds 760 kpc . There is an optical jet in the same apparent direction as the radio jet . The host galaxy was analyzed by Inskip et al. (2010), and they found that it is better reproduced with a disk+bulge model, plus a nuclear point source that contributes 33 to the total flux.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 120 as a monitored AGN, finding constrained BLR opening angle, inclination, and minimum radius, while other posterior distributions remain too broad.

quotes.quote: In this work we apply our single-epoch model to a more extended sample of AGN that were targets of monitoring campaigns and modelling of the BLR ( grier17 , williams18 ). These AGN display a large range of black-hole masses and accretion rates.

3C 120 Some of the parameters can be constrained for this AGN and agree within the errors with the full light-curve result: we find = 3c120_opening degrees, = 3c120_inc degrees and = 3c120_rmin light-days. The remaining parameters have very broad posterior probability distributions (Fig. posterior_3c120 ), which indicates that they cannot be accurately constrained from the single-epoch spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 120 as an AGN whose X-ray dip timescales can be matched by hot inner accretion-disc collapse timescales from a hybrid-viscosity prescription.

quotes.quote: We construct simplified disc models using this viscosity prescription and estimate disc collapse timescales for 3C 120, 3C 111, and GRS 1915+105. The Shakura-Sunyaev parameter resulting from our model ranges from 0.02 to 0.08. Our inner disc collapse timescale estimates agree well with those of the observed X-ray dips.

X-ray dip timescales for 3C 120 range from 5 to 120 days. The upper panel of figure ( fig:5_120days3c120 ) depicts the combination of parameters that yield t_ infall = 5 days while the lower panel shows the parameter combinations for t_ infall = 120 days. Taken together, these plots reveal that the range of X-ray dip timescales for 3C 120 can be attained with our model for 0.2 < 1 , 1 ^ 2 30 (which corresponds to 0.04 < _ hyb < 0.07 ) and 14 R_ g R_ out 175 R_ g . In figure ( fig:146_402days3c111 ) we show the range of parameters that yield t_ infall ranging from 146 to 402 days for 3C 111. It reveals that 146 < t_ infall < 402 days can be attained for 3C 111 with 0.2 < 1 , 1 ^ 2 40 (which corresponds to 0.02 < _ hyb < 0.08 ) and 13 R_ g R_ out 38 R_ g . Figure ( fig:1_6sgrs ) is a similar plot which depicts the range of parameters that result in values of t_ infall ranging from 1 to 6 seconds for the galactic microquasar GRS 1915+105. We see that the parameter range 0.2 < 1 , 1 ^ 2 25 (which corresponds to 0.03 < _ hyb < 0.07 ) and 75 R_ g R_ out 250 R_ g is acceptable in this case. Figure ( fig:tvisc3c120 ) explores the parameter space with R_ out held constant. We find that infall timescale t_ infall decreases with increasing , while it increases with increasing ^2 . For 3C 120, we find that t_ infall 39--54 days with R_ out held constant at 90 R_ g . Similarly for 3C 111, the range for t_ infall with R_ out = 30 R_ g is 263--378 days. Sensitivity of t_ infall to model parameters The first point evident from figures ( fig:5_120days3c120 ), ( fig:146_402days3c111 ) and ( fig:1_6sgrs ) is that the observed X-ray timescales for each source studied here can be explained with a reasonable parameter range in our model. The next obvious question to examine is the parameter(s) to which our results are most sensitive. To recapitulate, the quantity r_ L /H is a measure of how ``tightly tied'' the protons are to the mean magnetic field. It is called the magnetic rigidity, and quantifies the extent to which the protons are magnetized. A low value for would indicate that the protons are strongly magnetized, while a high value (the upper limit being 1) would indicate the opposite. The quantity ^ 2 B_ ^ 2 / B_ 0 ^ 2 is the ratio of the energy density of the turbulent magnetic fluctuations to that of the ordered magnetic field, and can be regarded as a measure of the strength of the turbulence. The quantity R_ out denotes the outer radius of the hot, inner accretion disc. In order to examine the sensitivity of the result for t_ infall , we vary each parameter in turn (while holding the rest of them fixed). The results of such a sensitivity analysis for 3C 120 are shown in table ( tab:sensitivity ) and figure ( fig:sensitivity3c120 ). These results are applicable to the other two sources as well. The reference values of parameters for this study are: _ ref = 0.5 , ^ 2 _ ref =10 , R_ out_ ref = 90 R_ g . It is evident from figure ( fig:sensitivity3c120 ) that t_ infall is a monotonically decreasing function of , while it increases monotonically with ^ 2 and R_ out . In each row of table ( tab:sensitivity ), the parameter shown in red is varied while the rest are held fixed. This table shows that the values for t_ infall are least sensitive to variations in and most sensitive to variations in R_ out . Varying R_ out by 10 results in a 15 variation in t_ infall . By contrast, varying by 10 results in only a 0.1 change in t_ infall , and a change of 10 in ^2 yields a 0.5 change in t_ infall . As mentioned earlier, our fiducial model for GRS 1915+105 assumes a non-rotating black hole, while there is some evidence that it harbours a rotating black hole. In our fiducial model for GRS 1915+105, we find that increasing the black hole spin parameter a_ * from 0 to 0.98 necessitates a 12 increase in R_ out in order to yield an infall timescale t_ infall = 1 second. Conversely, if R_ out is kept fixed at 75 R_ g , increasing a_ * from 0 to 0.98 results in a 17 decrease in t_ infall . Summary and Conclusions To recapitulate, X-ray dips in systems such as AGN 3C 120 and 3C 111, and the rise time of the bursts in X-ray intensity in galactic microquasar GRS 1915+105 are thought to arise from the collapse of the inner accretion disc, which occurs over the radial infall timescale (Eq. eq:l ). X-ray dip timescales for 3C 120 are in the range 5--120 days with most dips happening over 5--75 days.

sources.summary: UGC 3087 is discussed as the radio source 3C120 with a Seyfert 1 nucleus; its optical jet is visible and distorts inner isophotal profiles.

quotes.quote: UGC 3087 : Previous studies of UGC 3087 have mainly concentrated on its nuclear activity. It is a strong radio source (3C120) and the optical spectrum suggests a Seyfert 1 nucleus (Tadhunter 1993). It has a faint optical jet in the same apparent direction as the radio jet. The optical jet is clearly visible in our images and distorts isophotal profiles in the inner region.

sources.summary: Suzaku observed 3C 120 as a broad-line radio galaxy whose spectrum contains absorbed power-law emission, reflection, and both narrow and highly broadened iron-line components.

quotes.quote: The broad line radio galaxy 3C 120 (at z=0.033 ) was observed 4 times by \ during 2006 in February and March, with a total net exposure of 145 ks with XIS. The spectrum can be well modeled with an absorbed power-law of =1.84 0.02 , a reflection component at high energies consistent with R=0.5 0.2 and both narrow and broad components of the iron line. The narrow line core is resolved, with a velocity width of 8200 km s ^ 1 ( =76 11 eV), which is broader than the typical uncertainties in the XIS response function width at 6 keV. This is consistent with the line width in the previous \ observation (Ballantyne et al. 2004, Ogle et al. 2005).

sources.summary: The paper finds J0433+0521's 327 MHz VLBA morphology consistent with 2.3 GHz core-jet images, but traces an extended jet spanning 300 mas.

quotes.quote: J0433+0521 (3C120) The VLBA images at 327 MHz presented here are consistent with the images at 2.3 GHz taken by , also using the VLBA. The results at 2.3 GHz presents the core and jet of 3C120 with 3 jet components, all located within approximately 100 mas of the core. The results presented here show an extended jet that spans 300 mas.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C120 photometric reverberation data, favoring a nearly face-on disk-like broad-line region and implying a higher black-hole mass than estimates using an average scaling factor.

quotes.quote: We used well-sampled photometric reverberation mapping light curves with sharp variation features in both the continuum and the H light curves to determine the geometry type of the H BLR for 3C120. Modelling of the light curve, under the assumption that the BLR is essentially virialised, argues against a spherical geometry and favours a nearly face-on disk-like geometry with an inclination i = 10^ 4^ and an extension from 22 to 28 light days. The low inclination may lead to a larger black hole mass M_ BH than that derived when using the average geometry scaling factor f=5.5 .

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 120's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 is a flat-spectrum FR I radio source coinciding with the lenticular galaxy Mrk 1506 at a redshift of z= 0.033 ( D 150 Mpc). It hosts a well-studied radio-loud Sy 1.5 AGN veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 , which also belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. It is strongly variable at most wavelengths and possesses a prominent one-sided and bended jet extending from 1 pc to 100 kpc scales (PA 95 ; walker_radio_1987 ).

sources.summary: 3C 120 is grouped with Seyfert-like HERGs showing clear Fe-K lines, following the X-ray/[O III] relation and favoring disk/corona-dominated X-rays.

quotes.quote: Among our sample of targets, four HERGs including Cen A, NGC 1275, 3C 120, and 3C 111 reveal clear Fe-K lines, while the objects classified as LERGs --- namely M 87, , , and NGC 6251 --- do not.

In particular, the three HERGs in the sample (NGC 1275, 3C 111, and 3C 120) seem to be located in the same region as Seyferts, thus obeying the correlation, while the LERGs seem to be located significantly off the track.

This finding can be considered as further support for the scenario for the disk/corona emission dominating the X-ray spectra in ``Seyfert-like'' sources NGC 1275, 3C 111, and 3C 120, and the jet emission dominating the X-ray output of the outliers like .

sources.summary: The paper uses broad-line and radio variability in 3C 120 to place its radio and likely gamma-ray emitting jet region at parsec scales from the central engine.

quotes.quote: For 3C 120, positive lags of about 0.3 yr are found between the 15 GHz emission and the H , H and He II 4686 lines, including broad-line data in a newly published paper, indicating the line variations lead the 15 GHz ones. Each of the broad-line light curves corresponds to a radio outburst. R_ jet = 1.1--1.5 parsec (pc) are obtained for 3C 120.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 120 as a Seyfert 1 in an edge-on S0 galaxy with a 60 kpc H filament, jet-cloud interaction, and possible collision-driven gas inflow.

quotes.quote: 3C 120 is a Seyfert 1 type hosted by an edge-on S0 galaxy. It reveals a very extended H filament of 60 kpc Heckman1979 , which might be an indication of gas inflow. Its jets are known to be interacting with surrounding clouds, which has been suggested to be the result of the collision between galaxies Gomez2006 .

sources.summary: The paper reports no significant 15 GHz core circular polarization for 3C 120, while noting earlier limits and intermittent historical detections with a positive-sign preference.

quotes.quote: 0430 + 052 (3C 120): We find a limit of |m_c| < 0.29 in the core at 15 GHz in February of 2003. HW99 also found limits between 0.2 and 0.3 in the core at 15 GHz throughout 1996. Both signs of circular polarization (with a clear preference for positive CP) were detected intermittently in the historical integrated measurements (WdP83 and K84) at 8.9 GHz and below.

gmw_00000054.name: 3C 135
gmw_00000054.overview: 3C 135 is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770] classified as an FR II radio source [arXiv:2010.11195, arXiv:1310.2770] with a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770]. Its emission-line region is elongated along the radio jet axis [arXiv:0710.3105] and features a conical structure of knots extending up to 77 kpc [arXiv:2010.11195].

sources.summary: 3C 135 is an FR II/HEG with conical emission-line knots reaching 77 kpc, central rapid rotation, weaker outer velocity gradients, and declining line ratio.

quotes.quote: 3C 135: FR II/HEG, z=0.125, 1 = 2.26 kpc (see Fig. 3c135 ). An irregular series of emission line knots form a conical structure, mainly toward the SW, reaching a distance of 77 kpc and tracing the edge of the western radio lobe. The ELR shows rapid rotation in the central regions, while the velocity gradients sharply decreases in the outer diffuse structures. The / \ ratio shows a general decreasing trend toward larger radii.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C135 as having an emission-line region closely aligned with the overall radio source, elongated along the jet axis, and including disconnected emission toward the northwest hotspot.

quotes.quote: 3C135 (Figs 3C135-montage 3C135-overlay ) features an emission line region which is closely aligned with the overall radio source. The radio source is very large compared to the emission line region. The emission line region is elongated along the radio jet axis and features a disconnected region of emission towards the Northwest hotspot.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 135's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 135 is a FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy LEDA 16952 at a redshift of z= 0.1274 ( D 620 Mpc). It contains a radio-loud Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_1996 with polarized broad emission lines cohen_polarimetry_1999 . 3C 135 features the classical FR II supergalactic-scale biconical radio lobes in the east-west directions (PA 75 ; e.g., leahy_study_1997 ).

gmw_00000055.name: 3C 227
gmw_00000055.overview: 3C 227 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1003.2467, arXiv:2010.11195] containing a bright active nucleus [arXiv:1003.2467] and a broad-line [arXiv:1003.2467, arXiv:0810.3764] Seyfert 1.5 core [arXiv:1310.2770]. Its extended emission line region spans up to 50 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2010.11195] and exhibits an overall X-shaped morphology [arXiv:2010.11195].

sources.summary: The paper describes Chandra and radio/infrared observations of 3C 227, finding X-ray detections from both hotspot regions, a clear X-ray secondary hotspot, and radio/X-ray peak offsets.

quotes.quote: Optical counterparts of 3C 227's hotspots have recently been discovered using ground-based observations bmpv03,mpb03 . The two components of the double W hotspot and the more compact E hotspot all emit in the near-infrared. In the X-ray, similarly, emission is detected from both hotspot regions, as shown in Figs 227-hswl and 227-hsel . In the W hotspot X-ray emission is clearly detected from the three compact components P1/2, P3 and P4, while there is weak emission not just from the compact E component F1 but also from the region around it.

Thus 3C 227 is an example of a system where the secondary hotspot clearly is an X-ray source. What is most striking in the W hotspot region is the offsets between the peak of the radio and the peak of the X-ray in the X-ray detected components. At this resolution the brightest hotspot is resolved into two components (P1 and P2), and both of these are associated with some X-ray emission, but the brightest X-ray emission comes from a resolved structure which is comparable in size to P2 (with a long axis of 3'' or 5 kpc) and the X-ray centroid of this structure is displaced 0 8 0 1 (1.3 kpc) in the direction of the nucleus from the peak of P1.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 227 as a broad-line FR II radio galaxy with a visible host and bright active nucleus, whose extinction-corrected brightness may place it among quasars.

quotes.quote: 3C 227 is a broad line FR II radio galaxy at z=0.086 . We can clearly see the host galaxy ( M_H=-22.66 ) and the bright active nucleus on this NIR image. Both the optical and UV images show a very bright unresolved nucleus martel+99,allen+02 .

The strength of the nucleus destroys any information on the color-map. However, cohen+99 found a strong Balmer decrement, and a likely V band extinction of A_V 1.7 mag. using Keck spectropolarimetry. They and prieto+93 conclude that the intrinsic brightness of the object is likely to put it in the quasar class.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 227 as a broad-line galaxy whose lobes lack obvious jets but contain complex hotspots, including western knots and an eastern primary-secondary-like structure.

quotes.quote: 3C 227 is a Broad-Line Galaxy (Simpson et al. 1996) at redshift z=0.0861 . Black et al. (1992) noted that there is no obvious jet detection in either lobe. The hotspots in both lobes have a complex morphology. The western one consists of two distinct knots (Fig. 4), the eastern one of those is again composed of two knots which remind of a primary -- secondary hotspot scenario, similar to the situation in 3C 105S.

sources.summary: 3C 227 is an FR II/BLO whose ELR fills the MUSE field, reaches 50 kpc, shows ordered rotation, and has an overall X-shaped morphology.

quotes.quote: 3C 227: FR II/BLO, z=0.086, 1 = 1.62 kpc (see Fig. 3c227 ). The ELR in this source covers the whole MUSE field of view, reaching distances of 50 kpc. It is dominated by a bright linear structure, oriented at PA 120^ , showing evidence of ordered rotation out to a radius of 5 . A second structure of lower surface brightness gives an overall X-shaped structure to the ELR, similar to that seen in 3C 079. On the NE side this features wraps toward higher values of PA , and it also shows a large velocity gradient and a higher / .

sources.summary: 3C 227 shows core ISM and dimmer IGM, ragged lobes bending around the host major axis, and possible backflows too weakly constrained by the IGM.

quotes.quote: 3C 227 ( z=0.087 ) : 3C 227 exhibits both core ISM and dimmer IGM emission near the host galaxy. The IGM morphology is difficult to study due to the chip boundary. The radio lobes appear to be ragged and bend around the major axis of the host galaxy, suggesting that strong backflows similar to those in the hydrodynamic XRG formation models are at work, but the IGM is too weak near these mini-wings to assess this hypothesis. The galaxy was included in the hardcastle07b study of particle acceleration in hot spots.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0945+07 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This BLRG/FRII is studied in detail by Prieto93 , who found spectacular extended emission line structures. Also, the optical morphology of this system was studied by Heckman86 , and they reported the detection of several knots and tails which very likely correspond to line-emitting gas. Our GMOS-S image (Figure pks0945_online ) does not reveal any of these features, but it shows a shell of _V =24.4 mag arcsec ^ 2 7 kpc to the NW of the nucleus. In fact, emission line contamination of the shell is not a serious issue because strong emission lines do not fall in the ' filter employed in our observations.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 227 as its displayed superdisk-candidate radio galaxy, showing a central radio brightness depression and reporting combined VLA data with no H I emission detection.

quotes.quote: Refs: [ a ] MASS2015 ; [ b ] AFM2015 ; [ ] OKY2015 ; [ d ] LPB1997 ; [ e ] BBLP1992 ; f WR2006 ; [ g ] GKW2000 As an example, we have displayed in Fig fig:superdisk for one of them (3C 227), the grey scale image and the radio brightness profile taken along the major radio axis. The sharply bounded central brightness depression marks the location of the superdisk inferred to be associated with this source (see also BHTB1988 , BBLP1992 , and ``An Atlas of DRAGNs'' 1 http://www.jb.man.ac.uk/atlas/ edited by J. P. Leahy, A. H. Bridle R. G. Strom, for high resolution radio continuum images of these sources).

The total observation time was 11 hours including the calibration and other overheads (total 5 hrs including 4 hrs on-source for 3C 227, and total 2 hrs including 1.5 hr on-source for each of the remaining three sources). To achieve a reasonable sensitivity and uv-coverage, the observations were carried out with a bandwidth of 16 MHz centered at the redshifted H i frequency and using 512 spectral channels. This yielded an overall velocity width of 3500 at a spectral resolution of 7 . The relatively high spectral resolution enabled a more effective RFI flagging. We opted for a large velocity coverage considering the total lack of a priori information about the width of the putative H i emission line. The 512 channels provided enough ``line-free'' (and RFI-free) channels to be able to properly subtract the continuum and thus establish a flat spectral baseline. The data reduction was done in the Common Astronomy Software Applications (CASA) package version - 4.6.0 CASA2007 and the Astronomical Image Processing System (AIPS) version - 31DEC17 gustav96 following standard procedures. As a consistency check, the initial flagging and calibration for one of the targets (namely, 3C 98) were carried out both manually and using the VLA Scripted Calibration Pipeline version - 1.3.8 CASA2007 . Subsequently, for flagging and calibration of the rest of the data, we only used the pipeline. Additional data flagging, initial imaging and self-calibration of the continuum data, and the final continuum imaging were done using AIPS. The AIPS task UVSUB was then used to subtract the continuum, CVEL to accurately convert the observed frequency to Heliocentric velocity, and thereafter, IMAGR was run on the residual data to make image cubes at various spectral resolutions. Residual spectral baseline was removed by using the task IMLIN on the spectral data cube. Finally, we visually inspected the cube for any emission signatures and to extract the spectrum of the central radio emission gap. For 3C 227, the data from the two observing sessions were analyzed separately and then combined with inverse variance weighting at the final stage subsequent to the continuum subtraction.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 227's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 227 is a FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy SDSS J094745.14+072520.5 at a redshift of z= 0.0858 ( D 414 Mpc), containing a radio-loud Sy 1.5 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . In addition to the supergalactic-scale biconical radio lobes in the east-west directions (PA 90 ; e.g., black_study_1992 ), 3C 227 possesses one of the most extended emission line regions with our line of sight grazing the edge of the AGN ionization cone ( > 200 kpc; baum_extended_1988,prieto_extended_1993 ). /IRS and MIPS data are available for this object, in which it appears point-like.

sources.summary: The paper notes 3C 227 has a covering factor above unity and clear large-scale [O III] emission, including faint extended emission over 20 kpc southeast of the galaxy.

quotes.quote: Four sources (3C 93.1, 3c 129, 3C 227, 3C 388) show covering factors larger than unity.

However, for at least 5 ( 26 ) galaxies in our sample, all HEGs (3C 33, 3C 180, 3C 227, 3C 234, 3C 285), [ O III ] extended emission is clearly observed at larger scales (on kpc scale) than that of H .
For example, in the case of 3C 227, our images show faint extended [ O III ] emission (over 20 kpc) towards the southeast of the galaxy that is not visible in the corresponding H image.

gmw_00000056.name: 3C 264
gmw_00000056.overview: Hosted by the elliptical galaxy NGC 3862 in the cluster Abell 1367 [arXiv:1905.06634, arXiv:2110.07373], 3C 264 is a Fanaroff-Riley I radio galaxy [arXiv:1905.06634, arXiv:1909.05323] featuring a face-on circular dust disk [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256, arXiv:0901.0913] and a double-horn molecular gas disk [arXiv:1001.5009]. The active galactic nucleus powers a relativistic, multiwavelength jet [arXiv:2009.09039, arXiv:0712.0595] and exhibits variable very-high-energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:2108.05460, arXiv:2210.09797].

sources.summary: The paper presents radio and X-ray analysis of the TeV-emitting Fanaroff-Riley I radio galaxy 3C 264, describing its jet, host environment, black hole, and head-tailed radio structure.

quotes.quote: In March 2018, reported the detection by VERITAS of very-high-energy emission ( VHE; > 100 GeV ) from 3C 264. This is the sixth, and second most distant, radio galaxy ever detected in the TeV regime. In this article we present a radio and X-ray analysis of the jet in 3C 264. We determine the main physical parameters of the parsec-scale flow and explore the implications of the inferred kinematic structure for radiative models of this ray emitting jet.

3C 264 is a nearby ( D_ L =94 Mpc ) Fanaroff-Riley I radio galaxy whose jet has been extensively studied in the radio, optical, and X-ray bands and references therein 1997ApJ...474..179L, 1997ApJ...483..178B, 2004A&A...415..905L, 2010ApJ...708..171P, 2015Natur.521..495M . The active source is hosted by the elliptical galaxy NGC 3862, located off-center in the rich cluster Abell 1367, and harbouring a supermassive black hole with mass M_ BH 4.7 10^8 M_ . This mass was derived by from the stellar velocity dispersion using the relation by . On kilo-parsec scales, 3C 264 presents a complex head-tailed radio structure embedded in a vast region of low surface brightness emission, which may be indicative of a dense environment.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3862 as an Abell 1367 radio galaxy with a nearby companion, circular dust disk, nuclear emission, blue nucleus, and optical jet.

quotes.quote: NGC 3862 (3C 264) NGC 3862 is part of the cluster Abell 1367 and has a nearby lenticular companion (IC 2955) at a distance of 54'' (22 kpc). There is an apparent faint face-on circular dust disk (Crane 1993) 610 pc ( 1.5'' ) in diameter which is studied in detail by Baum (1997). The apparent disk is edge-darkened, especially the southern half. This might be a projection effect or could indicate that the inner parts of the dust disk are swept clear by either the jet or some other nuclear process (Hutchings 1998). The \ emission is strongly peaked at the nucleus and diffuse low-level emission is seen throughout the disk.

There is a strong blue nuclear point source. 3C 264 is a head-tail radio source displaying S-shaped wiggles extending to a long bifurcated tail of diffuse radio emission (e.g., Baum 1988; Parma 1991; Lara 1999). The optical jet, discovered by Crane (1993), seems to interact with the surrounding ISM (Baum 1997).

sources.summary: VERITAS observed 3C 264 as a nearby FR-I radio galaxy, detecting an active VHE state and finding hard, variable VHE emission without clear knot-collision evidence.

quotes.quote: 3C 264 is an FR-I type radio galaxy at redshift z =0.0216 . It is considered a more distant ( 6 times) analog to M 87, which is a well-known VHE emitter. 3C 264 is an MeV-GeV source, and the extrapolation of its 3FHL spectrum ( _ 3FHL = 1.65 ) to the VHE band (F( > 200 GeV) 1.6 Crab flux) suggests it should be detectable with VERITAS. A remarkable aspect of 3C 264 is its rapidly evolving knot structure revealed by long-term Hubble Space Telescope observations .

Given several compelling motivations, 3C 264 was observed with VERITAS for 10 h in 2017 and a weak excess was initially observed. A follow-up campaign was organized for 2018, and the source was immediately detected in an active VHE state ( 1 Crab flux) in a similar data sample ( 12 h) (ATel 11436). This led to the initiation of a large VERITAS campaign, and a significant MWL follow-up effort including VLA, VLBI, Chandra, HST, Swift and various ground-based optical facilities, with the hope of observing intra-jet phenomena.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 264 as a nearby radio galaxy with a superluminal optical jet, transitional blazar/radio-galaxy orientation, and survey-selection implications.

quotes.quote: The parameters of track (D) were chosen for the curve to pass near the few sources with relatively high _ peak and low L_ peak , one of which is the well-known source 3C 264, which hosts a superluminal optical jet . The general lack of sources in this region is important evidence for velocity gradients in type I jets, since `simple' (single-velocity flow) de-beaming of the high-peak-frequeency BL Lacs should lead to a population of radio galaxies in this region. Although 3C 264 is nominally a radio galaxy, it is close to the `transition zone' between radio galaxies and blazars with an orientation angle at or below 10 degrees 3c264_veritas_2020 . Thus it seems more likely that 3C 264 is a slightly misaligned low-power analog to the higher-power jets that live above it in the plane.

The source is well-studied enough for inclusion likely only because it is very nearby ( z = 0.02 ; d_A =91 Mpc) and has a prominent optical jet. It is found in both the 3C and 4C surveys; a theoretical source at the same radio luminosity would drop out of the 3C survey beyond z 0.03 , and from the 4C survey beyond z 0.08 . Thus similar sources likely exist, but would require dedicated deep multi-wavelength surveys to detect and characterize.

sources.summary: The paper reports VLBI polarimetry of 3C 264, finding pc-scale core and jet polarization, especially a highly polarized knot, while noting uncertain inner-jet field orientation and a longitudinal field region.

quotes.quote: We observed the four FRI radio galaxies viz., 3C 66B, 3C 78, 3C 264 and 3C 270, at 8.4 GHz ( ) with the Very Large Baseline Array (VLBA) 4 The NRAO is operated for the National Science Foundation (NSF) by Associated Universities, Inc. (AUI), under a cooperative agreement. The European VLBI Network (EVN) is a joint facility of European, Chinese, South African and other radio astronomy institutes funded by their national research councils. and five stations of the European VLBI Network ^4 viz., Effelsberg, Onsala, Yebes, Medicina, and the phased WSRT, on 2002 March 1. The observations were made in a ``snap-shot'' mode with the observing time per target totalling five to eight hours.

The fractional polarization in the pc-scale radio cores is m_ 0.4-1 , with the degree of polarization rising to m_ i-j 5-10 in the inner jet ( 1 pc from the core; see Table 1). We also detected jet polarization of m_ j 20 further from the cores of 3C 78 and 3C 264, and m_ j reaches 60 in a knot about 1.5 pc from the core of 3C 264. These values are comparable to those observed for the pc-scale jets of BL Lacs and quasars, indicating the presence of appreciably ordered B fields and little depolarization.

It is noteworthy that the two radio galaxies with the highest jet-to-counterjet intensity ratios ( i.e., those expected to be most highly Doppler beamed of the four), viz., 3C 78 and 3C 264, have more detected polarization and structure in their jets than the other two sources. 3C 78 shows a clear alternation of regions with transverse and longitudinal inferred B field along its jet, reminiscent of similar alternating B field structures observed in the BL Lacs OJ 287 GabuzdaGomez01 and 1418+546 (P05), which have been interpreted as evidence for ``global'' intrinsic fields associated with the jets. We also observe a region of roughly longitudinal B field shifted toward the southern edge of the jet of 3C 264 ( 1.3 pc from the core). This could be a consequence of shearing of the jet due to its interaction with the surrounding medium Laing99 , or alternatively may come about because the jets have a helical B field (Lyutikov, Pariev, Gabuzda 2005). Such polarized ``sheaths'' have been observed in the quasar 1055+088 (Attridge, Roberts, Wardle 1999; P05) and a number of BL Lacs (P05).

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C264 as a narrow-angle-tail FRI radio galaxy in an S0 host, with an optical jet, face-on dust disk, and parsec-scale polarized jet emission.

quotes.quote: The radio morphology of 3C264 is of the narrow-angle-tail type ODeaOwen85 . It is hosted by an S0 galaxy 93 Mpc away that lies in a dense part of the Abell cluster 1367 Schombert86 . Like 3C66B and 3C78, the radio jet in 3C264 also has an optical counterpart Crane93 . HST images show that the optical jet is projected onto a face-on dust disk deKoff00 .

3C264: Our observations reveal polarized emission at 5 and 8 GHz in two regions in the jet 1 and 4 mas away from the core, similar to that observed by Kharb05 . We could obtain an RM map using only the 5 and 8 GHz maps.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 264 as a variable radio galaxy with emission above 30 GeV, a hard spectrum, no significant sub-GeV excess, and existing TeV detection.

quotes.quote: We find statistical evidence of flux variability on 6 month timescales for seven of the 26 radio galaxies analysed: 3C 111, 3C 120, 3C 264, IC 4516, NGC 1218, NGC 2892 and PKS 0625-35.

In most cases we detect no significant excess above 30 GeV . The exceptions to this are 3C 264, 4C +39.12, B2 1447+27, Fornax A, NGC 1218, NGC 2329 and PKS 0625-35. Two of these are detected at TeV energies: PKS 0625-35 Abdalla-2018-MNRAS and 3C 264 Mukherjee-2018-ATel-11436 . Apart from Fornax A, these radio galaxies all exhibit fairly hard spectra over the energy band considered in this analysis. In addition, 3C 264 and 4C +39.21 share very similar SED characteristics across the energy band. Intriguingly, no significant excess above background is detected for either of these two radio galaxies at energies below 1 GeV .

sources.summary: The paper notes 3C 264's elliptical host, relativistic jet, LAT photon, later VERITAS VHE detection, and HAWC non-detection consistent with low expected flux.

quotes.quote: 3C 264 is a radio galaxy hosted by the elliptical galaxy NGC 3862, at a distance of about 90 Mpc. A compact radio source powers a relativistic jet imaged in radio and in the optical 2004A&A...415..905L,1993ApJ...402L..37C . 3C 264 shows in the LAT data up to a highest energy photon of 97 GeV. It was listed as a TeV=N source in the 3FHL catalog, and considered as such in this analysis.

However, it was later detected in the VHE range by the VERITAS Collaboration 2018ATel11436....1M . The photon flux measured by VERITAS above 300 GeV indicates that this object should be too faint for HAWC.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 264 as an FR-I radio galaxy with variable VHE emission, an unusual high-frequency synchrotron peak, and an orientation unlike M 87.

quotes.quote: 3C 264 is an FR-I type radio galaxy at z =0.0216 . It was observed with VERITAS for 57 hr from 2017-19, resulting in the 7.8 discovery of VHE emission . Its VHE flux is variable on monthly timescales and was elevated in 2018.

The 3C 264 SED is unusual for a radio galaxy; its synchrotron peak near the X-rays has relatively high frequency. However, many aspects of the SED can be qualitatively reproduced with an SSC model using parameters typical of BL Lacs. Comparing the SEDs of 3C 264 and M 87 shows differences that are plausibly explained by 3C 264 being oriented closer to the line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 264 as a blazar-like FR I radio galaxy, improving its VHE spectrum fit with pp-interaction emission that slightly exceeds LHAASO sensitivity.

quotes.quote: 3C 264 The FR I RG 3C 264 is the fourth RG detected at VHE band 2020ApJ...896...41A . In 2017-2019, a mild VHE variability is detected by VERITAS, while other energy bands are basically in the low-state. Although 3C 264 is classified as a RG, the observation of apparent speeds constrains its jet viewing angle to a small value _ obs <10^ 2019A&A...627A..89B .

In our modeling, we also treat 3C 264 as a blazar, and fix _ obs = 5 and =10 . Compared to the one-zone leptonic modeling in Ref.  2020ApJ...896...41A , our modeling improves the fitting of VHE spectrum by further considering the emission from pp interactions. Since 3C 264 is the most distant RG, the EBL absorption becomes significant.

sources.summary: The appendix describes 3C 264 as a CO double-horn molecular disk system with a visible optical disk, jet-related synchrotron emission, and a smooth circular host image.

quotes.quote: 3C 264: NGC 3862 (in Abell 1367). This galaxy has a double horn profile, in both transition lines, typical of a molecular gas disk (see Figure onlineSpectra ). The velocity width of this galaxy is about 200 km/s. The disk profile can be seen in the optical image, which reveals also a jet Crane93 . suggest that the optical synchrotron emission, clearly visible in the optical image from the HST, is associated with the jet.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 3862 is best fit by host-galaxy components, with no significant AGN infrared contribution and no evidence for a warm IR-bright obscuring structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 3862 The best fit model for this source requires only components from the host galaxy suggesting that the AGN does not contribute significantly to the IR flux. The largest difference between the best fit model and the data is the 70 m but that point is an upper limit and within the uncertainty on the best fit model spectrum. The forced torus model in this source is quite reasonable albeit rather warm with a peak around 10 m , providing a firm upper limit on any possible torus component of 17 of the flux at 5 m , 50 of the flux at 15 m , and 13 of the flux at 30 m . However, it is also unnecessary to explain the infrared spectrum as the best fit has a BIC of 391.9 and the forced-torus fit has a BIC of 420.3 . Therefore, we see no evidence for a warm IR-bright obscuring structure in NGC 3862.

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 264.0 as a nearby bright radio galaxy hosted by NGC 3862, with optical and X-ray jets plus twin-tail radio structure.

quotes.quote: 3C 264.0 (NGC 3862, LEDA 36606) is among the closest known bright radio galaxies with an optical jet (also detected at X-ray energies by Chandra X-ray Observatory) at a redshift of 0.0217 and hence had distance of 94 Mpc . It is hosted by NGC 3862, a large elliptical galaxy offset to the south-east from the center of the cluster Abell 1367. On large scales it exhibits a twin-tail radio structure extending to the north-east . On arcsec scales it consists of a compact core, with a one-sided, nearly knot-free jet extending also to the north-east and a weak counter jet . The optical jet counterpart extends for only roughly 2 arcsec beyond the galaxy core.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3862 (3C 264) as a radio galaxy with a jet-like structure and BL Lac-like X-ray spectrum dominated by a featureless power law.

quotes.quote: NGC 3862 (3C 264) It is a bright radio source (3C 264) with a clear jet-like structure (Bridle Vall e e, 1981). Its optical spectrum is that of a normal elliptical galaxy with only one very weak emission line [ N II ] and no detectable Balmer emission (Elvis et al. 1981). Elvis et al. (1981) suggested that its nucleus was similar to a low-luminosity BL Lac object based on its strong nuclear X-ray emission revealed by \ and basically featureless optical spectrum.

However, for NGC 3862, even if only 2 - 9 keV spectrum is fitted, the photon index is large at 2.72 ^ +0.19 _ 0.18 ( ^ 2 = 28.4/41). The spectrum of NGC 3862 is similar to the steep spectra found in BL Lac objects (photon index 2.5; e.g., Ciliegi, Bassani Caroli 1995; Kubo et al.

sources.summary: The narrow-angle-tail galaxy 3C 264, near the Abell 1367 X-ray centroid, has both extended emission and a strong point-like component associated with the radio source.

quotes.quote: 3C 264 This narrow-angle-tail radio galaxy lies 7 arcmin away from the X-ray centroid of the cluster Abell 1367, and 8.4 arcmin off-axis in the PSPC observation. We use the parametrised off-axis PSPC PSF (Hasinger \ 1995) to perform a radial-profile fit in a standard 2-arcmin source region, which excludes most of the emission from the cluster. The results (Table profile ) suggest that there is some extended emission associated with the radio source, in addition to a strong point-like component. Our result is consistent with the count rate quoted by Edge R ottgering (1995).

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 264 as an Abell 1367 tailed radio source with a bright one-sided multiwavelength jet, inner dusty disk, apparent ring, and NICMOS bright nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3C 264; z=0.021718 3C 264 is a tailed radio source in the cluster Abell 1367 http://www.jb.man.ac.uk/atlas/dragns.html . On the arcsec scale there is a bright one-sided jet which is detected in the radio baum+97,lara+99 , optical crane+93 , and X-ray tilak06,padgett+05 . baum+97 note the existence of an inner dusty disk which causes an apparent ring in the galaxy surface brightness where the disk ends. The NICMOS image shows a bright nucleus, the jet and the ring noted by Baum et al.

sources.summary: 3C 264 is a head-tail galaxy with northeastern radio lobes, a larger radio envelope around the AGN, and nearly circular optical and diffuse X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 264 ( z = 0.021 ) : 3C 264 is a head-tail galaxy whose radio lobes both extend to the northeast, but there is a large envelope of radio emission around the AGN which is larger than the extent of the 2 _ sky DSS optical isophotes. This data set is subarrayed to 1/8 of the ACIS-S3 area and suffers from a readout streak. We subtracted the streak and this removed a relatively small number of photons from the diffuse ISM. Both the host galaxy and the diffuse X-ray emission appear almost circular on the sky such that the position angle of the ellipses we fit (Table eccentricitytable ) are not well constrained.

sources.summary: The paper uses elliptical NGC3862 as an AGN-classification example, noting its A1367 rank, prior AGN classification, 3C-264 radio/optical jet activity, and emission-line spectrum near the LIN threshold.

quotes.quote: Among the galaxies in our sample, we might consider for example the elliptical NGC3862 (CGCG 97-127), which is the second brightest galaxy of A1367 and was classified as an AGN by V e ron-Cetty V e ron (2006). It harbors the radio galaxy 3C-264 (Gavazzi et al. 1981) and an optical jet (Crane et al. 1993), leaving little doubt that it contains a central monster. Its optical spectrum (taken either at Loiano or from SDSS) displays a strong NII emission line (of EW 4.81 ) and a weak H EW of 1.51 . After adding 1.3 to the underlying absorption, the galaxy just misses the H >3 threshold, thereby fails to be classified as a LIN but not as a RET according to the criteria of Cid Fernandes et al. (2010, 2011) Similar examples (see Section results ) convinced us to lower the threshold for LIN from 3 to 1.5 in the present analysis. .

sources.summary: The proceedings identify 3C 264 as a newly added VHE radio galaxy and describe VERITAS observations motivated by jet-knot interaction, with variable VHE emission but no strong flare.

quotes.quote: The VHE gamma-ray catalog of radio galaxies still contains only a handful of sources. The newest addition is 3C 264 . The VERITAS observations of this source were motivated by the upcoming interaction of two knots moving with different velocities along the jet. Despite an elevated flux in optical and X-rays and the detection of a variable VHE gamma-ray emission, no strong VHE gamma-ray flare has been seen, suggesting that the real interaction of the knots might still happen in the near future .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC3862 as the A1367 AGN point source, fitting its Chandra spectrum over 1-5 keV and finding that ApBC can report bounds when the single-coupling method fails.

quotes.quote: The AGN NGC3862 within A1367 (A1367).
