We have initially analyzed the spectra from the observation divided into 3 periods (as in Table 2). They are well described by a hard power-law spectrum with 0.5 with neither intrinsic absorption nor Compton reflection and are almost identical. Therefore, we consider hereafter only the total spectrum. Table 8 gives the fit results, and Figure 7 shows the unfolded spectrum and the best-fit model. We see that allowing for the presence of Compton reflection does not improve the fit, and /2 0.3 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 382 with Suzaku, XMM-Newton, and Swift/BAT, finding a relativistic Fe K line, warm absorption, and ionized reflection from an accretion disk.

quotes.quote: A Relativistic Fe K Emission Line in 3C 382? Inspection of Figure 3 (upper panel) suggests the intriguing possibility that the 5.7 keV and 6.9 keV emission features represent two components of a single, broader feature, i.e. a relativistic Fe K line. More precisely, they would correspond to the broad red-wing and sharp blue-peak components of such a line, respectively (e.g., Fabian et al. 1989). As an initial test to this hypothesis we replaced these two features with a single relativistic line component, diskline in +XSPEC+ (Fabian et al. 1989), in the model discussed in the previous section and reported in Table 3.

This paper presented a 116 ks \ observation of the Broad-Line Radio Galaxy \ ( z =0.057). A shorter (20 ks), unpublished \ EPIC exposure was discussed, as well as a \ BAT spectrum from integrating 5-years of survey observations. The main results of the data analysis are: The soft X-ray spectrum - Below 2 keV, the XIS data indicate the presence of a warm absorber, with parameters consistent with those derived from the analysis of a previous \ HETGS spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper uses NuSTAR observations of 3C 382 to argue that its X-ray continuum is dominated by a Seyfert-like thermal Comptonizing corona with weak reflection features.

quotes.quote: NuSTAR Reveals the Comptonizing Corona of the Broad-Line Radio Galaxy 3C 382 D. R. Ballantyne 1 , J. M. Bollenbacher 1 , L. W. Brenneman 2 , K. K. Madsen 3 , M. Balokovi 3 , S. E. Boggs 4 , F. E. Christensen 5 , W. W. Craig 5,6 , P. Gandhi 7 , C. J. Hailey 8 , F. A. Harrison 3 , A. M. Lohfink 9 , A. Marinucci 10 , C. B. Markwardt 11 , D. Stern 12 , D. J. Walton 3 , and W. W. Zhang 11 1 Center for Relativistic Astrophysics, School of Physics, Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta, GA 30332; david.ballantyne@physics.gatech.edu 2 Harvard-Smithsonian CfA, 60 Garden St. MS-67, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA 3 Cahill Center for Astronomy and Astrophysics, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA 91125, USA 4 Space Science Laboratory, University of California, Berkeley, California 94720, USA 5 DTU SpaceNational Space Institute, Technical University of Denmark, Elektrovej 327, 2800 Lyngby, Denmark 6 Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Livermore, California 94550, USA 7 Department of Physics, University of Durham, South Road, Durham DH1 3LE, UK 8 Columbia Astrophysics Laboratory, Columbia University, New York, New York 10027, USA 9 Department of Astronomy, University of Maryland, College Park, MD 20742-2421 USA 10 Dipartimento di Matematica e Fisica, Universit a degli Studi Roma Tre, via della Vasca Navale 84, 00146 Roma, Italy 11 NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Maryland 20771, USA 12 Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA 91109, USA Broad-line radio galaxies (BLRGs) are active galactic nuclei that produce powerful, large-scale radio jets, but appear as Seyfert 1 galaxies in their optical spectra. In the X-ray band, BLRGs also appear like Seyfert galaxies, but with flatter spectra and weaker reflection features.

This cooling behavior is typical of Comptonizing corona in Seyfert galaxies and is distinct from the variations observed in jet-dominated sources. In the high flux observation, simultaneous \ data are leveraged to obtain a broadband spectral energy distribution and indicates that the corona intercepts 10 of the optical and ultraviolet emitting accretion disk. \ exhibits very weak reflection features, with no detectable relativistic \ line, that may be best explained by an outflowing corona combined with an ionized inner accretion disk.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 382 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: 3C 382 is a nearby (z=0.058) broad-line radio-loud AGN having a supermassive black hole of mass (1.0 0.3) 10^9 M _ Fausnaugh2017 . In the X-ray regime, this source has a strong soft-excess and Wozniak1998, Grandi2001 showed that the soft-excess can not be explained by extended thermal emission. 3C 382 have been observed with XMM-Newton and Swift /XRT from 2008 to 2021. For the spectral analysis, we used the composite model as, TBabs*zTBabs*(powerlaw+powerlaw+zGauss) . The component zGauss was used to fit the Fe K _ line at 6.42 0.14 keV with a width of 98 59 eV for z=0.058.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes 3C 382 as a nearby broad-line radio galaxy with variable optical lines, Fanaroff-Riley II radio structure, an inclined jet, and a bright X-ray nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3C 382 is a nearby ( z=0.058 ), well-studied BLRG with strong and variable, broad optical lines (FWHM 11,800 for H ; Eracleous Halpern 1994). It exhibits a classical Fanaroff-Riley II radio morphology, with a 1.68 -long jet extending NE of the core and two radio lobes, with total extension of 3 \ (Black et al. 1992). The inferred inclination angle of the jet of 3C 382 is i > 15 \ (Eracleous Halpern 1998). The nucleus of 3C 382 is a bright X-ray source (F _ 2-10 keV 3 10^ 11 ).

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes 3C 382 NuSTAR spectra and argues spectra ratios favor a higher corona temperature at higher X-ray flux rather than the literature trend.

quotes.quote: 3C 382: Through fitting two NuSTAR spectra with thermal Comptonization model compPS Poutanen1996 , claimed the corona temperature k T _e increased from 231 ^ +50 _ 88 keV in the high flux observation (NuSTAR Obs. A) to 330 30 keV in the low flux data (NuSTAR Obs. B, see Table. 6AGNs ). Galactic absorption with N_H = 6.98 10^ 20 cm^ 2 and a ionized warm absorber with N_H = 1.4 10^ 21 cm^ 2 and log =2.5 and a narrow FeK line fixed at 6.4 keV are included in model. However, this model appears inconsistent with the spectra ratio at > 30 keV (Fig. osufs osratio ), the latter suggests a higher E _c in Obs. A instead.
We conclude that for Obs. B, the low k T _e solution is more reasonable, and 3C 382 shows higher corona temperature (instead of lower corona temperature) at higher X-ray flux (Table. 6AGNs ).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 382 as a luminous AGN categorized differently from an earlier outflow study, noting XMM grating evidence for a highly ionized absorber.

quotes.quote: The remaining four (IC 4329A, NGC 5548, 3C 382, and 3C 390.3) are categorized as having outflows in 1997MNRAS.286..513R and non-outflow sources in our study.
Grating spectroscopy with XMM-Newton of 3C 382 revealed a highly ionized ( = 2.69 erg cm s ^ 1 ) absorber with a column of 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 2010MNRAS.401L..10T .
The most luminous sources in our sample, those with L_ 14-195 keV 44.5 , are, from most to least luminous, 4C +74.26, MR 2251--178, 3C 390.3, 3C 382, 1H 0419--577, and Mrk 926.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 382's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 382 is a flat-spectrum FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy Z 173-14 martel_hubble_1999 at a redshift of z= 0.0579 ( D 267 Mpc). It contains an AGN with a Sy 1 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 that is a member of the nine-month BAT AGN sample. 3C 382 features supergalactic-scale biconical radio lobes with a prominent jet extending to the north-eastern lobe (PA 50 ; e.g., black_study_1992 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 382 as a broad-line radio galaxy with an atypically strong nucleus that extends the radio-optical correlation and overlaps the X-ray selected BL Lac region.

quotes.quote: The two broad-line objects in the sample, 3C 382 and 3C 390.3, show atypically strong nuclei, as would be expected from their ground-based classification as N-galaxies.

The correlation is strong over several orders of magnitude of luminosity, extending further than that of Chiaberge \ (1999) primarily because of the inclusion of the luminous broad-line objects 3C 382 and 3C 390.3.

In fact, Fig. \ alpha shows that they occupy a region which overlaps with the RBLs, and is clearly distinguishable from the region occupied by X-ray selected BL Lacs (XBLs); the only two objects to overlap with the XBLs are the broad-line radio galaxies 3C 382 and 3C 390.3.

gmw_00000065.name: 3C 403
gmw_00000065.overview: 3C 403 is a narrow-line [arXiv:2403.00580, arXiv:0810.3764] Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770, arXiv:2012.15082] with a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770] and an X-shaped radio morphology [arXiv:2012.15082]. The galaxy features a nuclear water megamaser [arXiv:astro-ph/0408327, arXiv:1310.2770] and hosts ionized gas extending over 45 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1903.10768].

sources.summary: The review highlights 3C 403 as a narrow-line XRG studied with radio, optical, and X-ray data, with synchrotron active-lobe emission, diffuse inverse-Compton wing emission, anomalously flatter wing spectra, and connected lobe-wing polarization.

quotes.quote: For instance, Kraft2005 conducted a comprehensive study employing radio (VLA), optical (HST), and X-ray (Chandra) observations on 3C 403 to model the compact components of the active lobes. Their findings pointed to a synchrotron origin, positioning it as a notable example of synchrotron X-ray emission from the jet of a potent narrow-line radio galaxy see also the case of 3C 433: Miller2009 . Furthermore, their examination of the inhomogeneous diffuse X-ray emission near the western lobe and wing suggested an Inverse-Compton origin involving Cosmic Microwave Background photons, assuming a moderate departure from equipartition.

For example, Rottmann2001 delved into this issue by examining nine prominent XRGs, further identifying 3C 223.1 and 3C 403 as candidates where the wings exhibit flatter spectral indices compared to the active lobes and the jet hotspots. Subsequent work by Dennett-Thorpe2002 on these two sources also provided evidence of comparable or flatter spectral values in the wings than in the active lobes, with the spectral values in wing-lobe regions varying between 0.8 to 0.6 . This discovery has spurred multiple groups to conduct detailed mappings of XRGs, including the aforementioned sources, using multi-frequency observations and spectral mapping.

In numerous XRGs, the smooth transition of magnetic field lines into wing-lobe structures suggests that the two pairs of lobes are likely interconnected entities, implying a shared evolutionary process rather than distinct and independent developments e.g., 3C 315, 3C 223.1, 3C 34, 3C 136.1, 3C 403, PKS 2014-55 Hogbom1974,Black1992,Johnson1995,Rottmann2001,Dennett-Thorpe2002,Cotton2020 .

sources.summary: The paper reports a water megamaser in the radio-loud FR II galaxy 3C 403, locating unresolved maser emission near the active nucleus and favoring a nuclear accretion-disk origin.

quotes.quote: We report the first detection of a water megamaser in a radio-loud galaxy, 3C 403, and present a follow-up study using the VLA. 3C 403 has been observed as a part of a small sample of FR II galaxies with evidence of nuclear obscuration. The isotropic luminosity of the maser is 1200 L _ . With a recessional velocity of z 17680 km s ^ 1 it is the most distant water maser so far reported. The line arises from the densest ( > 10 ^ 8 cm ^ 3 ) interstellar gas component ever observed in a radio-loud galaxy.

The interferometric observation performed with the VLA in its A configuration confirms that also the megamaser in 3C 403 has a nuclear origin. Only VLBI interferometric observations at milliarcsecond resolution (spatial scales of 1 parsec) will allow us to determine (or to provide an upper limit to) the extent of the emission and to pinpoint the exact location of the H _2 O emitting region(s) in order to see if the maser is associated with an accretion disk or radio jets.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3CR 403 as a worked example of its color-profile method, measuring seeing, deriving radial R-K behavior, and estimating nuclear K-band luminosity while testing seeing and gradient uncertainties.

quotes.quote: For 3CR 403, FWHM=0 89 0.08, and therefore the error on the seeing is 9 - slightly larger than that typical for the studied sample. Each HST image has been then convolved with a Gaussian with the appropriate FWHM and interpolated to match the pixel size of the TNG image. On the `matched' HST image and on the reduced TNG one we performed photometry on annuli of increasing radius out to 3 and an average color (and its associated error) was derived for each annulus, providing us with the radial color dependence of the source.

The above procedure has been repeated twice to take into account the uncertainty on the seeing determination: the HST image has been convolved with a Gaussian with a FWHM increased and decreased by its 1 uncertainty and the whole analysis repeated. In Table tab3 the derived luminosities are listed. The difference in the final L_ K^ for the different seeing is within the 1 error and of order 0.07 dex. As shown in Fig. color3C403_2 (upper panel), there is a diffuse IR excess at radii 1 25 due to a small color gradient, neglected so far. Such gradient has been fitted with a linear model R-K^ _ model =A+Br at radii 1 25, and extrapolated at smaller radii [ the profile and the fitted model are shown in Fig. color3C403_2 (middle panel) ] . This best-fit model is then used as the host galaxy reference F_ K^ /F_ R _ gal at each radius (the associated error has been taken as the scatter around the best-fit) to derive the effective IR excess (Fig. color3C403_2 , lower panel). In Table tab4 the estimated luminosities are listed, also showing the effects of the seeing uncertainty, which is now of order 0.15 dex. The color gradients indeed represent the main limiting factor for the accuracy of this method.

sources.summary: The paper uses previous Chandra results for 3C 403, emphasizing X-ray detections in E-lobe hotspots F1 and F6, radio-coincident peaks, inward X-ray extensions, and non-detected secondary hotspots.

quotes.quote: The hotspots of 3C 403 were discussed extensively by khwm05 , and so we do not present images or spectral fits here. To summarize the results that are most important for this paper, the hotspots F1 and F6 in the E lobe have clear X-ray detections, with measured photon indices 1.7 . F6 may be a jet knot, consistent with the detection of jet-related X-ray emission in the same lobe, and if so F1 is the primary hotspot. The peaks of the X-ray emission from F1 and F6 are clearly coincident with the peaks in the radio, unlike several of the other hotspots considered in this paper, but both have X-ray extensions back towards the nucleus that are not present (or at least not nearly as prominent) in the radio. There is no detection of the radio-bright secondary hotspots F2 and F3 and there is no diffuse X-ray emission from the hotspot region.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 403 as an X-shaped narrow-line radio galaxy with older diffuse lobes and a brighter current lobe system, focusing observations on its western hotspot.

quotes.quote: The Narrow Line Radio Galaxy 3C 403 at redshift 0.059 belongs to the peculiar class of X-shaped sources which combine two pairs of lobes extending in different directions from the same core. One of these lobe systems is typically more extended and diffuse, while the other one is shorter, brighter and clearly confined. The more extended lobes would be the remainders of an earlier phase of jet activity, while the shorter, brighter systems would mark the current working direction of the jet. Black et al. (1992) show the overall morphology (their Fig. 13). Hardcastle et al. (1998) suggest that the knot F6 is the primary hotspot in the western lobe.

sources.summary: The paper revisits 3C 403 X-ray modeling, contrasting thermal and power-law interpretations of its soft emission and noting complications from an unexplained 0.85 keV feature.

quotes.quote: 3C 403 ( z=0.059 ) : This dataset was previously studied in detail by kraft05 who attempted to isolate the ISM emission from the nucleus by performing a spectral analysis in which the soft part of the spectrum was fit by an absorbed power law between 1.0-2.0 keV, with a line-dominated thermal fit between 0.3-1.0 keV. They then fit an elliptical profile to these thermal events. This line of reasoning is supported by the absence of a bright core corresponding to the PSF at 0.3-1.0 keV. However, we find an acceptable fit in which an unabsorbed power law dominates between 0.3-2.0 keV. This analysis is also complicated by the presence of a feature at 0.85 keV which is not fit by either kraft05 or ourselves.

sources.summary: The paper maps 3C 403 ionized gas over 45 kpc, mostly in regular rotation, with nuclear high-velocity blueshifted gas and broader lines near the line of nodes.

quotes.quote: 3C 403: FR II/HEG, 1 = 1.15 kpc. The ionized gas extends over 45 kpc forming a diffuse structure in the NE - SW direction. Most of the gas is apparently in very regular rotation with a line of nodes oriented at PA 30^ with a large velocity gradient in the central 3 kpc, reaching an amplitude of 300 , followed by a smooth decrease at larger radii. High velocity blueshifted gas, reaching 800 is seen on the nucleus. Similarly to what is seen in 3C 033, lines are broader in a region almost aligned with the line of nodes.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 403 as a narrow-line FR II radio galaxy whose east jet has knots F1 and F6, a measured redshift, and large viewing angle.

quotes.quote: 3C 403: 3C 403 is one of the best examples of synchrotron emission from the jet of a powerful narrow-line radio galaxy Kraft2005 . The mass of its SMBH is 1.8 10^ 8 as estimated from its K-band bulge luminosity Vasudevan2010 . According to the unified models of FR II radio galaxies, the jets of narrow-line radio galaxies are at a large viewing angle of > 45 Kraft2005,Barthel1989 . The east jet of 3C 403 includes two significant knots, F1 and F6. The measured redshift of the host galaxy is z = 0.059 , corresponding to a luminosity distance of 260.6 Mpc (1 = 1.127 \ kpc) .

sources.summary: The appendix describes 3C 403 as a CO(1-0)-detected double-horn source with an elliptical central system, halo boundary, and weak dust lanes.

quotes.quote: 3C 403: It is clearly detected in the only transition observed, CO(1-0), with a velocity width of 500 km/s. It presents the double horn profile already mentioned before and shown in Figure onlineSpectra . suggest this galaxy consists of two systems: a central elliptical region surrounded by a low-surface brightness halo with a sharp boundary at a distance of 3 Kpc northwest of the nucleus. In the northwest region of the halo, two or three very weak dust lanes are barely discernible.

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C 403 as an XRG with detected shells and radio-spectral constraints whose wing sizes and spectra are inconsistent with simple backflow models.

quotes.quote: In addition, more direct evidence for merger has recently come from the recent detection of shells in two XRGs, 3C 403 (Almeida et al. \ 2011) and 4C + 00.58 (Hodges-Kluck et al. \ 2010b).

A modified version of this scheme was proposed by Dennett-Thorpe et al. \ (2002), based on their analysis of radio spectral variation across two XRGs, 3C 223.1 and 3C 403. Although a careful analysis of photometric and spectroscopic evidence led Dennett-Thorpe et al. \ (2002) to conclude there is no evidence in the galaxy or its environs for a recent merger activity in either 3C 223.1 or 3C 403 (see, also, Landt et al. \ 2010, for a similar assertion about a larger sample of XRGs), they point out that the backflow models are not consistent with the relative sizes and spectra of the wings in these sources. Hence Dennett-Thorpe et al. suggest that the required rapid change in the jet axis within several Myr is either caused by a delayed SMBH merger following ingestion of a small galaxy by the radio galaxy, which left no obvious signature in the latter's stellar population, or, alternatively, the axis flip may have occurred due to accretion disk instabilities (e.g., Natarajan Pringle 1998).

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 403's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 403 is a FR II radio source identified with the early-type galaxy LEDA 63758 martel_hubble_1999 at a redshift of z= 0.059 ( D 271 Mpc) with a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It features a complex super-galactic-scale X-shaped radio morphology with a twin jet feeding biconical radio lobes (PA 70 ; e.g., black_study_1992 ), and a nuclear water mega-maser tarchi_discovery_2003,tarchi_innermost_2007 . After first being detected in the MIR with , 3C 403 was followed up with /IRAC, IRS and MIPS and is only marginally resolved in all images.

sources.summary: The ANTARES stacking analysis identifies 3C403 as the radio galaxy with the smallest p-value, describes its FRII X-shaped morphology, and reports it as an interesting target.

quotes.quote: Their properties are thus investigated in more details in the following sections. t Radio galaxies The radio galaxy with the smallest p-value is 3C403, which is found with a pre-trial p-value of p=2.3 10^ 4 , equivalent to 3.7 .

The galaxy 3C403 is an FRII that presents a peculiar radio morphology, called ``-X-shape'' or ``-Winged" (see figure 1 of ) due to a recent change of the jet axis orientation, that could be the signature of a binary black hole merger or the fusion with a small galaxy.
Of the individual sources, the radio galaxy 3C403 ( P=0.013 ) and the blazar MG3 J225517+2409 ( P=0.15 ) are reported as interesting targets.

gmw_00000066.name: 3C 424
gmw_00000066.overview: 3C 424 is a radio galaxy at a redshift of 0.127 [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1310.2770] that exhibits a borderline FR I/II morphology [arXiv:1310.2770] and a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770]. It resides in a dense environment near the edge of a galaxy cluster [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1210.6027] and displays faint northern tidal distortions [arXiv:0712.0595].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 424 as dominated by a bright east-west emission-line feature plus perpendicular diffuse emission with high velocity dispersion northwest of the nucleus.

quotes.quote: 3C 424: FR II/LEG, 1 = 2.29 kpc. The emission line structure is dominated by a bright linear feature in the EW direction which shows an overall blueshift of 100 . More diffuse emission extends along a perpendicular axis out to 30 kpc with an overall redshift of 150 \ on the SE side but reaching 450 \ on the NW side where we also find the regions of highest velocity dispersion, up to 450 . Generally, the nuclear regions have a higher / \ ratio that the rest of the emission line nebula.

sources.summary: The atlas describes 3C 424 as a dense-environment source with northern tidal distortions, many companions in WFPC2 imaging, cluster-edge placement, and northern unresolved or resolved sources.

quotes.quote: 3C 424; z=0.127 Faint tidal distortions are visible to the north on our NICMOS image, and this source lies in a dense environment, with numerous large companions visible on the WFPC2 dekoff+96 image. The radio source apparently lies on the edge of the cluster. Two bright unresolved sources are visible to the north, with a resolved object NW, on the edge of the NICMOS chip, barely detected at R .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 424 as a nearby FR I low-excitation radio galaxy in a dense companion-rich environment, with a possible inner X-ray jet but no cluster X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 424 is a nearby FR I - LEG that lies at redshift 0.127 in a dense environment, with numerous companions clearly detected in the optical HST image dekoff96 . The radio source apparently is close to the edge of a galaxy cluster dekoff96 , however cluster emission is not detected in the X-ray band. The X-ray nucleus has an extension 0.6 \ to the NW which might be the inner segment of a jet.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 424's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 424 is a borderline FR I/II radio source identified with the early-type galaxy LEDA 65411 at a redshift of z= 0.127 ( D 613 Mpc) with a Sy 2 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It features an unusual super-galactic scale radio morphology with faint smooth lobes and faint jets along the north-south axis (PA 25 ; e.g., black_study_1992 ). 3C 424 remained undetected in and appears as a weak point source in the first three bands.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C424 as an FR II radio source whose northern jet is rejected as a true jet while S3 is adopted as the southern jet component.

quotes.quote: 3C424 : we have not considered the northern `jet' of this object to be a true jet. It is however quite possible that some of the internal structure in this northern component should be treated as jet material. We have treated S3 as the only jet component in the southern lobe.

gmw_00000067.name: 3C 445
gmw_00000067.overview: 3C 445 is a nearby elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770] hosting a bright, unresolved Seyfert 1.5 nucleus [arXiv:1909.13756] and classified as a double-double radio galaxy [arXiv:1511.00904, arXiv:0810.3764]. The system features a lobe-dominated radio morphology [arXiv:1909.13756, arXiv:0704.3053] and a triangular emission-line region extending 20 kiloparsecs southwest [arXiv:1903.10768].

sources.summary: Chandra spectroscopy of 3C 445 finds highly ionized, photoionized circumnuclear gas in the nucleus, with sub-parsec BLR-scale emission and a heavily obscured continuum likely tied to an outflowing clumpy disk wind.

quotes.quote: We present evidence for X-ray line emitting and absorbing gas in the nucleus of the Broad-Line Radio Galaxy (BLRG), 3C 445. A 200 ks Chandra LETG observation of 3C 445 reveals the presence of several highly ionized emission lines in the soft X-ray spectrum, primarily from the He and H-like ions of O, Ne, Mg and Si. Radiative recombination emission is detected from O vii and O viii , indicating that the emitting gas is photoionized. The He-like emission appears to be resolved into forbidden and intercombination line components, which implies a high density of >10^ 10 cm ^ 3 , while the Oxygen lines are velocity broadened with a mean width of 2600 km s ^ 1 (FWHM). The density and widths of the ionized lines indicate an origin of the gas on sub-parsec scales in the Broad Line Region (BLR).

The X-ray continuum of 3C 445 is heavily obscured either by a partial coverer or by a photoionized absorber of column density N_ H =2 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 and ionization parameter =1.4 erg cm s ^ 1 . However the view of the X-ray line emission is unobscured, which requires the absorber to be located at radii well within any parsec scale molecular torus. Instead we suggest that the X-ray absorber in 3C 445 may be associated with an outflowing, but clumpy accretion disk wind, with an observed outflow velocity of 10000 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: HE2221--0221 is identified as a double-double radio galaxy with a bright nucleus that nearly outshines its host, broad Pa emission, reddened NIR colors, stellar features, and strong coronal emission.

quotes.quote: HE2221--0221 is also known as 3C 445 and classified as a ``double double'' radio galaxy 2000MNRAS.315..371S . It has a redshift of z=0.057 ( D_L=254.7 ). In near-infrared images, it appears as round elliptical with very bright unresolved nucleus. Our imaging study 2014A&A...561A.140B shows that the host is almost over-shone by the nucleus. The Pa line is very broad and strong.

Indeed, the shape and the NIR colours are extremely red and the continuum fit yields an extinction of A_V=1.6 mag. Nevertheless, the spectrum shows some stellar features, and the stellar contribution is around 30 . The presence of strong [ Si vi ] emission is indicative of nuclear activity .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 445 as a broad-line FR II radio galaxy whose Balmer profiles show a vanished blue system, lobe-dominated radio emission, and heavy nuclear obscuration.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 ( z=0.0562 ; Osterbrock et al. 1976) is also a widely studied broad-line radio galaxy with double-peaked Balmer emission lines, in which irregular, non-symmetric profiles were suggested to result from mass motions of the ionized gas in a relatively small number of ``clouds'' or ``streams'' at one time (Osterbrock et al. 1976). Indeed, the Balmer lines in 3C 445 are more complex than most ordinary double-peaked emitters (see the samples in Eracleous Halpern 2003 and Strateva et al. 2003, 2004). In the following three decades, there have been several spectral observations of its emission-line profiles (e.g., Crenshaw et al. 1988; Eracleous Halpern 1993; Corbett et al. 1998; Buttiglione et al. 2009).

Through the comparison of the emission-line profiles, the blue system of the H line suddenly disappeared completely between 1974 and 1986, but in the next two decades after 1986, the profile of the H line has no significant variation in the velocity or the amplitude. In detail, the three components contained in the Balmer-line profiles exhibit different variations, i.e., the absent blue system, and other two emission components without significant variation. We also checked other emission-line profiles of the H line in the literature (e.g., Cohen et al. 1999; Keel et al. 2005; Jones et al. 2009).

3C 445 was identified as a FR II radio source (Kronberg et al. 1986) with a round elliptical galaxy (Madrid et al. 2006) and a very bright unresolved Seyfert 1.5 nucleus (V e ron-Cetty V e ron 2010). 3C 445 is clearly lobe-dominated with a steep radio spectrum between 2.7 and 4.8 GHz ( ^ 4.8 _ 2.7 = 0.7 ) and a core-to-lobe intensity ratio of R = 0.039 (Morganti et al. 1993).

sources.summary: The paper fits 3C 445 with an added hot soft component possibly tied to a jet, finds model-dependent N_H values, and ultimately classifies it as non-variable in N_H.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 Data reduction/fitting: We used an extraction region of 7 for , as some extended emission is present. The background was taken from an annulus, of radii 10 20 . The source spectra shows a prominent excess at around 2 keV that is best-fit with a second, very hot apec component. It is not obvious whether star-formation, or perhaps the presence of a jet, could result in such very hot gas. Torres-Alba2018 used the two- apec model to explain the soft emisson of a large sample of U/LIRGs, and obtained a T _ 2 distribution of median 0.97 0.18 keV, with a long tail extending up to 4.5 keV. However, this galaxy is not classified as a U/LIRG, nor does it show obvious morphological signs of a merger (that could explain the dense star formation required). The detection of radio emission points toward the presence of a jet, as does the slightly elongated morphology.

Analysis of results: The data is well-fitted by all models, although requires unusually large reflection constants ( A_ s90 and A_ s0 ). It also results in a larger than the other models. Furthermore, and are barely in agreement in their N_ H,los determinations, while results in systematically lower values (incompatible with the other models in 3/5 observations). The most remarkable difference is in the observation, in which models the observed flux with lower obscuration than the other models, and compensates this with a lower intrinsic flux value. Precisely because of this, is the only model that classifies the source as ` N_ H,los variable', according to the p-value. However, the ^2_ red comparison shows that, even for , an alternative fit exists when imposing no N_ H,los variability, with T<3 . Therefore, we opt to classify this source as `Non-variable in N_ H,los '.

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 445 as a nearby broad-line radio galaxy with complex X-ray absorption, cold reflection, Fe emission, and soft X-ray lines from photoionized gas.

quotes.quote: The nearby (z=0.057) radio galaxy \ has an FRII radio morphology (Kronberg, Wielebinski, Graham 1986), with a linear extension up to 10 . With a steep radio spectrum between 2.7 and 4.8 GHz ( _r =0.7) and a core-to-lobe intensity ratio R=0.039 (Morganti, Killeen, Tadhunter 1993), the source is clearly lobe-dominated. From the projected radio size of the source Eracleous Halpern (1998) infer an inclination i> 60 .

Using an archival 15 ks \ spectrum we have shown that the X-ray emission from the nearby BLRG \ is quite complex. In particular, its properties are remarkably similar to those of type-2 radio-quiet AGN, with a heavily absorbed continuum and a strong reflection component, a narrow \ emission line, and several soft X-ray lines consistent with reflection/scattering off a warm ``mirror''. The soft X-ray lines are of particular interest, as this is the first time that such features are detected in a BLRG and indeed in a radio-loud AGN.

sources.summary: Chandra observations of radio galaxy 3C 445 find southern-hotspot X-ray emission displaced from the radio-optical hotspot, ruling out one-zone models and favoring EC/CMB or separate synchrotron emission.

quotes.quote: University Blvd., Melbourne, FL 32901 2 Department of Physics, University of Maryland, Baltimore County, 1000 Hilltop Circle, Baltimore, MD 21250 3 Laboratory for High Energy Astrophysics, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, Codel 661, Greenbelt, MD 20771 4 Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Wyoming, WY 82071 5 Southeastern Association for Research in Astronomy (SARA) NSF-REU Summer Intern at Florida Institute of Technology We present new Chandra observations of the radio galaxy 3C 445, centered on its southern radio hotspot. Our observations detect X-ray emission displaced upstream and to the west of the radio-optical hotspot. Attempting to reproduce both the observed spectral energy distribution (SED) and the displacement, excludes all one zone models. Modeling of the radio-optical hotspot spectrum suggests that the electron distribution has a low energy cutoff or break approximately at the proton rest mass energy.

The X-rays could be due to external Compton scattering of the cosmic microwave background (EC/CMB) coming from the fast (Lorentz factor 4 ) part of a decelerating flow, but this requires a small angle between the jet velocity and the observer's line of sight ( 14^ ). Alternatively, the X-ray emission can be synchrotron from a separate population of electrons.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 445 as a nearby broad-line radio galaxy with hard X-ray spectrum and large absorption, fitted with partial-covering neutral gas.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 The nearby broad-line radio galaxy (BLRG) 3C 445 has very hard power law =1.08 and a large intrinsic absorption ( N_ H 1.4 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ) in our first-step analysis of the XMM-Newton observation data. In Sambruna1998 , the X-ray spectrum of 3C 445 observed with ASCA were described by a dual absorber with an intrinsic flat power-law ( 1.3 ) continuum. We have made our own analysis of the XMM-Newton data with two intrinsic absorbers, neutral partial covering and full covering cold gas in 0.6-12 keV band like Model C of Sambruna1998 .

Our analysis show a hard intrinsic power law ( =1.11 0.05) absorbed by partial covering ( 90 10 ) neutral gas with N_ H 1.32 0.06 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 and no full covering cold gas ( ^2/d.o.f = 1.27/159 ). We employ our XMM-Newton result for intrinsic luminosity calculation and statistics. Taking Sambruna1998 's model had negligible effects on the analysis in next sections.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 445 as a recurrent broad-line radio galaxy and double-double radio galaxy with aligned internal and outer hotspots selected for study.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of 3C 445 is a Broad-Line Radio Galaxy (Eracleous Halpern 1994) at a redshift of 0.05623. This source has been defined as an example of recurrent activity sources. In addition to the bright hotspots in a distance of 315 kpc (north) and 275 kpc (south) of the core, two additional internal hotspots at 47 kpc (north) and 77 kpc (south), well aligned with the outer hotspots and the nucleus are found. These are the characteristics of a Double-Double Radio Galaxy (Lara et al. 1999, Schoenmakers et al. 2000). Both outer hotspots were targets of our study (Figs. 8 9).

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 445 as a faint round-elliptical FR II host dominated by a bright nuclear point source, with spectropolarimetry indicating significant extinction.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 is an FR II radio source hosted by a M_H( Host )=-22.21 round elliptical at z=0.056 . In both the optical and IR images, the morphology is dominated by the bright nuclear point source. This point source dominates the color-map which thus provides no useful information on extinction. cohen+99 deduce between about 1.1 and 2.5 mag of V band extinction from their spectropolarimetric study. This is enough provide up to 0.45 H mag of extinction assuming a diffuse dust model after mathis90 .

sources.summary: The paper reports PKS2221-02s SPIRE measurements are dominated by a nearby companion, so the authors could not deblend them and use upper limits aided by PACS companion measurements.

quotes.quote: Of the 19 objects observed with SPIRE, we identify contamination of the measured flux by companion galaxies in 3 targets -- PKS1648+05, PKS2221-02 and PKS2356-61, (17 of the SPIRE sample) -- at 250 , and 4 objects, adding PKS0915-11, (22 of the SPIRE sample) at 350 and 500 .

PKS1648+05 and PKS2221-02 are dominated at SPIRE wavelengths by their nearby companion, so we were unable to deblend them even with GetSources. Therefore, we only include upper limits for the fluxes of these object in the SPIRE bands. For PKS2221-02, we have plotted in the SED (Figure SED-figure6 ) PACS measurements of the companion object, so the reader can gauge the degree of contamination to these upper limits.

PKS2221-02 - The flux drops rapidly for this object at SPIRE wavelengths, and the SPIRE images are likely to be dominated by the flux of a nearby companion galaxy that is clearly visible in the PACS bands (companion fluxes shown by grey triangles in the SED of Fig 7).

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 445 as a powerful FR II broad-line radio galaxy with lobe-dominated radio morphology, elliptical optical appearance, strong X-ray obscuration, and soft excess emission interpreted as scattering.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 3C 445 is a powerful FR II BLRG with a typical lobe-dominated radio morphology and an elliptical appearance in the optical band (Smith Heckman 1989). The source lies 0.5 from the cluster A 2440, which thus contaminates the spectrum of 3C 445 (Pounds 1990) from (which has the field of view of 1 2 ).
Similarly to Pounds (1990), we find the X-ray source is strongly obscured.

We see that the spectrum of 3C 445 contains a substantial soft X-ray excess component, modeled here as the third power-law component with consistent with null (Table 5). That component can be interpreted as due to scattering of about 5 per cent of the intrinsic emission by a hot plasma in the funnel of an obscuring torus (Krolik Kallman 1987).

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 445 as a broad-line radio galaxy with complex X-ray absorption, anomalous gas-to-dust ratio, and multiple absorbing layers in recent observations.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 : This Broad Line Radio Galaxy is an interesting case: sambruna98 and shinozaki06 both invoked two different absorbing components to fit their respective X-ray spectra. Moreover, the ratio of optical reddening to hydrogen column density is more than one order of magnitude beneath the galactic value. This indicates an anomalous gas-to-dust ratio maiolino01 . The most recent X-ray observations by grandi07 and sambruna07 , again, indicate the presence of multiple absorbing layers with differing covering factors.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 445 as the most distant AGN in the sample, reports no Spitzer PAH emission, and finds T-ReCS silicate emission characteristic of Type 1.5 AGN.

quotes.quote: The slit width used for the spectroscopy results in a spatial resolution between 7-236 pc for all the objects except for 3C 445 (406 pc), which is by far the most distant object in the sample.
The Spitzer spectra are free of PAH emission lines in only 4 objects, named NGC 3281, ESO 103-G35, IC 5063, and 3C 445.

In the T-ReCS spectra, only the spectra of NGC 1365 and 3C 445 show silicate features in emission while NGC 3081 shows very little absorption (see appendix A and _ 9.7 in Table tab:results ). The other sources (i.e. 17 out of 20) show very strong silicate absorptions. Note that NGC 1365 and 3C 445, the two objects showing the silicate feature in emission, are Type 1.5 AGN.

sources.summary: The paper finds 3C 445 has a triangular southwest emission-line region extending to 20 kpc, roughly constant redshifted gas velocity, and outward-increasing line ratio.

quotes.quote: 3C 445: FR II/BLO, 1 = 1.09 kpc. The emission line region in this source has a triangular shape, elongated in the SW direction out to a radius of 20 kpc. The gas velocity is roughly constant, with a redshift of 150 . The / \ ratio increases from the nucleus to the outer regions.

sources.summary: 3C 445 has an off-axis bright nucleus that prevents diffuse ISM ellipse fitting, but the galaxy remains in the sample because of surrounding hot IGM.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 ( z=0.056 ) : The bright nucleus of 3C 445 is far off-axis in this exposure; its brightness and high ellipticity make it impossible to measure the ellipse parameters of the diffuse ISM. Moreover, the martel99 HST image is dominated by the unresolved nucleus, so our DSS parameters may not be accurate. However, we include the galaxy in our sample because of the hot IGM surrounding the host.

sources.summary: The paper describes HE2221-0221 as a very symmetric, nearly circular elliptical with red nuclear colors and a high K-band AGN fraction from decomposition fits.

quotes.quote: 85 HE2221-0221 Very symmetric, almost circular elliptical with r_e=2.4 kpc. The nuclear colors are very red. This is consistent with the high AGN fraction of 65 obtained from our fits in K band.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 445's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 445 is a FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy LEDA 68751 madrid_hubble_2006 at a redshift of z= 0.0559 ( D 254 Mpc) with a Sy 1.5 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . It features the typical supergalactic-scale biconical radio lobes with only a partial jet in the direction to the southern lobe (PA 10 ; e.g., leahy_study_1997 ). After first being detected in the MIR with , 3C 445 was followed up with /ISOCAM siebenmorgen_isocam_2004 and Palomar 5 m/MIRLIN gorjian_10_2004 , where it appeared point-like.

gmw_00000068.name: 3C 449
gmw_00000068.overview: 3C 449 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy [arXiv:1210.7563, arXiv:astro-ph/9802175] identified with the elliptical galaxy UGC 12064 [arXiv:1310.2770], which hosts an active nucleus classified as a LINER [arXiv:1310.2770]. The galaxy features symmetric twin jets [arXiv:1303.6664, arXiv:1310.2770] and extended radio lobes that interact with the surrounding hot gas halo [arXiv:astro-ph/9802175, arXiv:astro-ph/0404440].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Chandra observations of 3C 449, interpreting its gas edge as a sloshing cold front and linking bent jets, lobes, and entrained X-ray gas to radio-galaxy dynamics.

quotes.quote: We present results from a 140 ks Chandra /ACIS-S observation of the hot gas around the canonical FR I radio galaxy 3C 449. An earlier, shorter 30 ks Chandra observation of the group gas showed an unusual entropy distribution and a surface brightness edge in the gas that could be a strong shock around the inner radio lobes. In our deeper data we find no evidence for a temperature increase inside of the brightness edge, but a temperature decrease across part of the edge. This suggests that the edge is a ``sloshing'' cold front due to a merger within the last 1.3--1.6 Gyrs. Both the northern and southern inner jets are bent slightly to the west in projection as they enter their respective lobes, suggesting that the sloshing core is moving to the east.

The radio source 3C 449 is a canonical example of an Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy fr74 and exhibits a complex radio morphology on spatial scales from parsecs to hundreds of kilo-parsecs. The source is characterized by an unresolved core, two symmetrically opposed jets, and very extended lobes perley79 . The jets are relativistic near their bases and decelerate significantly within 10 ^ (= 3.3 kpc) from the core.

sources.summary: The paper presents ROSAT observations of 3C 449, describing its extended X-ray halo, jet and lobe asymmetries, FRI radio-galaxy nature, and interaction between radio plasma and hot gas.

quotes.quote: We present ROSAT PSPC observations of the twin-jet radio galaxy 3C 449. The soft X-ray emission from this object is dominated by an extended halo with a scale comparable to that of the radio source. The asymmetry of the X-ray emission is reflected in that of the radio lobes, providing evidence that the behaviour of the jets is strongly influenced by the external medium.

A region of reduced X-ray surface brightness coincident with the southern radio lobe of 3C 449 suggests that the radio source has displaced thermal plasma from the X-ray-emitting halo. However, the minimum pressure in the radio lobe is considerably lower than our estimates of the pressure in the external medium. We discuss the implications for the dynamics of the radio source. galaxies: individual: 3C 449 -- galaxies: jets -- galaxies: active -- X-rays: galaxies 3C 449 (B2 2229+39) is a well-known FRI radio galaxy (Fanaroff Riley 1974) at a redshift of 0.0171.
In this paper we present new, sensitive observations of 3C 449 with the ROSAT PSPC, which allow us to map in detail the hot gas halo surrounding the source.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 449 as an FR-I radio galaxy whose asymmetric lobe structure reflects surrounding X-ray gas, with lobes underpressured relative to the environment.

quotes.quote: We observed the two radio galaxies 3C 66B ( z=0.0215 ) and 3C 449 ( z=0.0171 ) with XMM-Newton for 20 ks each. Fig. fr1s shows the X-ray emission measured by the three XMM cameras with radio contours overlaid, for both radio galaxies. Below we discuss what the observations reveal about the role of environment in shaping the radio lobes, the radio-source particle content and dynamics, and the impact of the radio galaxies on their environment.

For both sources, the round, sharply bounded lobe (3C 66B east and 3C 449 south) is embedded in bright X-ray emitting material, whereas the narrower plumes (3C 66B west and 3C 449 north) are relatively free of surrounding material. This suggests that the rounded lobes have formed where the jets flowed into dense material, and expansion along the jet axis was slowed; conversely, such lobe structures failed to form on the opposite side of the sources, because the jets encountered less dense material and continued to flow more freely.

We measured the external pressures in the environments of 3C 66B and 3C 449 and compared them with the minimum internal lobe pressures obtained from radio data. Both sources appear to be underpressured by factors of 20 , consistent with the earlier work on this type of source.

sources.summary: The paper reports 3C 449 jet spectral indices consistent with earlier data, no systematic gradients, symmetric inner jets near the sky plane, and an unmodeled velocity field.

quotes.quote: Of the five sources where lobe emission is undetectable over the region of interest, NGC 315 and (at a much lower level) 3C 31 show the spectral flattening away from the axis mentioned earlier, 0326+39 and 3C 449 have no systematic gradients and 1553+24 is not well enough resolved.

3C 449 The spectra of the jets in this source were measured by Feretti99 and studied in detail by K-SR . Our measured spectral indices are consistent with the mean value = 0.58 0.02 between 4.985 and 8.4 GHz with 2.5-arcsec resolution quoted by Feretti99 from the same visibility data once we account for recalibration. Note that the steep-spectrum emission surrounding the jets does not start until well outside our region of interest K-SR . The inner jets are very symmetrical, indicating that they are almost precisely in the plane of the sky. For this reason, we cannot model the jet velocity field, and have no good estimate of the deceleration scale, although by analogy with other sources it is likely to be 10 arcsec.

sources.summary: The paper uses Faraday-rotation observations of the FR I radio galaxy 3C 449 to constrain magnetic-field structure and strength in the hot intra-group plasma around it.

quotes.quote: The goal of this work is to constrain the strength and structure of the magnetic field associated with the environment of the radio source 3C 449, using observations of Faraday rotation, which we model with a structure function technique and by comparison with numerical simulations. We assume that the magnetic field is a Gaussian, isotropic random variable and that it is embedded in the hot intra-group plasma surrounding the radio source. For this purpose, we present detailed rotation measure images for the polarized radio source 3C 449, previously observed with the Very Large Array at seven frequencies between 1.365 and 8.385 GHz. All of the observations are consistent with pure foreground Faraday rotation. We quantify the statistics of the magnetic-field fluctuations by deriving rotation measure structure functions, which we fit using models derived from theoretical power spectra.

sources.summary: The appendix describes 3C 449 as a tentative double-horn CO source whose HST image shows inclined dust absorption and a torus or disk morphology.

quotes.quote: 3C 449: It has a tentative double horn profile that can be noticed from the CO(1-0) spectrum (Figure onlineSpectra ), but one side is stronger than the other one. From the HST image of this galaxy it is possible to see the dust absorbing the visible light, not completely edge on. According to the morphology of this galaxy suggests that we are viewing the near side of an inclined, geometrically thick torus or disk. The velocity width is about 500 km/s.

sources.summary: 3C 449 has bright ISM and IGM, FR I plumed radio lobes near the intragroup medium edge, and an AGN spectrum fit with power-law plus thermal components.

quotes.quote: 3C 449 ( z=0.017 ) : Both the ISM and IGM are bright in this 30 ks exposure, but the chip is somewhat smaller than the extent of the radio lobes. Notably, these lobes decollimate and show the characteristic FR I plumed structure near the edge of the intragroup medium; this structure and the hot atmospheres have been studied with XMM-Newton in detail by croston03 . tremblay07 find that the jet is parallel to a warped nuclear disk. The AGN was fit with the XSPEC models N_H (PL)+ apec , but the an acceptable fit was also found for a PL+ apec fit with no absorption and a smaller .

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 449's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 449 is a FR I radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy UGC 12064 at a redshift of z= 0.0171 ( D 72.4 Mpc), the southern component of a close pair of galaxies. It contains an active nucleus with optical LINER classification buttiglione_optical_2009,buttiglione_optical_2010 . 3C 449 features supergalactic-scale twisted twin jets along the north-south axis (PA 9 ; perley_structure_1979,feretti_vla_1999 ) and a nuclear dust disc 1.5 kpc in diameter (PA 155 ; martel_hubble_1999,tremblay_warped_2006 ).

sources.summary: The FRI radio galaxy 3C 449 has PSPC and HRI fits consistent with extended emission shaped by displacement of hot cluster gas by radio plasma.

quotes.quote: 3C 449 The PSPC observations of this FRI radio galaxy are reported in Hardcastle \ (1998c). The structure of the extended emission is poorly constrained by a more recent 19-ks HRI observation, but no point sources are detected which might have conspired to produce the ring-like structure seen around the southern radio lobe in the lower resolution PSPC observations, reinforcing the conclusion that this is caused by a displacement of the hot cluster gas by the radio-emitting plasma. The HRI and PSPC radial profile fits are consistent, and so we tabulate the PSPC results in Table profile , as the statistics are slightly better.

gmw_00000069.name: 3C 452
gmw_00000069.overview: The active galactic nucleus 3C 452 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:1305.3622, arXiv:1310.2770] identified with an elliptical host galaxy [arXiv:1310.2770] and classified optically as a Seyfert 2 [arXiv:1310.2770]. It features biconical radio lobes connected to the nucleus by a twin jet [arXiv:1310.2770], with nonthermal emission dominating near its radio hot spots [arXiv:0912.3001].

sources.summary: The paper accounts for 3C 452 jet-related soft emission, finds imperfect model agreement, and classifies the AGN as requiring line-of-sight N_H variability.

quotes.quote: 3C 452 Data reduction/fitting: We used an annulus of radii 12 20 to extract the background, in order to avoid a nearby source. 3C 452 also shows diffuse, soft, (very) extended emission, which is coming from a jet see Isobe2002 . Given how the extraction region used by is smaller than that of and , when including the jet emission it is necessary to use different jet normalization (i.e. the variation of the parameter norm _ jet does not imply that the jet is varying in flux). This source did not require any cross-normalization for the AGN emission, with the exception of that associated to the jet.

Analysis of results: The data is well-fitted by all models, even if they are not in perfect agreement. yields a significantly smaller value, and the determinations of _ Obs by and are incompatible within errors. favors an edge-on scenario, while favors a face-on one, although with very large errors. Additionally, results in a much harder spectrum for the jet emission, when compared to and . Our results for are compatible with those obtained by Zhao2020 , with the exception of _ Obs , which in their case results in a face-on scenario. Additionally, Zhao2020 introduce some AGN flux variability, which in our case is modeled via changes in the normalization of jet flux. All models agree that N_ H,los variability is required to explain the data, but yields smaller values for all observations. We thus classify this source as ` N_ H,los variable'.

sources.summary: For 3C 452, thermal and power-law emission are mixed across the radio lobes; the authors synthesize thermal emission and find nonthermal emission near hot spots.

quotes.quote: 3C 452 ( z=0.081 ) : This data set was studied in detail by isobe02 who find that the thermal and power law emission is well mixed throughout the region covered by the radio lobes. We attempt to isolate the two components using an analysis similar to diehl07 in which the image is broken into hard and soft components. The thermal and power law emission each make up a certain percentage of the luminosity in each component, so a synthesized image of the thermal emission can be constructed by adding the correct percentage of each component to the final image. Although clearly the method will not be able to identify individual counts as thermal or nonthermal in origin, if the spatial distribution of nonthermal counts is significantly different from that of thermal ones, we would expect to see a difference in the synthesized images. In fact, we find general agreement with isobe02 , although the nonthermal emission is dominant near the hot spots of the radio galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 452 as the only firm Compton-thick candidate in its sample, with strong absorption, reflection, low scattering fraction, and hidden-AGN properties.

quotes.quote: The first two targets, J2246.0+3941 and J0407.4+0339, are well-known 3C catalog FRII Radio Galaxies, and we refer to them as 3C 452 and 3C 105, while J0318.7+6828 is an FRII Giant Radio Galaxy (GRG) with a linear size of 2 Mpc. Optical spectra, available for all the sources, identify our targets as Type II AGN. The last, and farthest, J0918.5+0425 has been identified as an AGN for the first time by the nine-month Swift/BAT survey, but was then not detected in the 4.8 cut of the 22-month survey, most likely because of variability ( tue10 ).

Of these, 3C 452 is the only certain Compton-thick AGN candidate because of four properties: The source spectrum shows both high absorption (N _ H 4 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ) and strong Compton reflection, which also flattens the intrinsic power-law photon index. The relative strength of the reflection is consistent with a complex absorber where a large portion of the subtended solid angle is composed by gas that is optically thicker than the amount crossed along the line of sight; The XIS and PIN light curves show no significant short-term variability, and the spectral parameters of the source stay the same during six years.

sources.summary: The paper notes that 3C 452 is a complex source with a very flat spectrum, better fit by reflection and previously classified as Compton-thick.

quotes.quote: For the complex sources, we exclude 3C 452 whose flat spectrum is best characterized by reflection 2006ApJ...642...96E .
However, we do detect sources classified as Compton-thick in other studies based on a reflection dominated spectrum or strong Fe K EW (for instance 3C 452 and NGC 4945).

3C 452: 3C 452 has an extremely flat spectrum which is much better fit with the addition of a reflection model 2006ApJ...642...96E . The values quoted are from our own analysis of the data with a simplistic model.

sources.summary: The paper includes 3C 452 in its broadband spectral fitting, reports best-fit photon-index and absorption values, and attributes discrepant XMM results to variability.

quotes.quote: We simultaneously fit the broadband spectra of each object taken with Suzaku /XIS-FI (0.5--10 keV), Suzaku /XIS-BI (0.5--9 keV), Suzaku /HXD-PIN (16--40 keV), and NuSTAR /FPMs (8--66 keV; for the sources for which it was possible to have the widest spectral coverage) We do not include the HXD-PIN spectra for PKS 0326--288 and 3C 452 in the fitting, considering the poor signal-to-noise ratios and possible contamination by nearby sources, respectively.

3C 452 Our best-fit values, = 1.61_ 0.07 ^ +0.06 and N_ H ^ LOS = 43.39_ 5.78 ^ +2.24 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , are consistent with Fioretti13 , who obtained =1.55_ 0.11 ^ +0.14 and N_ H ^ LOS = 43.52_ 6.92 ^ +10.85 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 using Suzaku and Swift /BAT data. analyzed the same NuSTAR spectra as ours and derived N_ H ^ LOS =46.8 2.3 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 with the pexrav model by fixing at 1.73. This value is consistent with ours.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 452's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 452 is a FR II radio source identified with the elliptical galaxy LEDA 69671 martel_hubble_1999 at a redshift of z= 0.0811 ( D 378 Mpc). It contains an AGN with optical Sy 2 classification veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 that belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. The radio morphology is typical for a FR II source, with the supergalactic-scale biconical radio lobes connected to the nucleus by a twin jet along the east-west axis (PA 75 ; e.g., black_study_1992 ).

gmw_00000070.name: 3C 459
gmw_00000070.overview: 3C 459 is an FR II radio galaxy [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:0712.0595] with a heavily distorted host galaxy [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1310.2770] that shows evidence of a recent merger [arXiv:1210.6027, arXiv:0712.0595]. The system features a dual active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1903.10768] and hosts fast, ionized gas outflows [arXiv:1201.1503].

sources.summary: The paper includes 3C 459 among nearby radio galaxies with fast ionized and atomic outflows, warm turbulent H2 emission, and blue-shifted mid-infrared line wings linked to jet feedback.

quotes.quote: We present mid-IR Spitzer spectroscopy of 8 nearby radio-galaxies where fast outflows of ionized and atomic gas were detected by previous optical and radio observations. Our main results and conclusions are the following: In all of the sources, we detect high equivalent width H _2 line emission from warm (100-5000 K) molecular gas, implying warm H _2 masses ranging from 10^8 to 10^ 10 M _ . The observed H _2 to-PAH luminosity ratios are all above the values predicted from photo-ionization models, and we suggest that the H _2 emission is associated with the dissipation of a fraction of the kinetic energy provided by the radio jet.

In 5 sources (3C 236, 3C 293, 3C 459, 4C 12.50 and PKS 1549-79), we securely detect blue-shifted wings (up to 3000 km s ^ 1 ) on the [ Ne ii ] (and [ Ne iii ] for the highest SNR spectra) that matches remarkably well with the blue-shifted, broad H i absorption associated with the outflow. All but one of these sources have resolved and very broad mid-IR rotational H _2 lines with FWHM 500 km s ^ 1 (and up to 900 km s ^ 1 ), compared to only 2 overall amongst the 298 AGN with such spectra in the Spitzer archive Dasyra2011 .

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 459 as an FR II broad-line radio galaxy with a young stellar host, merger-like infrared structure, asymmetric compact radio emission, and eastern-lobe X-rays.

quotes.quote: 3C 459 is an FR II broad line radio galaxy. The host galaxy is dominated by a young stellar population tadhunter02 and its IR structure has been suggested to be the result of a recent merger floyd08 . The radio source is small and very asymmetric morganti99 . The high resolution radio map morganti93 reveals a weak western component; see Figure fig:3c459app . The extended X-ray emission around the nucleus is mostly on the E side, coincident with the E radio lobe.

sources.summary: The paper summarizes 3C 459 as a dual-AGN system with diffuse nuclear emission, an 80 kpc filamentary region, and two compact knots with distinct line properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 459: FR II/BLO, 1 = 3.58 kpc. We presented the results obtained for this source in balmaverde18 . We detected diffuse nuclear emission and a filamentary ionized gas structure forming a one sided triangular-shaped region extending out to 80 kpc. The central emission line region is dominated by two compact knots of similar surface brightness separated by 1 2 (5.3 kpc). Based on the dramatic differences in velocity, line widths, and line ratios, we argued that we are observing a dual AGN system, formed by a radio-loud AGN and a type 2 QSO companion.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2314+03 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This NLRG/FRII with strong evidence for a YSP at both optical Wills08 and MIR/FIR wavelengths Dicken09,Dicken10 , is classified as ULIRG based on its FIR luminosity, and appears clearly disturbed in our GMOS-S image (Figure pks2314_online ). We detect two broad and symmetrical fans of _V =23.6 (South) and 23.3 (East) mag arcsec ^ 2 , extending to a maximum distance of 33 kpc to the South of the nucleus, and giving an overall butterfly-like appearance. The southern fan shows some bright knots. Pointing to the North, we detect a faint tail in our processed images.

sources.summary: The atlas describes 3C 459 as an FR II radio galaxy with a strong infrared nucleus, heavily distorted off-center host, young stellar population, recent merger, and asymmetric compact radio source.

quotes.quote: 3C 459; z=0.219 A strong IR nucleus, and heavily distorted off-center host galaxy distinguish this FR II radio galaxy. The host is dominated by young stellar population tadhunter+00 , and must have undergone a recent merger. The radio source is small (10 ) and also very asymmetric morganti+99 .

sources.summary: The atlas gives 3C 459's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 3C 459 is a FR II radio source identified with the infrared ultra-luminous, disturbed early-type galaxy LEDA 70899 zheng_hst_1999 at a redshift of z= 0.2201 ( D 1125 Mpc). Its optical classification is controversial in the literature: it is classified as a type I AGN by , based on which presumably classified it as a Sy 1, while , , and do not find evidence for broad lines and, thus, classify it as a type II object. Finally, classify it as a LINER.

gmw_00000071.name: 4C +73.08
gmw_00000071.overview: The radio galaxy 4C +73.08 is a low-excitation FR II source [arXiv:astro-ph/9907034, arXiv:1310.2770] with a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1310.2770]. It features megaparsec-scale radio lobes [arXiv:1310.2770] enveloped by an extended cocoon of older plasma [arXiv:2003.09183], displaying a peculiar X-shaped morphology [arXiv:1604.05142].

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C 73.08 as an X-shaped radio galaxy near the FRI/FRII boundary, with distorted lobes and structure consistent with motion and precession.

quotes.quote: GRG J0949+7314 The radio galaxy 4C 73.08 was first studied by Mayer (1979), who noted that its luminosity is in the boundary region between FRI and FRII class sources. Also its X-shaped morphology does not naturally fit into either FR class. Basing on the 327, 608.5, and 1412 MHz radio total intensity and linear polarization maps J agers (1986) concluded that the peculiar structure of this source can be explained as a distortion of a linear radio galaxy due to the motion of the parent galaxy combined with the precessional motion of the nucleus. Strom et al. (2013) observed this source with the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope at 1381.1 MHz. In their deep map, a double-lobed structure can be clearly seen, with broad components that are nearly as wide as long.

sources.summary: 4C 73.08 is shown with combined interferometric and single-dish imaging to have extended cocoon emission around its Mpc lobes, implying older plasma surrounding refueled hotspots.

quotes.quote: 4C 73.08: thanks to a combination of interferometric and single-dish observations at 1.4 GHz, found an extended radio emission enveloping the whole Mpc-scale structure of the lobes. The map obtained in that work is presented in Fig. maps1 (middle-left panel). This is in agreement with , who proposed that the hotspots visible in the lobes of this source are more recent, refueled elements, surrounded by an older radio structure. We note that provides a dynamical age for the source lobes between 180 and 230 Myr, implying a much older age for the cocoon emission.

sources.summary: The low-excitation FRII source 4C 73.08 lies in a crowded X-ray field with weak extended emission near its optical position but insufficient counts for profile fitting.

quotes.quote: 4C 73.08 This low-excitation FRII source is in a crowded X-ray field. The optical position is coincident with a weak extended X-ray source, but there are too few counts for adequate radial profile fitting. We take the counts in a 2-arcmin source circle as an upper limit to point-like emission.

sources.summary: The atlas gives 4C +73.08's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: 4C +73.08 is a FR II radio source identified with the compact galaxy VII Zw 292 mayer_multifrequency_1979 at a redshift of z= 0.0581 ( D 271 Mpc) with a little-studied AGN. We treat the AGN as Sy 2, based on the existence of only narrow emission lines saunders_spectrophotometry_1989 . It features megaparsec-scale radio lobes (PA 65 ; mayer_multifrequency_1979 ).

sources.summary: The paper notes 4C 73.08 lacks IRS data, has unusually high X-ray-column extinction compared with near-infrared estimates, and mixed agreement among extinction methods.

quotes.quote: Note that no IRS data are available for 3C 277.3 and 4C 73.08.

The optical extinctions based in the X-ray column density using the standard dust to gas ratio Bohlin:1978 , are considerably higher than the optical extinctions estimated from the near-IR SED and X-ray luminosity: 2 to 8 times higher in most cases (even up to 15 and 26 times for 3C 321 and 4C 73.08, respectively).
On the other hand, although 4C 73.08 is located at the right side of Fig. histogram_all a, the optical extinction based in the two techniques agree within 1 .

gmw_00000072.name: Ark 120
gmw_00000072.overview: Ark 120 is a nearby, radio-quiet Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1703.06432, arXiv:1705.07781] located approximately 142 megaparsecs away [arXiv:2002.12348]. It is classified as a "bare" active galactic nucleus due to its lack of intrinsic, line-of-sight X-ray absorption [arXiv:1703.06432, arXiv:1904.07341].

sources.summary: Ark120 is the more challenging local AGN test case; the paper reconstructs its H light curve, measures a roughly 48-day lag, and derives a virial black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: Results on Ark 120 Implications from the spectrum Ark120 lies at redshift z = 0.0327 so that the H line falls into the NB 5007 30 filter. Compared to the case of PG0003+199, the observations of Ark 120 are more challenging: Firstly, compared to H , the H line is fainter contributing only to 50 of the flux in the NB band pass (Fig. fig_spectrum_ark120 ). In order to measure the continuum variations underneath the H line, light curves using neighbouring off-line intermediate-bands would be ideal but such bands were not available. While the V band covers H and the Fe complex, it is dominated to 83 3 by the AGN and host continuum (calculated using the transmission curve of filter and CCD shown in Fig. fig_transmission ) and therefore we used the V band, in addition to B band and NB 5007 .

Fig. fig_dcf_ark120 shows the cross correlation results applying in a first step the DCF to the entire light curves. The DCF of B and NB exhibits two distinct major peaks, one at a lag of 47.5 days and one at a lag of about zero days. Because the cross correlation of B and V yields a lag of 1.2 ^ +1.6 _ 2.6 days consistent with lag zero, we conclude that the lag zero peak in the DCF of B and NB comes from the continuum contribution contained in the NB. This contribution appears here quasi in autocorrelation, while the lag of 47.5 days is due to the line echo.

sources.summary: The paper uses spectropolarimetry of Akn 120 to infer Faraday depolarization, broad-line absorption/turbulence, inclination, black-hole mass, and magnetic-field behavior in its accretion disk.

quotes.quote: According to Smith et al. (2002) the mean polarization degree in the continuum of Akn 120 is equal to p_ 0.35 , and is equal to 0.4 in the H line emission. We use here the data obtained by Smith et al. (2002) in 1998 October. The mean observed position angle have the same value for the continuum and line emission 76^ . The inclination angle i=48^ , 0.67 . Its value has been derived by Braatz Gugliucci (2008) from water maser observations.

The central black hole mass in Akn 120 is equal to M_ BH 10^ 7.74 M_ (see Peterson et al. 2004). For the inclination angle i=48^ the polarization in the continuum from the accretion disc without magnetic field is expected at the level p_c( )=1.26 (Chandrasekhar 1960). This value is higher than the observed polarization degree and it means that the Faraday depolarization effect is really acting.

sources.summary: The paper reports six months of UV and X-ray monitoring of Ark 120, finding substantial variability, spectral steepening when brighter, and delays favoring disk reprocessing.

quotes.quote: We report the results of a six month \ monitoring campaign of Ark 120, a prototypical ``bare" Seyfert 1 galaxy. The lack of intrinsic absorption combined with the nearly contemporaneous coverage of the UV, and X-ray bands makes it possible to investigate the link between the accretion disk and the putative Comptonization corona. Our observations confirm the presence of substantial temporal variability, with the X-ray characterized by large-amplitude flux changes on timescales of few days, while the variations in the UV bands are smoother and occur on timescales of several weeks. The source also shows spectral variability with the X-ray spectrum steepening when the source is brighter.

We do not detect any correlation between the UV flux and the X-ray spectral slope. A cross correlation analysis suggests positive delays between X-rays and the UV emission, favoring a scenario of disk reprocessing. Although the strength of the correlation is moderate with a delay that is not well constrained ( 7.5 7 days), it is nevertheless indicative of a very large disk reprocessing region, with a separation between the X-ray and the UV emitting regions which could be as large as 1000 r_G . The Ark 120 correlation results are in agreement with those obtained in similar multi-wavelength monitoring studies of AGN.

sources.summary: The paper studies Ark 120 as a nearby radio-quiet AGN, finding four decades of optical and spectroscopic variability, a possible 20-year period, asymmetric H profiles, and possible SMBH-binary relevance.

quotes.quote: We study the long-term variability in the optical monitoring database of Ark 120, a nearby radio-quiet active galactic nucleus (AGN) at a distance of 143 Mpc ( z=0.03271 ). We compiled the historical archival photometric and spectroscopic data since 1974 and conducted a new two-year monitoring campaign in 2015-2017, resulting in a total temporal baseline over four decades. The long-term variations in the optical continuum exhibit a wave-like pattern and the H integrated flux series varies with a similar behaviour. The broad H profiles have asymmetric double peaks, which change strongly with time and tend to merge into a single peak during some epochs. The period in the optical continuum determined from various period-search methods is about 20 years and the estimated false alarm probability with null hypothesis simulations is about 1 10^ 3 .

The overall variations of the broad H profiles also follow the same period. However, the present database only covers two cycles of the suggested period , which strongly encourages continued monitoring to track more cycles and confirm the periodicity. Nevertheless, in light of the possible periodicity and the complicated H profile, Ark 120 is one candidate of the nearest radio-quiet AGNs with possible periodic variability and it is thereby a potential candidate host for a sub-parsec supermassive black hole binary.

sources.summary: The paper models Arakelian 120 accretion geometry, concluding its UV-dominated SED makes hard-X-ray reprocessing too weak and that most UV variability is intrinsic.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We explore the accretion geometry in Arakelian 120 using intensive UV and X-ray monitoring from Swift . The hard X-rays ( 1-10  keV) show large amplitude, fast (few-day) variability, so we expect reverberation from the disc to produce UV variability from the varying hard X-ray illumination. We model the spectral energy distribution including an outer standard disc (optical), an intermediate warm Comptonisation region (UV and soft X-ray) and a hot corona (hard X-rays). Unlike the lower Eddington fraction AGN (NGC 4151 and NGC 5548 at L/L_ Edd 0.02 and 0.03 respectively), the SED of Akn 120 ( L 0.05L_ Edd ) is dominated by the UV, restricting the impact of reverberating hard X-rays just from energetics.

This is in contrast to the results shown here for Ark 120. This AGN has L/L_ Edd 0.05 , and its SED is dominated by a peak in the UV which is larger than the hard X-ray component. The X-rays are therefore not energetically dominant. The X-ray reprocessed variability is thus incapable of constraining the accretion disc structure directly as its impact on the UV lightcurve is negligible.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ark 120 as a bare Seyfert 1 control case for wavelet timing, describing its smooth X-ray spectrum and noise-consistent wavelet power spectrum.

quotes.quote: ARK 120 An observation of the `bare' Seyfert 1 galaxy Ark 120 ( z = 0.03271 ) ark120paper (Obs ID: 0147190101) is examined in the 0.3 - 10 keV energy band. The X-ray spectrum of Ark 120 is unremarkable, exhibiting a smooth soft excess above a harder power law, and a narrow Fe K emission line ( nardini2011reflection_ark120_softexcess , nandi2021long_ark120_softexcess ). There is no observed warm absorber, hence it is labeled as `bare'.

Ark 120 serves as a good control test for our wavelet analysis. The object is very bright ( 30 counts per second), and shows low variability (lower panel of Fig. fig:ark_120_cwt ), compared to most other Seyfert 1s. This is consistent with work showing the excess variance, which is a measure of variability, is significantly (linearly) anti-correlated with black hole mass lu2001_excessvariance_BHmass . The measured PSD is very flat ( 0.96 ) at frequencies not dominated by the Poisson noise. In Fig. fig:ark_120_cwt , the wavelet spectrum appears completely consistent with a flat noise spectrum (e.g. compare to top panel of Fig. fig:noise_cwts ) with no obvious frequencies of importance.

sources.summary: The paper describes Arakelian 120 as a luminous, radio-quiet Seyfert 1 whose bare, lightly absorbed nucleus reveals a broad iron line and smooth soft X-ray excess.

quotes.quote: Arakelian 120 (aka Mrk 1095) is a luminous Seyfert 1 galaxy at a redshift z=0.0323 . It was the subject of an early attempt at reverberation mapping, and was important in demonstrating the compact size of the optical broad line region (Peterson 1985; Peterson Gaskell 1991). More recent optical monitoring campaigns have yielded an estimate of the mass of the central black hole of 2 10^ 8   (Wandel, Peterson Malkan 1999).

The nucleus is radio-quiet but shows a slight extension in its radio image (Condon 1998; Ho 2002). Ward (1987) used broad-band photometry to identify \ as a `bare' Seyfert nucleus, i.e. one free from significant reddening or contamination from the host galaxy. The host is a low-inclination spiral galaxy (Hubble type S0/a, inclination i 26 ; Nordgren 1995) .

The spectrum shows a Doppler broadened iron emission line ( FWHM 3 10^4  km s ^ 1 ) and a smooth, continuous soft excess which appears to peak at an energy 0.5  keV. This adds weight to the claim that genuine soft excesses (i.e. those due to a real steepening of the underlying continuum below 2  keV) are ubiquitous in Seyfert 1 spectra.
