sources.summary: The paper revisits ARK 120 XMM-Newton spectra, noting a previously reported blue-shifted Fe K line and arguing the claimed non-detection missed the relevant energy range.

quotes.quote: ARK 120 Laha et al. (2014) analyzed the spectrum of one XMM-Newton observation of ARK 120 (OBSID 0147190101). Tombesi et al. (2010a) reported the detection of a blue-shifted Fe K absorption line at the observed energy of E = 8.89 0.03  keV. In their eye-inspection of the Fe K band in Fig. A6, Laha et al. (2014) claim the non-detection of such feature.

However, we note that this might simply be due to the fact that the feature is outside of the energy band considered of E = 6--8.5 keV. Anyway, the broad-band model assumed by Laha et al. (2014) consists of a power-law continuum with = 1.97, two black-body components to model the soft excess with temperatures kT = 102 eV and kT = 240 eV (see their Table 5). They also include a soft X-ray emission line at the energy E = 0.554 keV and a pexmon reflection component with R = 0.75. They do not report a significant detection of a warm absorber in this source (see their Table 6).

sources.summary: Ark 120 is the Seyfert comparison object in the Suzaku matched-filter test, with no significant absorption features and a well-fit power-law spectrum.

quotes.quote: We first apply it to the famous AGNs Mrk 766 and Ark 120, and examine the existence of Ultra-fast Outflows (UFOs), an AGN outflow with a velocity close to the speed of light. The observational evidence of UFOs relies on highly blue-shifted absorption lines mostly found around the Fe K band Tombesi2010 . The features are suitable to test and verify if the MC method is applicable to Suzaku data.
A simple power-law model was chosen for Ark 120. The spectrum is well-reproduced by a power law with photon index = 1.93 0.02 .

Two absorption lines at 6.7 keV and 7.0 keV, which are reported as blue-shifted He-like and H-like Fe absorption lines in Mrk 766 Gofford2013 , are significantly detected at >4 confidence, as shown in the bottom-left panel in Figure 2. Meanwhile, we could not detect any absorption lines with more than 3 significance from Ark 120. These results are consistent with Gofford2013 .

sources.summary: The paper reports reverberation mapping of Ark 120, noting its extreme red asymmetry, possible binary interpretation, double-peaked rms spectrum, and complicated velocity-resolved lags.

quotes.quote: As the first paper of the series, we provide here our RM measurements of 4 AGNs: 3C 120, Ark 120, Mrk 6, and SBS 1518+593 (see Table tab:obj for their coordinates and some general information). Their luminosities place them among Seyfert galaxies and broad line radio galaxies rather than the more energetic quasar category. Ark 120 and Mrk 6 show extreme red asymmetric profiles, while 3C 120 and SBS 1518+593 show milder red asymmetries. None are extreme super-Eddington accretors, although 3C 120 and SBS 1518+593 have dimensionless accretion rates on order unity see also sec:BHmass du2015 . The light curves of all these sources have shown unambiguous peaks or dips, and are sufficient for us to measure \ time lags.

During our campaign, the mean spectrum of Ark 120 was not significantly double-peaked, but the rms spectrum shows clearly two peaks. One is located at roughly 4861 \ in the rest frame, and the other is strongly redshifted by 2500 km s ^ 1 .
Our velocity-resolved lag measurement of Ark 120 is also complicated.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 120 as a bare AGN enabling disc-Comptonisation spin constraints, finding Comptonisation-dominated X-rays, spin near 0.83, and a shrinking corona between epochs.

quotes.quote: Therefore, AGN showing no (or very weak) intrinsic absorption along the line-of-sight such as Ark 120, a so-called bare AGN, are the ideal targets for SMBH spin measurements. However, in our previous work on Ark 120, we found that its 2014 X-ray spectrum is dominated by Comptonisation, while the relativistic reflection emission only originates at tens of gravitational radii from the SMBH. As a result, we could not constrain the SMBH spin from disc reflection alone. Our aim is to determine the SMBH spin in Ark 120 from an alternative technique based on the global energetics of the disc-corona system.

We infer a SMBH spin of 0.83 ^ +0.05 _ 0.03 , adopting the SMBH reverberation mass of 1.50 10 ^ 8 M _ . In addition, we find that the coronal radius decreases with increasing flux (by about a factor of two), from 85 ^ +13 _ 10 R_ g in 2013 to 14 3 R_ g in 2014. This is the first time that such a constraint is obtained for a SMBH spin from this technique, thanks to the bare properties of Ark 120, its well determined SMBH reverberation mass, and the presence of a mild relativistic reflection component in 2014 which allows us to constrain the disc inclination angle.

sources.summary: The paper monitors the nearby Seyfert Ark 120 across X-ray, UV, and optical bands, finding larger-than-predicted lags that imply an oversized accretion disc.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we focus on Ark 120, a nearby Seyfert galaxy ( z = 0.0327 ; OsterbrockPhillips77 ). Assuming standard cosmological parameters (i.e. H_ 0 = 71 km s ^ 1 ; _ = 0.73 ; _ M = 0.27 ), Ark 120 resides at a distance of 142 Mpc.

In the case of our Ark 120 campaign, one might expect such a scenario to moderately impact the UVW1 band (2 600 ) but less so the B , V , and I bands --- however, we detect larger-than-predicted lags in all of these bands relative to the X-rays. As such, it may be the case that Ark 120 is another example of an AGN whose accretion disc appears to exist on a larger scale than predicted by the standard thin-disc model. Given our detection here of larger-than-expected X-ray-to-UV lags, Ark 120 remains an appropriate target for a more comprehensive monitoring campaign (i.e. with Swift ) in order to sample the multiwavelength variability more extensively flux and in time. To summarize, we have analyzed a multiwavelength monitoring campaign of Ark 120 --- a bright, nearby Seyfert galaxy. We used co-ordinated observations using the Swift satellite ( 50 days) and a series of ground-based observatories, covering roughly 4 months, and primarily using the Skynet Robotic Telescope Network .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Ark 120 with XMM-Newton, NuSTAR, Swift, and RXTE, finding soft-band variability, variable Fe K emission, a steep power spectrum, and a first detected soft X-ray lag.

quotes.quote: We present the spectral/timing properties of the bare Seyfert galaxy Ark 120 through a deep 420 ks XMM-Newton campaign plus recent NuSTAR observations and a 6-month Swift monitoring campaign. We investigate the spectral decomposition through fractional rms, covariance and difference spectra, finding the mid- to long-timescale ( day--year) variability to be dominated by a relatively smooth, steep component, peaking in the soft X-ray band. Additionally, we find evidence for variable Fe K emission red-ward of the Fe K core on long timescales, consistent with previous findings.

We detect a clearly-defined power spectrum which we model with a power law with a slope of 1.9 . By extending the power spectrum to lower frequencies through the inclusion of Swift and RXTE data, we find tentative evidence of a high-frequency break, consistent with existing scaling relations. We also explore frequency-dependent Fourier time lags, detecting a negative (`soft') lag for the first time in this source with the 0.3--1 keV band lagging behind the 1--4 keV band with a time delay, , of 900 s.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 120 as a bare Seyfert 1 galaxy with prominent soft X-ray excess, no ionized absorption, and broadband observations favoring an additional Comptonizing component.

quotes.quote: Ark 120 (a.k.a. Mrk 1095, z =0.0327) is an object where the soft X-ray emission is prominent (Vaughan et al. 2004). It is a Broad Line (H FWHM of 5800 km s ^ 1 , Wandel et al. 1999) Seyfert 1 galaxy, with an estimated black hole mass of 1.5 10 ^8 M _ (Peterson et al. 2004). It has been observed extensively in X-rays (see Nardini et al. 2011 and references therein), always showing a strong soft excess and never showing a Warm Absorber - it is indeed often referred to as a `bare' Seyfert 1.

No ionized absorption is apparent, either in the EPIC or in the RGS data, confirming the `bare' Seyfert 1 nature of Ark 120. The 0.3-80 keV spectral energy range provided by the quasi-simultaneous XMM- Newton / NuSTAR observations of the `bare' Seyfert 1 galaxy Ark 120 has allowed us to study the soft X-ray excess in this source with unprecedented accuracy and robustness.

sources.summary: Ark 120 has an unexpectedly straight primary PCA component despite a large soft excess, suggesting correlated soft-excess and continuum emission plus a possible distant reflection component.

quotes.quote: Ark 120 We find an extremely straight primary component from the PCA of Ark 120, which is unexpected given the extremely large soft excess shown by this source. This implies that the origin of the soft excess must be strongly correlated with the primary power law emission, and suggests a different origin for the soft excess in this source and the sources in mcg6objects and Ark564section . The gradual decrease with energy implies, as in 3C 273, that there is a relatively constant component which increases with flux but without any strong broad features. This could potentially be explained by a distant, neutral reflection component, and we note the possible narrow features around 6.4 keV in the primary PC. Nardini11_2 found that the Suzaku spectrum of Ark 120 was well described by a blurred reflection model.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Ark 120 as a bare Seyfert 1 galaxy whose X-ray variability is driven by hot-corona spectral changes and warm-corona luminosity variations.

quotes.quote: We present results from a detailed spectral-timing analysis of a long 486 observation of the bare Seyfert 1 galaxy Ark 120 which showed alternating diminution and increment in the 0.3 10 X-ray flux over four consecutive orbits in 2014. We study the energy-dependent variability of Ark 120 through broad-band X-ray spectroscopy, fractional root-mean-squared (rms) spectral modelling, hardness intensity diagram and flux flux analysis. The X-ray (0.3 10 ) spectra are well fitted by a thermally Comptonized primary continuum with two (blurred and distant) reflection components and an optically thick, warm Comptonization component for the soft X-ray excess emission below 2 .

Ark 120 is a broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy ve06 at a redshift of z=0.0327 th05 with Balmer lines full width at half-maximum FWHM(H _ ) 5800  km s ^ 1 wa99 , an estimated black hole mass of M_ BH 1.5 10^ 8 M_ pe04 and low Eddington ratio of L_ bol /L_ E 0.05 vas07 . The source is well known for showing a strong soft excess ma14 , a deficit of intrinsic ultraviolet/X-ray ionized absorption cr01,va04 and no significant contamination from the host galaxy wa87 therefore considered as a `bare' nucleus and an ideal target to shed light on the central engine.

sources.summary: The paper says Ark 120 has a high-velocity UFO whose possible Ly counterpart falls in a difficult FUSE wavelength region dominated by foreground absorption and airglow.

quotes.quote: The high velocity of the UFO in Ark 120 places its potential Ly counterpart near the shortest wavelengths observable with FUSE. Although this spectrum appears to be of poor quality, most of the ``noise" one sees in Figure fig:1b is due to the plethora of foreground interstellar absorption features present at short UV wavelengths. These are marked in red in the figure. Strong Ly and O 1 airglow are also present at 948 \ and 950 . These features and the interstellar absorption lines make it difficult to set a sensitive upper limit on Ly absorption in this spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper finds Ark 120 has a slightly skewed dual-horn H I profile and favors its broader, higher-quality measurements over low-S/N Nancay values.

quotes.quote: Ark 120: The profile of Ark 120 has a slightly skewed dual-horn shape (see Fig. \ batmen2 ). conducted an observation using the Nan cay 94 m telescope with an 8,192 channel autocorrelation spectrometer, which resulted in an HI detection with S/N=3.8. The low S/N in the observations would make it difficult to fully characterize the weaker, blueshifted side of the line profile, and likely accounts for the discrepancies between their reported measurements and ours. Where they find values of W _ 50 =194 33 km s ^ 1 , W _ 20 =233 50 km s ^ 1 , and V _ R =9807 17 km s ^ 1 , we report values of W _ 50 =337.3 ^ +17.0 _ 13.1 km s ^ 1 , W _ 20 =344.4 ^ +18.4 _ 17.9 km s ^ 1 , and V _ R =9806.38 ^ +9.22 _ 4.51 km s ^ 1 . On the other hand, report values of W _ 20 =370.3 6.8 km s ^ 1 and V _ R =9809.2 3.4 km s ^ 1 that are fairly consistent with our measurements within the uncertainties.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 120 as a nearby, well-studied Seyfert 1 with little X-ray absorption and three iron-band line components, one of them broad.

quotes.quote: Ark 120 Ark 120 is a nearby Seyert 1 galaxy e.g. osterbrock77 that is well-studied in the X-ray band. This source shows little or no evidence for X-ray absorption e.g. reeves16 . Previous spectral analysis of Ark 120 shows evidence for three line components in the iron band nardini16 . Two of the three line components are narrow emission lines, corresponding to a neutral Fe K emission line and an ionized Fe K emission line. The third line component is broader with FWHM 5000 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper highlights Ark 120 as a nearby AGN with long Swift and ground-based X-ray/optical lags, tied to its relatively large black-hole mass and slow variability.

quotes.quote: In addition, we include results from other known multiwavelength monitoring campaigns, including NGC 3783 Arevalo09,Lira11 , NGC 4051 Breedt10 , MR 2251 178 Lira11 , MCG 6 30 15 Lira15 , Mrk 817 Kara21 , and Ark 120, which may have one of the longest published time delays from a nearby AGN to date Lobban20 . With Swift , the V band (5 468 ) is observed to lag behind the X-rays with = 11.90 7.33 d, while additional ground-based monitoring with the Skynet Robotic Telescope Network https://skynet.unc.edu/ revealed a long delay between the X-rays and the i band (8 797 ) of = 12.34 4.83 d. Ark 120 has a larger black hole mass than most sources targeted by these campaigns ( M_ BH = 1.5 0.2 10^ 8 M_ ; Peterson04 ). In larger-mass systems, the spatial separation between regions that emit in two given wavelength bands is expected to be larger -- as such, for a given wavelength gap, the time delay is expected to be longer, up to the 12-d limit stated in equation eq:12-day . However, the slower variability of such larger-mass systems also requires that much longer observing campaigns are required to obtain robust measurements, hence the large uncertainties on these values.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 120 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: Ark 120 is a nearby (z=0.0327) Seyfert 1 AGN, which has a `bare' nucleus Crenshaw1999, Vaughan2004 . The results obtained from a detailed study on this source Nandi2021 are used in the present work. We considered all X-ray observations of this source from XMM-Newton , Swift /XRT, and Suzaku . The powerlaw+powerlaw model fitted results are presented in Table 3 of Nandi2021 . We found the power-law index for primary continuum ( ^ PC ) ) varies from 1.6 to 2.1 and the variation of corresponding luminosity ( (L^ PC ) ) is from 43.8 to 45.4.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 120 as a bright, radio-quiet Seyfert 1 AGN with little intrinsic absorption and major X-ray-observatory coverage showing a steep soft spectrum.

quotes.quote: Ark 120 is a bright Sy 1 AGN at a redshift of z=0.0327 , and has a relatively constant 2-10 X-ray flux of F_ 2-10 2.50 10^ 11 Vaughan2004_2 . The source is radio-quiet, and due to a lack of observed evidence for intrinsic absorption, Ark 120 has been labeled a ``bare'' Sy 1 nucleus Ward1987,Vaughan2004_2 . Ark 120 has been observed by most of the major X-ray observatories. An EXOSAT observation showed the source to have a steep soft X-ray spectrum Turner1989 , as did a subsequent ROSAT observation Brandt1993 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 120 as a bare broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy whose Suzaku spectrum supports blurred disc reflection, with structured iron emission and a high-energy hump.

quotes.quote: As a further test to this scenario we present the analysis of a recent 100 ks long Suzaku observation of Arakelian 120, a prototypical `bare' Seyfert 1 galaxy in which no complex absorption system is expected to mimic a soft excess or mask the intrinsic properties of this key component. We show that a reflection model allowing for both warm/blurred and cold/distant reprocessing provides a self-consistent and convincing interpretation of the broadband X-ray emission of Ark 120, also characterized by a structured iron feature and a high-energy hump.

Arakelian 120 ( z=0.0327 ) is a rare case of a `bare' Seyfert galaxy, hence it represents the optimal candidate to test whether the soft excess is a signature of blurred reflection from the inner accretion disc.

As stated above, the slight spectral curvature observed at 6 keV in Ark 120 cannot be explained as the product of (multiple) covering effects, suggesting instead the presence of a broad skewed profile within the iron emission feature and indicating a strong gravity regime.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ark 120 as an additional reflection-model check, finding a hybrid-model preference and better residuals above 10 keV and near 6-7 keV.

quotes.quote: Note that, in order to corroborate that the choice of the subsample does not affect the result on the best models, we choose three other objects (in addition to the first five), namely Mrk 1040, Mrk 915, and Ark 120, to which we also fit the nine initial models. We find that 12 out of the 18 objects (Mrk 335, Fairall 9, Mrk 1040, NGC 1365, Ark 120, NGC 3227, NGC 3783, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, IRAS 13197-1627, NGC 5548, and NGC 7469) prefer the hybrid model (i.e. Residuals of hybrid model of NGC 4151 and Ark 120 shows a better fit above 10 keV and between 6-7 keV, compared to the neutral and ionized models. Analyzing Suzaku data of Ark 120, conclude that in order to obtain a self-consistent interpretation of the broad-band X-ray emission of Ark 120, a reflection model allowing for both warm/blurred and cold/distant reprocessing is necessary.

sources.summary: The paper describes Akn 120 as a bare-nucleus AGN with no reported absorption, strong soft excess emission, and detected Fe K plus Fe XXV/XXVI lines.

quotes.quote: The Fe \ K line is significantly detected in 4 sources: NGC 3783, Mrk 841, Mrk 509 and Akn 120. NGC 3783 also shows the Fe XXVI line, and Akn 120 shows both the Fe XXV and Fe XXVI lines.

Akn 120 This source is refer to as a ``bare-nucleus" AGN, and thus, there are no reports of this source being affected by absorption. The spectra is characterized by a strong soft excess emission, as studied by Po2018 and Ma2014 .

sources.summary: The paper models Ark 120 as a bare Seyfert galaxy, finding accretion-disk inclination and emissivity results consistent with previous blurred-reflector measurements.

quotes.quote: We methodically model the broad-band Suzaku spectra of a small sample of six 'bare' Seyfert galaxies: Ark 120, Fairall 9, MCG-02-14-009, Mrk 335, NGC 7469 and SWIFT J2127.4+5654.
In addition, the best-fit obtained with Model E for Ark 120 requires an inclination of i=48^ , whilst not unreasonable, differs from the inclinations measured using Models C D.
The results found here for Ark 120 suggest consistent measurements for the emissivity index with Brenneman Reynolds (2009).

sources.summary: The atlas gives Ark 120's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Ark 120 is a spiral galaxy at a redshift of z= 0.0327 ( D 149 Mpc) hosting a radio-quiet Sy 1 nucleus veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 that belongs to the nine-month BAT AGN sample. The NLR morphology appears halo-like mulchaey_emission-line_1996 . The first ground-based N- and Q band photometry of the nucleus were obtained with IRTF in 1983 and UKIRT in 1984 ward_continuum_1987 .

sources.summary: Ark 120 is the Seyfert 1 AGN case study for the paper's shock-Comptonization model, which is fitted to its XMM-Newton spectrum and successfully explains its observed soft X-ray excess.

quotes.quote: As a case study, we construct a three-parameter Comptonization model of inclination angle _ obs , disk photon temperature kT_ in and downstream electron energy kT_e to calculate the predicted spectra in comparison with a 60 ks XMM-Newton /EPIC-pn spectrum of a typical radio-quiet Seyfert 1 AGN, Ark 120.
As a case study to conclude the current work, we analyze a 60 ks XMM-Newton /EPIC-pn spectrum of Ark 120 observed in 2003 with the standard XSPEC 12.8.2 package Arnaud96 to perform the ^2 statistics.

As a simplistic three-parameter model (i.e. _ obs , kT_e and kT_ in ) besides its normalization, we have constructed a grid of synthetic spectral model for Comptonized component ( compsh ) and further demonstrated that the model can successfully explain the observed soft excess feature for a typical Seyfert 1 galaxy, Ark 120, as a case study.

sources.summary: The paper notes unfit prominent spiral arms and models the AGN host with a bulge and exponential disk, while literature B/T estimates remain uncertain.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1095 (Fig. 11.20) --- \ \ There are prominent spiral arms in the image which are not fit. The best fit finds that the AGN host can be modeled with a bulge ( m_ bul 16.1 mag) component and an exponential disk without spiral arms ( B/T 0.25 ). While previous studies claim that the host certainly contains a disk, the B/T ratio appears to be somewhat uncertain and ranges from 0.35 (Bennert et al. 2010) to 0.49 (Bentz et al. 2009).

sources.summary: The review presents Ark 120 as a relatively bare local Seyfert with weak line-of-sight absorption, reprocessing gas outside the view, and an extended X-ray corona.

quotes.quote: In this case the AGN will appear ``bare'', with a minimum amount of X-ray and UV absorption (with column densities N_H 10^ 20-21 cm ^ 2 ), as observed in local type Seyferts such as Ark 120 2004MNRAS.351..193V or Ark 564 2015A&A...577A...8G ; or in general, as observed in luminous, unabsorbed, type 1 QSOs. However, the inner environment of such AGN will not be completely bare, as plenty of gas is still present outside the l.o.s., as demonstrated by the reprocessing features detected in long X-ray observations of Ark 120 2016ApJ...828...98R , or from the strongly blueshifted high-ionization broad emission lines observed in the spectra of luminous type 1 QSOs, indicating reprocessing in large columns of outflowing gas 2011AJ....141..167R .

In comparison, deep and broadband X-ray spectroscopic observations of the local Seyfert Ark 120 suggest an extended corona 10-40 R_g , 2014MNRAS.439.3016M , and a decrease of the corona size with increasing flux of the continuum source 2019A&A...623A..11P .

sources.summary: The paper models Ark 120 as a Seyfert galaxy with a central supermassive black hole and finds the Kerr solution is not recovered at 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: We apply our model to fit Suzaku data of four Seyfert galaxies (Ton S180, Ark 120, 1H0419--577, and Swift J0501.9--3239). These galaxies host at the center supermassive black holes that were previously interpreted as near-extremal Kerr black holes. For Ton S180 and 1H0419--577, our measurements are still consistent with the Kerr hypothesis. For Ark 120 and Swift J0501.9--3239, the Kerr solution is not recovered at 3- .

sources.summary: The paper reports a very high cutoff lower limit for Ark 120, compares several broadly consistent literature constraints, and identifies one statistically inconsistent Akylas result.

The 10--78 keV signal-to-noise ratios are calculated using FPMA spectra. The blackhole masses are from Koss_2017 , unless marked with a number referring to the following literature.(1) Malizia_2008 ; (2) 3C109_mass ; (3) PicA_4579_mass ; (4) CenA_mass . Lbol _ 14-195 keV is the bolometric luminosity estimated by the BAT 14--195 keV flux Koss_2017 and used for _ edd calculation. L _ 0.1-200 keV is the unabsorbed 0.1--200 keV luminosity, extrapolated using the best-fit results of pexrav to NuSTAR spectra and adopting the redshifts from the 105-month BAT catalogue and H_ 0 = 70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 . The compactness parameter l is derived from L _ 0.1-200 keV . Spectral fitting is carried out within the 3--78 keV band using XSPEC Arnaud_1996 . ^2 statistics is adopted and all the errors together with the upper/lower limits in this paper correspond to 90 confidence level with ^2 = 2.71 , unless otherwise stated. The relative element abundance is set to the default in XSPEC, given by . For each observation, the spectra of FPMA and FPMB are jointly fitted with a cross-normalization Madsen_2015 . In this paper we intend to perform uniform measurements of the / T_ e for the radio-quiet and radio-loud samples and bring them into comparison. In order to guarantee such comparison is model-independent, various models are employed, including pexrav , relxill , and relxillcp . pexrav Magdziarz_1995 is the model we used to fit the radio-loud sample in Kang_2020 , which fits the spectra with an exponentially cutoff power law plus a neutral reflection component, and is the most widely used model in measurement e.g., Molina_2019, Rani_2019, Panagiotou_2020, Balokovi_2020, Kang_2021 . For simplicity, the solar element abundance for the reflector and an inclination of cos i = 0.45 are adopted, which are the default values of the model. We allow the photon index , and the reflection scaling factor R free to vary. relxill Garc_2014 also models the underlying continuum with a cutoff powerlaw, but convolves the reflection component with disc relativistic broadening effect. However, some parameters are hard to constrain even with these high-quality spectra and hence have to be frozen. The inner and outer radius of the accretion disk, Rin and Rout , are fixed at 1 ISCO and 400 gravitational radii respectively as the default of the model. Besides we fix the blackhole spin a = 0.998 The fitting results however are insensitive to this choice. and the inclination angle i = 30^ . The accretion disk is presumed to be neutral and have the solar iron abundance, with the corresponding parameter logxi and Afe fixed at 0 and 1, respectively. We assume a disk with constant emissivity, setting the emissivity parameter Index2 tied with Index1 . The free parameters include Index1 , , E_ cut and the reflection fraction (with different definition from the R in pexrav ). A Comptonization model, relxillcp , is also adopted to directly measure the coronal temperature T_ e . relxillcp is a Comptonization version of relxill , replacing the cutoff power law with a nthcomp continuum. Other parameters are set in the same way as relxill . Meanwhile, a common component zphabs is added to all three models to represent the intrinsic photoelectric absorption, with the Galactic absorption ignored due to its inappreciable influence on spectra. As for the Fe K lines, in relxill and relxillcp the continuum reflection component and the Fe K line are jointly fitted, while a zgauss is added to pexrav to describe the Fe K line. Since a relativistically broadened Fe K line can not be well constrained in the majority of observations, we deal with the Gaussian component as follows. In the first place we fix the line at 6.4 keV in the rest frame and the line width at 19 eV the mean Fe K line width in AGNs measured with Chandra HETG, Shu_2010 to model a neutral narrow Fe K line. Then we allow the line width free to vary. If a variable line width prominently improves the fitting ( ^2 > 5 ), the corresponding fitting results are adopted. We summarize below the three models adopted in the XSPEC term and the corresponding free parameters. zphabs*(pexrav + zgauss) Free parameters include absorption column density n_ H , photon index , high energy cutoff and the strength of the reflection component R. zphabs*relxill n_ H , , E_ cut , emissivity parameter Index1 and the reflection fraction. zphabs*relxillcp Same as relxill , except that is replaced with T_ e . The best-fitting results of the key parameters are shown in Tab. tab:fitting_result . In a few sources the spectral fitting yields very high lower limits of E_ cut , up to 2360 keV (see 4 for further discussion on reliability of such high lower limits of E_ cut ).
